POSTS AND TELECOMMUNICATIONS TRANSFORMATION STRATEGY

2025–2030










Special Assistant

to the Chief Adviser

Ministry of Posts, Telecommunications and Information Technology




It is with a profound sense of responsibility and a clear vision for the future that I present the Posts and Telecommunications Transformation Strategy (2025–2030).

My tenure as the Special Assistant to the Chief Adviser was defined by a singular mission: to dismantle a legacy of regulatory capture and administrative opacity that had long stifled innovation and prioritized political patronage over public service. This was no easy task and I am greatly thankful to my technical and support team for their hard work as they went above and beyond with minimal support. We prioritized digital rights, institutional autonomy, and technical sovereignty. We inherited an ecosystem characterized by a fragmented licensing regime with nearly 3,500 entities and vast arrays of idle assets. This strategy represents our collective response to those institutional failures, providing a roadmap to transition Bangladesh into a unified, resilient, and rights-based digital powerhouse.

The philosophy underpinning this strategy is anchored in the absolute protection of the citizen. We have enacted a foundational legal trinity—the Cyber Safety, Personal Data Protection, and National Data Governance Ordinances of 2025, which institutionalizes data governance as a matter of national law for the first time in our history as well as introducing long overdue protection mechanisms for personal data. Most significantly, through the Bangladesh Telecommunication (Amendment) Ordinance 2025, we have provided a statutory shield for our democracy by permanently prohibiting internet shutdowns. We have transitioned connectivity from a discretionary privilege into a protected public necessity. Furthermore, we have ensured that state infrastructure can never again be used for political coercion by introducing clear check and balance frameworks in telecommunication surveillance, to protect citizen’s privacy from illegal and unwarranted surveillance.

We believe that institutional rebirth is the prerequisite for operational excellence. We have initiated a strategic turnaround for our state-owned enterprises, shifting them from fiscal liabilities to competitive infrastructure anchors. We restored the core licensing authority of the regulator, ensuring that spectrum management and licensing are driven by technical rigor rather than political directives. We have pivoted Teletalk toward a lean, rental-based network model and repealed the 127-year-old Post Office Act to redefine the national post as a modern logistics and financial engine. By rationalizing satellite pricing, we have finally made high-tech connectivity an affordable reality for maritime security and disaster response.

Our vision for technical sovereignty is embodied in the National Responsible Digital Architecture and Exchange hub. We have moved decisively to end the era of vendor lock-in by establishing the nation’s first National Source Code Repository, ensuring that the state owns and controls the code essential for its services. By optimizing our National Fiber Bank and fiberizing our mobile towers, we are building the digital rails required for a truly inclusive economy. This strategy is ultimately about trust. By mandating public hearings every four months and maintaining a transparent Action Tracker, we have made the regulator directly accountable to the people it serves.

To our successor and the dedicated public servants of the division, we leave behind a framework designed to outlast political cycles. The responsibility now lies in the discipline of implementation. We must remain vigilant against the return of entrenched interests and stay focused on the master targets for 2030: zero shutdowns, universal broadband access, and a digitally sovereign Bangladesh. The journey from a captured past to a sovereign digital future has begun. Let us ensure that no citizen is left behind.



Acknowledgement


We extend our deepest gratitude to Faiz Ahmed Taiyeb, Special Assistant to the Chief Advisor, for his visionary leadership and strategic guidance, which were instrumental in shaping the direction of this roadmap.

We also convey our sincere thanks to Abdun Naser Khan, Secretary of the Posts and Telecommunications Division (PTD), for his unwavering support in facilitating this initiative. We are equally grateful to the Post and Telecommunications Whitepaper Taskforce, convened by Dr. Md. Kamrul Hasan, for their outstanding work on the PTD Whitepaper, which served as one of the principal source documents informing this strategy document.

A special note of appreciation goes to Md. Azaher Uddin Anik and Arefin Mizan for their meticulous research, writing, and editorial contributions that brought this comprehensive strategy to life. We also acknowledge the significant efforts of ASM Ashraf, Asif Shahriyar Sushmit, and Zamilur Reza for their dedicated documentary assistance and coordination throughout the development process.

We are particularly grateful to the core institutions of the Posts and Telecommunications Division - Bangladesh Post Office, Bangladesh Telecommunication Regulatory Commission (BTRC), Bangladesh Telecommunications Company Limited (BTCL), Bangladesh Submarine Cable Company Limited, Teletalk Bangladesh Ltd, Telephone Shilpa Sangstha (TSS), Bangladesh Cable Shilpa Limited, Department of Telecommunications, Mailing operator and courier services licensing authority (MOCSLA) and the Bangladesh Satellite Company Limited; for providing the valuable data, insights, and institutional support necessary to formulate this strategy.

Finally, we thank the subject matter experts, stakeholders, and team members who shared their time, knowledge, and ideas that informed this compendium.

Disclaimer

This document, the Posts and Telecommunications Transformation Strategy (2025-2030), is published by the Posts and Telecommunications division (PTD), Ministry of Posts, Telecommunications and Information Technology, Government of the People's Republic of Bangladesh.

The information, data, and projections contained herein are based on currently available statistics, research, and strategic assessments as of the date of publication. While every effort has been made to ensure the accuracy and reliability of the content, the PTD makes no representations or warranties of any kind, express or implied, regarding the completeness, accuracy, or suitability of the information for any particular purpose.

The strategies, timelines, and key performance indicators (KPIs) outlined in this document are subject to periodic review and adjustment based on evolving technological landscapes, economic conditions, and government priorities. This document serves as a strategic roadmap and does not constitute a legally binding contract or statutory obligation unless otherwise enacted through specific legislative instruments or official gazette notifications.

The PTD assumes no liability for any errors, omissions, or discrepancies in the data provided, nor for any actions taken in reliance thereon. References to specific private sector entities, technologies, or platforms are for illustrative purposes only and do not constitute an endorsement.

It is hereby declared that no public funds were utilized in the drafting, design, or production of this document. Due to time constraints and the urgent need for strategic direction, Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools were extensively utilized to assist in the generation of graphics and textual content. However, all AI-generated material has undergone rigorous human review and extensive error-checking to ensure accuracy, contextual relevance, and alignment with national interests.

Executive Summary

This document presents the Digital Transformation Strategy for the Posts and Telecommunications Division (PTD), the central government agency responsible for oversight, policy direction, and governance of Bangladesh's telecommunications infrastructure, postal service network, and associated state-owned enterprises. The strategy is the product of a comprehensive sectoral review conducted between June and December 2025, drawing on institutional visits, Task Force findings, project documentation, regulatory analysis, international benchmarking, media analysis, and over 650 citizen submissions.

The strategy spans ten entities under PTD's purview: the Bangladesh Telecommunication Regulatory Commission (BTRC), Bangladesh Telecommunications Company Limited (BTCL), Teletalk Bangladesh Limited, Bangladesh Submarine Cable Company Limited (BSCPLC), Bangladesh Satellite Company Limited (BSCL), Bangladesh Cable Shilpa Limited (BCSL), Telephone Shilpa Sangstha Limited (TSS), the Department of Telecommunications (DoT), the Mailing Operator and Courier Services Licensing Authority (MOCSLA), and the Bangladesh Post Office. Together, these entities manage infrastructure assets exceeding BDT 579 billion, employ over 55,000 personnel, regulate a telecommunications market serving 190 million subscribers, and operate a postal network of 9,800 offices reaching every corner of the country.

The diagnosis reveals a sector of immense strategic importance that has been systematically undermined by governance failures, regulatory capture, institutional fragmentation, and fiscal indiscipline accumulated over the period 2009 to 2024. The strategy proposes a coherent reform architecture organized across nine thematic chapters, each addressing a distinct dimension of the transformation agenda. This executive summary presents the core findings and recommendations of each chapter.

Strategic Vision and Context (Chapter 1)

The strategy is anchored in a vision of the Posts and Telecommunications Division as the institutional steward of Bangladesh's digital connectivity ecosystem, delivering affordable, reliable, secure, and inclusive telecommunications and postal services that underpin national economic competitiveness, citizen welfare, and sovereign digital capacity. The mission translates this vision into operational terms: to reform, modernize, and govern PTD entities through evidence-based policy, institutional professionalism, regulatory independence, and measurable performance accountability.

Nine guiding principles structure the entire strategy. Sovereignty requires that critical digital infrastructure, spectrum assets, and data governance remain under national control. Inclusion demands that connectivity and digital services reach every citizen regardless of geography, income, or ability. Transparency mandates that regulatory decisions, licensing processes, financial management, and performance outcomes operate under public scrutiny. Sustainability insists that infrastructure investment and institutional design outlast political cycles and electoral transitions. Accountability ensures that every reform commitment is tied to measurable indicators, responsible entities, and time-bound milestones.

The strategic context reflects Bangladesh's position as a lower-middle-income country with 190 million mobile subscribers, broadband penetration reaching approximately 56 percent, an internet economy growing at over 20 percent annually, and a youthful population with median age below 28 years. However, this potential is constrained by a telecommunications sector where regulatory independence has been eroded, spectrum pricing is designed for revenue extraction rather than deployment efficiency, over twenty-three fragmented licence categories create intermediary rent extraction, and the Social Obligation Fund intended for digital inclusion has been subject to discretionary management. The strategy documents these failures in detail and proposes the most comprehensive restructuring of the sector since the corporatization of the Bangladesh Telegraph and Telephone Board.

Legal Landscape (Chapter 2)

The legal architecture underpinning the strategy rests on two landmark instruments enacted in 2025, supported by the broader national legal triad governing the digital economy.

The Bangladesh Telecommunication (Amendment) Ordinance 2025, approved in December 2025, represents the single most consequential legislative intervention in the sector in over a decade. Its provisions permanently prohibit government-directed internet shutdowns, a reform with no precedent in South Asia. The Ordinance restores BTRC's operational autonomy by separating the regulatory function from direct ministerial control, establishes a Parliamentary Accountability Committee to provide democratic oversight without executive interference, extends BTRC's regulatory scope to cover digital platforms and over-the-top services, and introduces a mandatory 72-hour data breach notification regime aligned with international standards such as the EU's General Data Protection Regulation.

The Telecommunications Network and Licensing Policy (TNL Policy) 2025, gazetted in September 2025, replaces the fragmented legacy licensing regime with four technology-neutral licence categories: Access Network Service Provider (ANSP), National Infrastructure and Connectivity Service Provider (NICSP), International Connectivity Service Provider (ICSP), and Non-Telecommunication Network Service Provider (NTNSP). This consolidation eliminates the intermediary layers, specifically the IGW Operators Forum, International Internet Gateway, and International Terrestrial Cable operators, that have extracted rents by standing between international bandwidth supply and domestic demand. The TNL Policy introduces performance-linked licence conditions including 50 percent tower fiberization within 18 months, coverage obligations for underserved areas, and a Significant Market Power framework for proportionate regulation.

The strategy also addresses the Postal Services Ordinance, which requires modernization to reflect Bangladesh Post's expanding role in logistics, financial services, and digital access. The chapter further examines the alignment of sectoral legislation with the Cyber Safety Ordinance (CSO), Personal Data Protection Ordinance (PDPO), and National Data Governance Ordinance (NDGO), which collectively form the legal triad governing data sovereignty, citizen privacy, and secure digital governance. The MOCSLA recruitment rules, pending since the authority's establishment in 2014, are identified as a critical gap requiring immediate resolution.

Telecom Infrastructure and Regulatory Capture (Chapter 3)

This chapter provides the most detailed publicly available assessment of Bangladesh's telecommunications infrastructure, market structure, spectrum governance, international connectivity, consumer protection regime, Social Obligation Fund management, and state-owned enterprise operations. The analysis exposes structural distortions that have constrained sectoral performance.

Regulatory Architecture: BTRC's operational autonomy was systematically dismantled following the 2010 amendments to the Bangladesh Telecommunication Act, which transferred effective decision-making authority to the executive branch. Commissioners were appointed through political patronage rather than merit-based selection, enforcement capacity was hollowed out, and the Research Division was never staffed. The result was a regulator captured by the interests it was supposed to oversee. The strategy proposes a Government Order formally codifying the separation of DoT's policy advisory role from BTRC's licensing and enforcement functions, reconstitution of the BTRC Research Division with independent analytical capacity, and the phased restoration of regulatory credibility through transparent decision-making and published reasoning.

Market Structure: The proliferation of over twenty-three licence categories created a layered market structure where intermediary operators extracted margins without adding proportionate value. The International Gateway (IGW) operators, International Internet Gateway (IIG) operators, and International Terrestrial Cable (ITC) operators collectively formed a toll-collection infrastructure between BSCPLC's international bandwidth and domestic end-users. Vertically integrated operators exploited this fragmentation through transfer pricing, with documented instances of bandwidth sold to affiliated entities at BDT 8 per Mbps when market rates were BDT 120 per Mbps. The TNL Policy's consolidation into four categories directly addresses this distortion.

Spectrum Governance: Spectrum pricing has prioritized revenue maximization over deployment efficiency. Auction designs have favoured incumbent operators, performance-linked conditions have been absent or unenforced, and secondary trading frameworks remain undeveloped. The strategy proposes a comprehensive Five-Year National Spectrum Plan addressing 5G mid-band allocation (3.3 to 3.8 GHz), performance-linked pricing, technology-neutral assignments, and enterprise licensing provisions.

International Connectivity: BSCPLC operates three submarine cable systems: SEA-ME-WE 4 (operational since 2005, end of life 2030), SEA-ME-WE 5 (operational since 2016, end of life 2037), and SEA-ME-WE 6 (expected operational Q1 2026, adding 13,200 Gbps capacity, end of life 2045). Bandwidth has been historically overpriced at the wholesale level, constraining internet affordability. The private consortium BPCS (SIGMAR) cable provides limited competitive pressure. Starlink's entry in May 2025, with 880 Gbps gateway capacity and 1,800 active terminals, introduces the first meaningful alternative to terrestrial and submarine connectivity.

Social Obligation Fund: The SOF, intended as the primary instrument for universal digital inclusion, has been managed through discretionary allocation rather than systematic results-based financing. The strategy proposes restructuring the SOF to a results-based model that disburses funding only against verified delivery of measurable outcomes, confirmed by independent audits.

State-Owned Enterprises: BTCL, with capital assets exceeding BDT 579 billion and over 12,600 employees, operates legacy fixed-line infrastructure that generates declining revenue against rising operational costs. Teletalk holds approximately 6 percent mobile market share and reported a net loss of BDT 1.5 billion in FY2022-23. BSCPLC is one of the few profitable SOEs but its pricing policies have constrained downstream affordability. BSCL manages Bangabandhu-1 satellite with growing but still limited commercial revenue. BCSL manufactures copper and fibre optic cables under procurement frameworks that have not kept pace with market demand. TSS is repositioning as a domestic digital device manufacturer through strategic partnerships.

Sectoral Impacts Beyond Posts and Telecommunications (Chapter 4)

The strategy recognizes that the posts and telecommunications sector's significance extends far beyond its institutional boundaries. Telecommunications infrastructure and postal networks serve as enabling platforms for citizen-centric service delivery, e-commerce logistics, financial inclusion, disaster resilience, agricultural market connectivity, health and education access, and democratic processes.

Citizen-Centric Service Design: The strategy adopts a three-tier service design framework aligned with the National E-Service Guideline: digital-first (fully online services for digitally literate populations), assisted access (Nagorik Sheba centres with trained intermediaries for populations with limited digital literacy), and in-person services (retained for transactions requiring physical verification). Bangladesh Post's 9,800 offices represent the country's most extensive physical service network and are being integrated with the Nagorik Sheba programme to create community-based digital service hubs offering NID, passport, tax filing, SIM registration, and connectivity services.

E-Commerce and Logistics: The strategy positions Bangladesh Post as the national last-mile logistics backbone for rural commerce. A reverse logistics middleware system enables two-way urban-rural e-commerce parcel delivery. API integration with the Domestic Mail Monitoring System supports real-time tracking. Cold chain infrastructure (14 chiller chambers, 84 deep freezers) enables agricultural and perishable goods logistics. The 2025 pilot that transported 65 tons of mangoes directly to consumers demonstrates the operational potential.

Financial Inclusion: Bangladesh Post's postal banking infrastructure, combined with the Nagad mobile financial services platform, positions the postal network as a vehicle for reaching financially excluded populations, particularly in rural areas. The strategy envisions expanded postal financial products including savings, micro-insurance, and remittance services by 2030.

Democratic Infrastructure: The postal ballot modernization programme, developed in coordination with the Election Commission, establishes a Standard Operating Procedure for ballot handling with digital tracking integration, ensuring chain-of-custody integrity for absentee and overseas voting. This positions Bangladesh Post as a contributor to democratic infrastructure.

Cross-Sectoral Coordination: The strategy establishes a coordination framework requiring PTD entities to engage systematically with the health, education, agriculture, social protection, disaster management, and justice sectors, ensuring that connectivity investments translate into measurable development outcomes rather than isolated infrastructure outputs.

Strategic Policies for Sovereignty and Innovation (Chapter 5)

This chapter translates the strategy's sovereignty principle into concrete policy frameworks governing data, technology, manufacturing, and environmental sustainability within the posts and telecommunications sector.

Digital Sovereignty: The strategy mandates vendor neutrality in all telecom infrastructure procurement, prohibiting single-vendor dependency in core network elements. The National Source Code Policy requires government ownership of all publicly funded software with mandatory escrow and audit provisions. The National Cloud Policy establishes a four-tier data classification framework (D0 Open through D4 Critical) with sovereign-by-design architecture and Zero Trust implementation for all government cloud deployments. Critical data must reside in nationally controlled data centres, with 100 percent sovereign cloud hosting targeted by 2030.

Cybersecurity and Critical Information Infrastructure: The strategy designates telecommunications networks, submarine cable landing stations, satellite ground stations, and data centres as Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) subject to enhanced security obligations. All major operators must achieve ISO 27001 certification by 2028, with the 72-hour mandatory breach reporting regime fully operational. A sectoral CERT establishment and biannual cybersecurity drills provide layered defence against evolving threats.

Local Manufacturing and Import Substitution: The strategy positions BCSL and TSS as anchors of a domestic digital manufacturing ecosystem. BCSL's optical fibre production capacity is being expanded to meet demand generated by the tower fiberization mandate. TSS has signed strategic partnership agreements with technology firms for CCTV equipment manufacturing, lithium-ion battery repair, 4G/5G dual-band WiFi router assembly, and computer accessories production. These initiatives contribute to import substitution, local employment, and technology transfer.

Environmental Sustainability: Green ICT practices are integrated across the strategy. The BTCL Hathazari Green Data Centre, developed as a PPP with ADB consultation, will pioneer renewable energy sourcing and modern containment cooling. Renewable energy adoption at tower sites is incentivized through spectrum fee rebates. A producer responsibility framework for electronic waste establishes recycling requirements and annual reporting obligations.

Emerging Technologies: The strategy establishes governance frameworks for 5G, IoT, AI, blockchain, and satellite technologies. A risk-based AI governance model requires algorithmic impact assessments for public-sector deployments. The National Blockchain Policy focuses on sovereign, permissioned networks for land records, identity validation, and supply chain integrity. The NGSO satellite framework, anchored by the Starlink operational precedent, enables multiple satellite operators with clear revenue sharing and local gateway requirements.

Institutional Reform (Chapter 6)

The institutional reform chapter addresses the human and organizational dimensions that legislative change alone cannot resolve. The analysis is grounded in documented patterns of governance failure across PTD entities, including politicized leadership appointments, irregular recruitment, unauthorized deputation extensions, absence of performance management systems, and board governance compromised by conflicts of interest.

The reform agenda operates across five interconnected tracks:

Human Resource Integrity: A unified digital Human Resource Information System (HRIS) will be deployed across all PTD entities, integrating personnel records, payroll, promotions, deputation tracking, and performance management into a single auditable platform. Merit-based recruitment and promotion frameworks, standardized across entities, will replace the discretionary practices that enabled irregular appointments.

Board Governance: Boards of Directors across all SOEs will be reconstituted to include a majority of independent members with sectoral expertise. The PTD Secretary will be separated from the Board Chair position to prevent conflicts of interest between policy oversight and operational control. Independent mixed selection boards, including external experts and CAG observers, will conduct leadership appointments.

Accountability and Oversight: Comprehensive HR compliance audits by the Comptroller and Auditor General will be integrated into the regular audit cycle, with findings reported to Parliament. Secure whistleblower channels will guarantee anonymity and protection against retaliation. Fast-tracked disciplinary mechanisms will prevent accused officers from evading proceedings through retirement or transfer.

Structural Reform: A dedicated Telecommunication Cadre will be rebuilt under the Department of Telecommunications through competitive civil service recruitment, restoring technical capacity and institutional continuity lost since the corporatization of BTTB. Strict limits on deputation and ad-hoc extensions will rebuild professionalism across entities.

Entity-Specific Reform Programmes: Each of the ten PTD entities has a documented reform programme with specific done and pending actions. BTCL is pursuing fibre mapping digitization, MVNO technical integration with Teletalk, IPTV launch, ERP deployment, Green Data Centre development, and co-location service modernization. Teletalk has expanded 4G coverage to 85 percent of sites and signed tower sharing agreements for 2,300 additional sites. BSCPLC is commissioning SMW-6. Bangladesh Post has installed GPS trackers on 85 mail vans, established e-commerce API integration, initiated cold chain infrastructure, and is developing the National Address Management System. TSS has signed MoUs for CCTV manufacturing and battery repair. BSCL is expanding satellite services across South Asia. BCSL is modernizing optical fibre production lines. BTRC is preparing ERP specifications and the National Equipment Identity Register relaunch. MOCSLA is finalizing recruitment regulations.

Project Reform (Chapter 7)

The strategy includes the most comprehensive public assessment of PTD's development project portfolio, covering 42 projects with a combined investment exceeding BDT 20,000 crore. The Task Force review identified systemic governance failures including weak feasibility analysis, procurement irregularities, scope deviations from approved Development Project Proformas, implementation delays, dependency failures between projects, and falsified progress reporting.

The strategy documents specific interventions:

Cost Re-baselining: Inflated procurement costs for IT equipment have been slashed against international benchmarks, with a new value-for-money standard enforced for all future tenders. The cost per connection has been re-evaluated, eliminating the artificially inflated per-unit costs that characterized earlier procurement cycles.

Component Cancellation: Non-viable components have been identified and cancelled, saving hundreds of crores in public funds. The CRVS Software component (BDT 502.09 crore) and the Digital Village initiative (BDT 251.16 crore) were scrapped due to the failure of the Chinese G2G loan agreement and lack of feasibility. Funds have been reallocated or returned to the exchequer.

Third-Party Verification: A mandatory third-party verification mechanism has been established, with independent technical auditors physically inspecting random sites to validate contractor-submitted data before any bill is processed. The definition of connectivity has been shifted from physical installation to verified active usage, ensuring payments are released only for functional connections.

New Generation Projects: The strategy establishes a new generation of development projects designed with the governance safeguards absent from their predecessors. These include the Nagorik Sheba expansion, BTCL Green Data Centre, fourth submarine cable feasibility, Teletalk network modernization, Bangladesh Post digital logistics platform, and TSS manufacturing scale-up. Each project is subject to mandatory feasibility studies, third-party evaluations, centralized procurement databases, and ERP-driven monitoring.

Social Obligation Fund Reform: The SOF governance framework has been restructured from an input-based allocation model to a results-based financing system. Disbursements are linked to independently verified outcomes including broadband speeds, latency, active user adoption, and connectivity quality, rather than to input expenditure certificates.

Strategic and Governance Reforms (Chapter 8)

This chapter consolidates the entity-level and project-level reforms into eleven sector-wide governance frameworks that collectively define the operating environment for the posts and telecommunications sector through 2030.

Regulatory Architecture Reform: Formal separation of DoT's policy advisory function from BTRC's licensing and enforcement mandate, with published Government Orders, joint steering committees, and transparent decision-making protocols.

Market Design Modernization: Implementation of the TNL Policy's four-category licence structure, managed migration from legacy licences, the Significant Market Power framework, and open-access obligations to prevent anti-competitive concentration.

Spectrum Governance: The Five-Year National Spectrum Plan addressing 5G allocation, secondary trading, performance-linked pricing, and enterprise licensing, with public consultation and published reasoning for all spectrum assignments.

Digital Regulatory Systems: The BTRC ERP platform integrating licensing, revenue management, spectrum allocation, QoS monitoring, and enforcement. The National One-Stop Digital Permit Portal with 15-day and 30-day statutory SLA timelines for tower construction, fibre laying, and equipment import approvals.

Consumer Protection: District-level QoS performance scorecards published monthly, a unified digital complaint management system across web, mobile, and USSD channels, and modernized customer codes requiring plain-language tariff communication and explicit consent for value-added services.

Data Governance: ISO 27001/27701 adoption mandates for all operators, the 72-hour breach notification regime, sectoral CERT establishment, and Critical Information Infrastructure designation for core telecommunications assets.

Digital Inclusion: Results-based SOF financing, data and device affordability vouchers, zero-interest handset programmes for low-income populations, digital navigator helpdesks for rural and marginalized users, and measurable targets for closing the urban-rural connectivity divide.

Investment Climate: Clear, time-bound licensing processes, published fee schedules, independent dispute resolution mechanisms, intellectual property protections, and streamlined foreign investment approval pathways designed to restore investor confidence.

Emerging Technology Governance: 5G readiness including urban pilot deployment by 2028 and metropolitan commercial services by 2029, IoT interoperability standards, AI governance frameworks, satellite service licensing, and regulatory sandbox provisions.

Sustainability: The Green Data Centre at Hathazari, renewable energy incentives for tower sites, e-waste producer responsibility frameworks, and carbon footprint reporting requirements.

Evidence-Based Policymaking: The BTRC Research Division producing quarterly market analyses and regulatory impact evaluations, the Centre of Excellence for Digital Public Infrastructure conducting independent project evaluations, and the Reform Programme Management Office operating quarterly accountability dashboards.

Implementation Roadmap (Chapter 9)

The strategy is organized into a four-phase implementation roadmap with conditional sequencing, where each phase establishes the prerequisites for the next.

Phase

Period

Key Deliverables

Phase 1

2025 to 2026

Legal and institutional foundations: BTRC Autonomy Government Order gazetted; TNL Policy migration framework published; Postal Services Ordinance consultation draft circulated; Five-Year Spectrum Plan published; all SOE boards reconstituted; Digital HRIS deployed; BTRC ERP specified and procurement initiated; SMW-6 commissioned (13,200 Gbps); Teletalk tower sharing agreement (2,300 sites) operationalized; GPS fleet tracking integrated for Bangladesh Post; 500 post offices converted to Nagorik Sheba centres; TSS manufacturing pilots initiated; Reform PMO dashboards operational; CAG HR compliance audit completed.

Phase 2

2027 to 2028

Market restructuring and infrastructure scaling: All operators migrated to TNL Policy licence categories; MVNO commercially launched (BTCL-Teletalk); BTCL IPTV launched; SMP framework determinations published; Spectrum secondary trading operational; 80 percent tower fiberization achieved; Hathazari Green Data Centre PPP structured and construction commenced; BTRC ERP fully deployed; One-Stop Permit Portal operational; QoS Public Dashboard live; ISO 27001 achieved by major operators; National Address Management System implemented; Nagorik Sheba expanded to 3,000 centres; 5G urban pilot launched; NEIR fully integrated with customs databases.

Phase 3

2029 to 2030

Smart governance and maturity: 5G commercial deployment in metropolitan areas and all district headquarters; Research Division producing quarterly publications; Centre of Excellence conducting annual independent evaluations; Parliamentary Accountability Committee conducting regular oversight; Nagorik Sheba network reaches 8,000 centres; Bangladesh Post digital logistics platform fully operational; Postal financial services expanded; Hathazari Green Data Centre Tier III certified; ISO 27001 achieved by 100 percent of operators; Bangabandhu-2 procurement advanced.

Phase 4

2030 to 2035

Long-term vision: Bangabandhu-2 satellite commissioned; Fourth submarine cable operational (diversifying beyond SEA-ME-WE corridor); 6G research and spectrum preparedness; 95 percent household broadband availability; Comprehensive data sovereignty capabilities established; Bangladesh positioned as a regional digital connectivity hub with submarine cable landing, satellite gateway, and data centre hosting services.

The roadmap includes a consolidated risk management framework addressing political discontinuity, institutional resistance, fiscal constraints, technology evolution, market concentration, and cybersecurity threats. Each risk category is matched with specific mitigation instruments embedded in the strategy's legal, institutional, and financial architecture.

Strategic Imperative

Bangladesh's posts and telecommunications sector stands at an inflection point. The legislative, policy, and institutional reforms initiated in 2025 represent the most comprehensive restructuring of the sector in a generation. The Amended Ordinance's prohibition of internet shutdowns, with no precedent in South Asia, signals a fundamental commitment to digital rights and economic stability. The TNL Policy's elimination of intermediary licence layers addresses a structural distortion that has inflated connectivity costs for over a decade. The Starlink licensing demonstrated that new market entrants can be accommodated within weeks when regulatory processes are functional. The tower sharing agreements show that infrastructure expansion can be financed through commercial partnerships rather than public expenditure alone. The postal reforms demonstrate that legacy institutions can be repositioned for digital-age relevance.

These reforms have begun. The question is no longer whether transformation is possible but whether the institutions, legal frameworks, and accountability mechanisms established through this strategy can sustain reform momentum across electoral cycles, leadership transitions, fiscal pressures, and the resistance of entrenched interests. The strategy's architecture is deliberately designed to address this challenge. Legal commitments are gazetted in ordinances and government orders rather than administrative circulars. Institutional reforms are embedded in board reconstitution and digital systems rather than dependent on individual leadership. Financial discipline is enforced through third-party verification and results-based financing rather than self-reported compliance. Performance accountability is published through dashboards and parliamentary oversight rather than internal reporting.

The ultimate measure of this strategy will not be the comprehensiveness of its analysis or the ambition of its targets. It will be whether, by 2030, every citizen in Bangladesh has access to affordable and reliable connectivity, whether the telecommunications market operates under transparent and predictable governance, whether the postal network has been transformed from a letter delivery service into a digital inclusion platform, whether public investment in the sector generates verified outcomes rather than procurement expenditure, and whether Bangladesh is recognized as a credible participant in regional and global digital governance. This strategy provides the analytical foundation, policy architecture, and implementation roadmap to achieve these outcomes. The responsibility for execution belongs to the institutions and public servants entrusted with the national interest.




1. Strategic Vision and Context

1.1 Background

Bangladesh’s posts and telecommunications sector has, for over a decade, operated under conditions of structural fragmentation, regulatory capture, and institutional under-performance. The White Paper on Bangladesh’s Telecom Crisis established, through extensive institutional audit, field visits, public hearings, and document analysis across ten entities, that the sector’s difficulties are not primarily technological but governance-driven. Of the 42 projects executed under PTD totalling BDT 20,009.82 Crore, 18 high-value initiatives (BDT 16,744.03 Crore) were prioritised for scrutiny due to size or flagged irregularities, revealing widespread deficiencies in strategic planning, feasibility assessments, and sustainability metrics. None of the major entities—BTCL, Teletalk, BTRC, Postal Directorate, DoT, BSCPLC, TSS, or BCSL—maintained structured master plans.

This planning vacuum produced a cascade of consequences: misaligned infrastructure investments, underutilised assets, inflated expenditures, poor service quality, and limited digital access—disproportionately affecting students, small businesses, and marginalised communities. The International Long Distance Telecommunication Service (ILDTS) Policy of 2010, which governed the sector for fifteen years, created a layered licensing architecture of International Gateways (IGWs), International Internet Gateways (IIGs), Interconnection Exchanges (ICXs), and National Internet Exchanges (NIXs) that introduced unnecessary intermediary costs. As the Special Assistant to the Chief Adviser for Telecom and ICT characterised it, this structure created an “innovation-less bonsai structure” in which intermediaries extracted hundreds of crores through regulatory shielding while adding minimal infrastructure value.

The regulatory architecture itself suffered from compromised independence. The 2010 amendments to the Bangladesh Telecommunications Act centralised licensing authority within the Ministry, effectively curtailing BTRC’s ability to independently regulate and develop the sector. Board-level interference in state-owned entities—exemplified by non-competitive appointments and the rule requiring the PTD Secretary to serve as chairman of entity boards—created persistent conflicts of interest. Structural weaknesses including dormant talent pipelines at DoT, reliance on deputation and contract staff at MOCSLA, and prolonged vacancy backlogs at the Directorate of Posts eroded institutional capacity from within.

Concurrently, the national policy environment has evolved decisively. The National Digital Transformation Strategy (2025–2030) established nine strategic pillars—from Unified Digital Public Infrastructure and Data Sovereignty to Future-Ready Human Capital—that collectively demand a telecommunications backbone capable of delivering interoperable, secure, and affordable connectivity to every citizen. The legal triad of the National Data Governance Ordinance (NDGO), Personal Data Protection Ordinance (PDPO), and Cyber Safety Ordinance (CSO), all enacted in 2025, creates enforceable obligations for data governance, privacy, and cybersecurity that require every PTD entity to align its operations with new compliance standards. The PTD sector cannot merely digitise its existing processes; it must fundamentally restructure its institutions, modernise its regulatory framework, and reorient its operational priorities to serve as the “physical rail” upon which the nation’s digital ambitions travel.


1.2 Current Institutional Landscape and Challenges

The challenges confronting PTD’s institutional ecosystem are systemic, interconnected, and documented. This section catalogues them across six dimensions that collectively define the reform imperative.

1.2.1 Absence of Strategic Planning and Master Plans

The single most consequential finding of the White Paper is that no major PTD entity possesses a structured master plan. Projects have been routinely launched without credible demand assessments or sustainability metrics. This has resulted in infrastructure that does not correspond to market needs, assets that remain underutilised after completion, and capital expenditures that fail to translate into citizen-facing service improvements. The Directorate of Posts, for example, operates one of the largest physical networks in the country—10,000 post offices—yet has no coherent digital roadmap to transform this network into a platform for e-commerce logistics, financial inclusion, or citizen service delivery. TSS, once positioned as a domestic telecom equipment manufacturer, has experienced a declining manufacturing base without a strategic plan for repositioning itself within the 5G and IoT supply chain. This planning vacuum is not a resource problem; it is a governance failure that this strategy must address as a first-order priority.

1.2.2 Regulatory Fragmentation and Capture

The licensing and regulatory architecture inherited from the ILDTS 2010 framework created concentrated ownership structures, cross-layered affiliations, and artificial monopolies. BTRC and MOCSLA both exhibited regulatory inertia, fragmented enforcement, and limited transparency. MOCSLA’s largely manual, non-integrated processes and incomplete licensee database left many courier operators informal or under-regulated, limiting revenue capture and consumer protection in the rapidly growing e-commerce delivery ecosystem. Frequent policy reversals and ad hoc decisions heightened investor uncertainty and weakened long-term strategic planning.

The Telecommunications Network and Licensing Policy 2025 has begun to address these structural deficiencies by simplifying the licensing architecture from over twenty categories to four core types: Access Network Service Providers (ANSP), National Infrastructure and Connectivity Service Providers (NICSP), International Connectivity Service Providers (ICSP), and Non-Terrestrial Networks and Service Providers (NTNSP). Existing IGW, IIG, ICX, and NIX licences will expire naturally, with most lapsing by 2027. The policy introduces technology-neutral licensing, formal recognition of Mobile Virtual Network Operators (MVNOs), Significant Market Power (SMP) remedies, mandatory infrastructure sharing, and a National Quality of Service Dashboard with near-real-time performance data. However, policy approval is only the first step; the translation of policy into enforceable guidelines, licensing instruments, and institutional practice constitutes the core implementation challenge.

1.2.3 Governance Weaknesses in State-Owned Entities

Human resource and governance failures recur across PTD entities. The White Paper documented sector-wide patterns of non-competitive appointments, frequent departures from merit-based recruitment, credential-deficient promotions, and politically influenced board decisions that undermined institutional capability, accountability, and service delivery. The structural rule that the PTD Secretary serves as chairman of entity boards created an inherent conflict of interest between the regulatory and operational functions of the Division. At BTRC, non-competitive appointments and board-level interference compromised regulatory impartiality. At TSS, politically influenced recruitment weakened the organisation’s technical capacity. At BTCL, the transition from the legacy Bangladesh Telegraph and Telephone Board left unresolved liabilities and an organisational culture resistant to commercial discipline.

The Bangladesh Telecommunication (Amendment) Ordinance 2025 has taken significant steps to remedy these governance deficiencies at the regulatory level by restoring BTRC’s institutional independence, introducing an accountability committee chaired by the head of the relevant parliamentary standing committee, mandating quarterly public hearings, and establishing safeguards against conflicts of interest. The parallel challenge—reforming governance structures within the operational entities (BTCL, Teletalk, BSCCPLC, BSCL, TSS, BCSL, DoP)—requires equally systematic attention through this strategy.

1.2.4 Infrastructure Underutilisation and Connectivity Gaps

Bangladesh’s bandwidth consumption stands at approximately 8,500 Gbps, of which 4,000 Gbps is supplied through submarine cables operated by BSCCPLC and the remainder through terrestrial links from India. Under the previous administration, more than 65 per cent of BSCCPLC’s submarine cable capacity remained unused. Under the current reform dispensation, bandwidth supply has increased by over 2.2 terabits in one year—a growth rate of 105 per cent—and BTRC has mandated that all IIG operators use at least 50 per cent submarine bandwidth, reducing dependence on Indian terrestrial supply. The government has signed agreements to participate in the revised routes of SEA-ME-WE 4, SEA-ME-WE 5, and SEA-ME-WE 6 submarine cable systems.

Despite these advances, structural connectivity gaps persist. Only 30 per cent of mobile towers are connected via optical fibre, with most relying on low-capacity microwave links. Thousands of towers operate with only 300 Mbps of bandwidth shared among thousands of users. The Social Obligation Fund (SOF), intended to finance connectivity in underserved areas, has been characterised by dormant funds, poor coordination, absence of merit-based project selection, weak impact evaluation, and procurement irregularities. BTCL’s fixed-line network, while extensive, requires fundamental modernisation to serve as a viable broadband access platform. The entry of Starlink—with four ground stations operational in Gazipur, Rajshahi, and Jashore and 880 Gbps of installed capacity—introduces a complementary non-terrestrial layer, but its integration into the broader connectivity architecture requires careful regulatory and commercial design.

1.2.5 Postal Sector Stagnation

The postal sector, governed by the Post Office Act of 1898 (as amended in 2010), operates one of the most geographically distributed institutional networks in Bangladesh. The 2010 amendment expanded the Directorate of Posts’ mandate to include universal postal service provision, e-commerce facilitation, remittance transfer, postal banking, and data management. However, the operational reality has not kept pace with the legislative mandate. The Directorate of Posts faces prolonged vacancy backlogs, manual and paper-based processes, and an absence of digital infrastructure capable of supporting e-logistics, digital financial services, or interoperable citizen service delivery. Meanwhile, MOCSLA’s inability to effectively regulate the courier and mailing sector—despite its rapid growth driven by e-commerce—represents both a governance failure and a significant foregone revenue opportunity.

1.2.6 Skills Deficit and Institutional Capacity Erosion

The sector suffers from a critical skills mismatch between institutional needs and available human capital. The abolition of the dedicated Telecom Cadre removed the institutional mechanism for developing and retaining specialised technical expertise within PTD and its entities. DoT, which should serve as the Division’s policy and technical arm, operates with dormant talent pipelines. Reliance on deputation and contract staff across multiple entities has produced a workforce without institutional continuity or deep sectoral expertise. TSS, which requires engineering capacity for its manufacturing and R&D mandate, has been particularly affected by recruitment practices that prioritised political considerations over technical qualifications. The reintroduction of a Telecom Cadre and the development of structured capacity-building programmes are essential preconditions for any sustainable reform.


1.3 Vision Statement

“To establish a transparent, accountable, and digitally sovereign posts and telecommunications ecosystem that delivers affordable, secure, and inclusive connectivity as the foundation for Bangladesh’s national digital transformation.”

This vision recognises that the posts and telecommunications sector is not an end in itself but the foundational infrastructure upon which every other dimension of digital transformation depends. The National Digital Transformation Strategy’s ambitions for interoperable Digital Public Infrastructure, 95 per cent end-to-end digital government services, and a $10 billion ICT export economy by 2030 cannot materialise without a telecommunications backbone that is reliable, affordable, and available to every citizen. The vision is grounded in three foundational commitments: governance integrity as the precondition for infrastructure investment; digital sovereignty as the principle for technology and vendor decisions; and inclusive access as the measure of success.

1.4 Mission Statement

“To reform PTD’s institutional architecture, modernise its regulatory framework, and operationalise its entities as transparent, performance-driven organisations that deliver universal connectivity, competitive market structures, and citizen-centric postal and telecommunications services—aligned with the Bangladesh National Digital Architecture (BNDIA) and the legal requirements of the NDGO, PDPO, and CSO.”

The mission translates the vision into three actionable mandates. First, institutional reform: restructuring governance, recruitment, and accountability mechanisms across all ten entities to eliminate the conflicts of interest, planning vacuums, and procurement failures documented by the White Paper. Second, regulatory modernisation: completing the implementation of the Telecommunications Network and Licensing Policy 2025 and the Bangladesh Telecommunication (Amendment) Ordinance 2025 through enforceable guidelines, digital regulatory platforms, and independent oversight mechanisms. Third, operational transformation: deploying measurable programmes for infrastructure expansion, service quality improvement, postal sector digitisation, and workforce development that deliver citizen-facing outcomes tracked through published KPIs.


1.5 Guiding Principles

The following principles guide all reform initiatives, policy decisions, and institutional actions under this strategy. They are derived from the governance failures identified by the White Paper, the policy framework established by the National Digital Transformation Strategy, and the legal obligations created by the 2025 legislative triad.

Principle

Description

Governance-Led Reform

Connectivity outcomes depend fundamentally on governance quality rather than technology alone. Every infrastructure investment, licensing decision, and institutional restructuring must be preceded by governance safeguards: transparent procurement, independent feasibility assessment, published performance metrics, and accountability mechanisms.

Regulatory Independence and Accountability

BTRC must operate as an independent regulator, free from ministerial interference in licensing, tariff setting, and enforcement decisions. This independence must be matched by accountability through the parliamentary oversight committee, mandatory public hearings, and published regulatory impact assessments.

Digital Sovereignty

Government ownership of critical source code, vendor-neutral procurement, open standards, and control over national data assets. Technology decisions must prevent vendor lock-in and ensure that Bangladesh retains the capacity to operate, maintain, and evolve its telecommunications infrastructure independently.

Evidence-Based Planning

All projects must be grounded in rigorous, independent feasibility studies, credible demand assessments, and sustainability metrics. The absence of master plans across PTD entities must be replaced by structured planning frameworks that are publicly available and subject to third-party evaluation.

Citizen-Centric Service Design

All services—whether telecommunications, postal, or regulatory—must be designed from the citizen’s perspective. This means mobile-first interfaces, Bangla-first accessibility, end-to-end digital delivery, and elimination of the intermediary structures that compel citizens to rely on middlemen.

Competitive Neutrality

State-owned and private operators must be treated under consistent regulatory standards. Public entities are essential to guarantee coverage in underserved areas, but this obligation must not translate into regulatory favouritism that distorts market outcomes.

Fiscal Discipline and Transparency

Every project must embed cost-benefit analysis, milestone-based capital expenditure, and public financial reporting. The era of projects launched without sustainability metrics and completed without impact evaluation must end.

Interoperability and Alignment

All PTD systems, data repositories, and service platforms must be designed to interoperate with the Bangladesh National Digital Architecture (BNDIA) and comply with the data governance requirements of the NDGO. The “collect once, use many times” principle applies equally to telecommunications infrastructure and postal networks.

Inclusive Connectivity

Affordable, reliable connectivity is a foundational right, not a commercial luxury. Reform must specifically address the rural-urban digital divide, the affordability gap, and the exclusion of marginalised communities—students, women, persons with disabilities—from digital participation.



1.6 Global Benchmarks

The reform programme draws on international models where comparable governance and structural challenges have been addressed through systematic regulatory modernisation. These benchmarks are referenced not as aspirational targets to be copied wholesale, but as evidence-based reference points for the design of PTD’s specific reform interventions.

Country / Model

Key Achievements

Relevance to PTD Reform

Estonia

X-Road interoperability platform connecting all government and private sector systems; 99% of services delivered online; blockchain-secured digital identity; annual productivity savings equivalent to 2% of GDP.

Federated architecture model demonstrating that interoperability does not require centralisation. Estonia’s success was built on governance reform preceding technology deployment—directly relevant to PTD’s challenge.

India (TRAI / Aadhaar / UPI)

Independent Telecom Regulatory Authority (TRAI) with transparent tariff-setting and public consultation processes; Aadhaar digital identity for 1.4 billion citizens; UPI payment platform processing billions monthly.

Demonstrates that regulatory independence and scale-appropriate DPI can coexist. TRAI’s transparent consultation process is a model for BTRC’s reform. India’s spectrum auction mechanisms provide benchmarks for Bangladesh’s planned auctions.

United Kingdom (Ofcom / GDS)

Independent converged regulator (Ofcom) overseeing telecoms, broadcasting, and postal services; Government Digital Service (GDS) establishing user-centric design standards; agile procurement and skill-based recruitment.

Converged regulatory model relevant to PTD’s oversight of both telecommunications and postal sectors. Ofcom’s approach to SMP remedies and infrastructure sharing provides direct precedents for BTRC’s implementation of the 2025 Policy.

Singapore (IMDA)

Infocomm Media Development Authority (IMDA) integrating regulation with industry development; centralised tech procurement; rapid prototyping; AI integration in governance.

Demonstrates institutional model where regulator also drives innovation—relevant to DoT’s proposed evolution into a central policy integrator.

Rwanda

Rapid digitisation of postal services into e-logistics and financial inclusion platforms; mobile-first government services; structural transformation of state telecom operator.

Lower-middle-income country that achieved postal sector transformation through governance reform and digital platform deployment—directly comparable to Bangladesh’s Directorate of Posts challenge.







Bangladesh’s approach must be calibrated to its own institutional context. The critical lesson from all benchmarks is consistent: sustainable digital transformation requires governance reform to precede and accompany technology deployment. Countries that invested in infrastructure without first addressing regulatory capture, procurement discipline, and institutional accountability—as Bangladesh’s telecom sector did over the past fifteen years—produced underutilised assets and eroded public trust. This strategy is designed to avoid repeating that sequence.


1.7 Reform Urgency

The urgency of comprehensive reform across PTD’s institutional ecosystem stems from multiple converging pressures, each of which compounds if reform is delayed.

National Digital Transformation Dependencies. The National Digital Transformation Strategy targets 95 per cent end-to-end digital government services, a $10 billion ICT export economy, and 100 per cent fibre-to-tower connectivity by 2030. None of these targets can be achieved without a reformed telecommunications infrastructure. PTD entities control the physical layer—submarine cables, terrestrial fibre, mobile networks, satellite links, and postal delivery networks—upon which every digital service depends. Every month of governance paralysis in these entities delays the national programme.

Market Structure Transformation Window. The Telecommunications Network and Licensing Policy 2025 creates a narrow implementation window. Existing IGW, IIG, ICX, and NIX licences expire by 2027. The transition from the old licensing regime to the new four-tier framework must be managed through detailed guidelines, licensee migration plans, and infrastructure transfer protocols. Failure to execute this transition competently risks regulatory uncertainty, investment hesitation, and service disruption.

Satellite Connectivity Integration. The entry of Starlink—with operational ground stations and a proposal to establish Bangladesh as a regional internet transit hub serving neighbouring countries—introduces both an opportunity and an integration challenge. Starlink’s 880 Gbps installed capacity must be regulated and integrated in a manner that complements rather than undermines submarine cable investments and terrestrial fibre deployment. This requires immediate regulatory clarity on bandwidth export, transit routing, and competitive neutrality between satellite and terrestrial providers.

5G Deployment Readiness. Teletalk holds 60 MHz of spectrum in the 3.5 GHz band for 5G services, but its experimental deployments have not progressed to commercial-scale operations. The Telecommunications Policy 2025 mandates that mobile operators connect 50 per cent of their towers to fibre within 18 months and 80 per cent within three years. Without accelerated fibre deployment and a credible 5G rollout strategy—potentially through spectrum auctions open to all qualified operators—Bangladesh risks falling further behind regional competitors who are already deploying commercial 5G networks.

Postal Sector Relevance. E-commerce transaction volumes continue to grow while the Directorate of Posts’ market share in parcel and logistics services shrinks. The window for transforming post offices into platforms for e-logistics, financial inclusion, and citizen service delivery is narrowing as private courier and logistics operators consolidate market positions. Without rapid digitisation and institutional reform, the postal network’s 10,000 physical touchpoints—the most distributed government infrastructure in the country—will become stranded assets rather than transformation enablers.

Fiscal Sustainability. Several PTD entities operate at a loss or with declining revenues. BTCL’s legacy fixed-line business is structurally unprofitable; Teletalk’s subscriber base and revenue lag far behind private operators; TSS’s manufacturing revenues have diminished. Continued subsidisation without reform produces no return. This strategy’s emphasis on fiscal discipline, milestone-based capital expenditure, and commercial orientation is essential to ensure that public investment translates into sustainable operations.

International Credibility. Bangladesh’s ability to attract foreign direct investment in telecommunications, participate in international submarine cable consortia, and negotiate with global technology partners (including Starlink, Huawei, Nokia, and potential MVNO investors) depends on demonstrating a predictable, transparent, and rules-based regulatory environment. The reforms of 2025—the Ordinance amendment, the Licensing Policy, the ban on internet shutdowns—have begun to rebuild this credibility. The implementation phase must sustain it.


1.8 Strategic Objectives (2025–2030)

The following strategic objectives define the measurable outcomes that this strategy is designed to achieve. Each objective responds directly to the challenges documented in Section 1.2 and aligns with the corresponding pillars of the National Digital Transformation Strategy.

Strategic Objective

Description and Key Actions

Indicative KPIs

Governance and Institutional Reform

Mandate structured master plans for all ten PTD entities; implement open, competitive appointment processes for top management; establish independent board governance with balanced professional representation; reintroduce a dedicated Telecom Cadre; create a cross-agency steering committee with quarterly reporting to the Cabinet Division.

100% entity master plan completion; Open recruitment for all MD/Chairman positions; Telecom Cadre re-established; Quarterly dashboard publication

Regulatory Modernisation

Complete implementation of the Telecommunications Network and Licensing Policy 2025 through enforceable guidelines; operationalise BTRC’s independence per the 2025 Ordinance; deploy the National Quality of Service Dashboard; implement SMP remedies and infrastructure sharing mandates; digitise and integrate MOCSLA’s regulatory processes.

New licensing guidelines issued; QoS Dashboard operational; SMP determinations completed; MOCSLA online registration and tracking platform launched

Infrastructure Expansion and Optimisation

Achieve 50% fibre-to-tower connectivity within 18 months and 80% within 3 years; execute SEA-ME-WE 4/5/6 submarine cable upgrades; integrate Starlink capacity into the national bandwidth architecture; deploy BTCL MVNO service; complete BTCL network modernisation.

Fibre-to-tower percentage; Submarine cable capacity utilisation above 70%; BTCL MVNO subscribers; Bandwidth export volume

Postal Sector Digital Transformation

Digitise Directorate of Posts operations; establish e-logistics and parcel tracking platform; launch postal digital financial services; integrate post offices into the national e-service delivery framework; reform MOCSLA into an effective courier sector regulator.

E-logistics platform operational; Digital transactions per post office; MOCSLA registered operators as % of market

Domestic Manufacturing and Supply Chain

Reposition TSS as a domestic hardware and IoT device producer; establish strategic partnerships for 5G equipment, CCTV systems, and Wi-Fi router manufacturing; launch software incubation centre.

TSS revenue from new product lines; Local assembly/manufacturing output; Strategic partnership agreements executed

Workforce Development

Reintroduce Telecom Cadre; implement structured capacity-building programmes across all entities; establish technical competency frameworks aligned with 5G, satellite, cybersecurity, and data governance requirements.

Telecom Cadre positions filled; Staff trained per entity per year; Technical certification completion rates

Fiscal Sustainability

Implement milestone-based capital expenditure frameworks; establish cost-benefit analysis requirements for all projects above BDT 50 Crore; reform SOF governance; achieve revenue growth targets for BSCCPLC, BTCL, BSCL, and TSS.

SOF fund utilisation rate; Entity revenue growth; Project cost overrun reduction

Citizen-Centric Service Delivery

Ensure affordable broadband access benchmarked to ITU affordability targets; deploy mobile-first, Bangla-first interfaces for all PTD digital services; establish citizen feedback mechanisms for telecom and postal services.

Broadband affordability index; Citizen satisfaction survey scores; Complaint resolution time



1.9 Alignment with the National Digital Transformation Strategy

This strategy is designed as the sectoral implementation framework for the telecommunications and postal dimensions of the National Digital Transformation Strategy (2025–2030). The alignment is structural, not nominal. Each PTD reform initiative maps directly to one or more of the nine strategic pillars established by the national strategy.

NDTS Strategic Pillar

PTD Alignment and Contribution

Unified Digital Public Infrastructure

BSCCPLC submarine cable upgrades, BTCL fibre network modernisation, Starlink integration, National Fiber Bank coordination—collectively delivering the physical connectivity layer upon which DPI operates.

Citizen-Centric Governance

Postal sector digitisation, BTCL service delivery improvements, QoS Dashboard, citizen feedback mechanisms—ensuring that telecom and postal services meet the “Digital by Default” and “Bangla First” mandates.

Data Sovereignty and Legal Certainty

Compliance of all PTD entities with NDGO, PDPO, and CSO requirements; digital sovereignty in procurement; government source code ownership; vendor-neutral technology choices.

National Connectivity Backbone

Fibre-to-tower targets, submarine cable capacity expansion, Starlink non-terrestrial layer, rural connectivity through reformed SOF—directly constituting the connectivity backbone referenced by the national strategy.

Autonomous Regulatory Oversight

BTRC independence under the 2025 Ordinance; MOCSLA reform; DoT evolution as policy integrator—establishing the regulatory architecture within PTD’s domain.

Economic Resilience and Innovation

MVNO market entry, competitive licensing framework, bandwidth export potential, TSS manufacturing repositioning—creating the conditions for investment, innovation, and export growth.

Future-Ready Human Capital

Telecom Cadre reintroduction, technical capacity building, TSS software incubation centre—developing the specialised workforce the sector requires.

Institutional Interoperability

Integration of PTD systems with BNDIA standards, postal network integration with e-service delivery framework, MOCSLA digitisation—eliminating data silos within the sector.

Proactive Security and Resilience

Compliance with CSO cybersecurity requirements, CIS (Centre for Information Support) establishment replacing NTMC, lawful interception under judicial oversight—ensuring telecommunications infrastructure supports national security without compromising civil liberties.





This alignment ensures that PTD’s reform programme is not an isolated sectoral exercise but an integral component of the national digital transformation. Progress on PTD’s strategic objectives directly advances the national strategy’s KPIs; conversely, failure within the telecommunications and postal sector cascades as delay across every other dimension of digital transformation.



— End of Chapter 1 —


2. Legal Landscape

2.1 Why Legal Reform Matters

The posts and telecommunications sector in Bangladesh has, for over a decade, operated under a legal architecture that simultaneously enabled regulatory capture, insulated intermediaries from competition, and failed to keep pace with the transformation of digital markets. Chapter 1 documented the governance failures that this architecture produced: the absence of master plans, procurement irregularities across BDT 20,009.82 Crore in projects, politically compromised regulatory independence, and a postal sector governed by a statute enacted in 1898. The legal reforms of 2025 represent the most comprehensive restructuring of the sector’s legislative foundations since the enactment of the Bangladesh Telecommunication Act in 2001.

The importance of legal reform to the digital transformation programme cannot be overstated. Infrastructure investments, institutional restructuring, and service delivery improvements all operate within a framework of legal permissions and constraints. When the legal framework concentrates licensing authority within a Ministry, as the 2010 amendments did, independent regulation becomes structurally impossible regardless of the quality of the regulator’s personnel. When a postal statute lacks provisions for digital logistics, electronic postage, or data protection compliance, no operational initiative can achieve sustainable transformation. When surveillance powers lack judicial oversight and internet shutdowns can be ordered without legal constraint, investor confidence erodes and citizen trust collapses.

This chapter examines the four principal legal instruments that collectively reshape PTD’s operating environment: the Bangladesh Telecommunication (Amendment) Ordinance 2025, the Telecommunications Network and Licensing Policy 2025, the Postal Services Ordinance 2025 (draft), and the Mailing Operator and Courier Service Development and Regulatory Authority Ordinance 2025 (pending). It analyses each instrument’s key provisions, situates them within the broader national legal reform programme—including the Cyber Safety Ordinance (CSO), Personal Data Protection Ordinance (PDPO), and National Data Governance Ordinance (NDGO)—and identifies the implementation imperatives that determine whether legislative intent translates into operational reality.

Internationally, the relationship between legal framework quality and telecommunications sector performance is well established. The International Telecommunication Union’s ICT Regulatory Tracker consistently demonstrates that countries with independent regulatory authorities, technology-neutral licensing, and enforceable consumer protections achieve higher connectivity outcomes per capita investment. The European Electronic Communications Code (2018), India’s Telecommunications Act (2023), and Singapore’s Telecommunications Act (revised 2024) each represent recent legislative modernisations that Bangladesh’s 2025 reforms parallel in structural intent, though adapted to the specific governance challenges documented by the White Paper.


2.2 Bangladesh Telecommunication (Amendment) Ordinance 2025

2.2.1 Background and Legislative Context

The Bangladesh Telecommunication Act 2001 (Act No. XVIII of 2001) established the Bangladesh Telecommunication Regulatory Commission (BTRC) as an independent statutory body with authority over licensing, tariff regulation, spectrum management, and market oversight. This foundational statute was designed to separate the regulatory function from the operational function—a principle recognised internationally as essential to effective sector governance.

The Bangladesh Telecommunication (Amendment) Act 2010 fundamentally altered this architecture. The 2010 amendments transferred final authority over licence issuance, renewal, service approval, and tariff-setting from BTRC to the Ministry of Posts and Telecommunications. As the White Paper characterised it, this legislation marked the “Beginning of the End” for independent regulation. The Ministry’s assumption of licensing authority created a structural environment in which regulatory decisions could be influenced by political considerations rather than market analysis, consumer welfare, or evidence-based policy. The ILDTS Policy 2010, which followed shortly thereafter, was a direct product of this compromised regulatory environment—introducing a segmented licensing framework that lacked baseline demand analysis, failed to implement competitive safeguards, and enabled the formation of intermediary structures that extracted hundreds of crores in value while adding minimal infrastructure investment.

The Bangladesh Telecommunication (Amendment) Ordinance 2025, approved by the Advisory Council on 24 December 2025, reverses the most consequential elements of the 2010 amendments and introduces new provisions that did not exist in either the original 2001 Act or its 2010 iteration. It represents a legislative response to fifteen years of documented regulatory failure.

2.2.2 Key Provisions

Restoration of BTRC’s Regulatory Independence. The Ordinance restores to BTRC the authority over licensing, service approval, and tariff regulation that the 2010 amendments had transferred to the Ministry. This restoration is not merely administrative; it re-establishes the structural separation between policymaking (which remains with PTD) and regulation (which reverts to BTRC). International best practice, as codified in ITU recommendations and exemplified by regulators such as India’s Telecom Regulatory Authority (TRAI), the United Kingdom’s Ofcom, and Singapore’s Infocomm Media Development Authority (IMDA), consistently demonstrates that regulatory independence from the political executive is the single most important institutional precondition for competitive, investor-friendly telecommunications markets.



Permanent Prohibition on Internet Shutdowns (Section 97). The Ordinance introduces a permanent ban on state-ordered internet and telecommunications shutdowns, recognising continuous internet access as a citizen’s right. This provision is significant both domestically and internationally. Between 2018 and 2024, Bangladesh experienced multiple instances of internet shutdowns, including nationwide mobile internet blackouts during the July 2024 protests, platform-specific blocks on social media and messaging applications, and connectivity restrictions in refugee camps. These shutdowns imposed measurable economic costs, disrupted essential digital services, and eroded Bangladesh’s international credibility. The ban aligns Bangladesh with an emerging global norm: the United Nations Human Rights Council has repeatedly condemned internet shutdowns as violations of the right to freedom of expression, and the African Commission on Human and Peoples’ Rights adopted a resolution in 2016 declaring that shutdowns violate the African Charter. Bangladesh is among the first countries in South Asia to enact a permanent statutory prohibition.

Abolition of the NTMC and Establishment of the Centre for Information Support (CIS). The National Telecommunications Monitoring Centre (NTMC), which operated under the Ministry of Home Affairs, is abolished. In its place, a Centre for Information Support (CIS) is established under the Ministry of Home Affairs with narrowly defined functions and explicit safeguards. The NTMC’s operations had been characterised by opacity, absence of judicial oversight, and the potential for abuse of surveillance capabilities. The CIS is subject to the procedural requirements of the Cyber Safety Ordinance 2025, which mandates judicial authorisation for lawful interception.

Anti-Surveillance Protections (Section 71). The extraction of SIM registration data or device identification data for surveillance purposes is made a punishable offence. This provision addresses the documented practice of using telecommunications subscriber data for extrajudicial monitoring. It creates a legal boundary between lawful interception under judicial authorisation and the previously unconstrained access to subscriber metadata.

Accountability Committee and Public Hearings. An accountability committee, chaired by the head of the relevant parliamentary standing committee, is established to oversee BTRC’s operations. Mandatory public hearings must be conducted every four months, with published follow-up actions. This dual mechanism—parliamentary oversight and mandatory public engagement—is designed to balance BTRC’s restored independence with transparent accountability. The model draws on international precedents: TRAI in India conducts public consultations on all major regulatory decisions; Ofcom in the United Kingdom publishes impact assessments with every regulatory intervention; the Australian Communications and Media Authority (ACMA) operates under a legislative requirement for regular performance reviews by the Minister.

Revised Speech Offences. The Ordinance narrows speech-related offences to criminalise only incitement to violence, aligning the telecommunications law with the Cyber Safety Ordinance 2025. The overly broad provisions of prior legislation—which had been used to prosecute journalists, bloggers, and activists for online speech—are replaced by a narrower formulation that balances security interests with free expression. This alignment ensures that the telecommunications regulatory framework does not operate as a parallel censorship mechanism.

Reduced Licence Processing Timelines and Scaled Penalties.
