The Ordinance introduces streamlined timelines for licence processing and reduces penalty provisions that had been characterised as disproportionate. The objective is to lower barriers to market entry and reduce the regulatory burden on operators, consistent with the broader market liberalisation goals of the Telecommunications Network and Licensing Policy 2025.

2.2.3 Implementation Requirements

The Ordinance establishes the legislative framework; its translation into operational reality requires several implementation actions. BTRC must develop and publish new procedural guidelines for its restored licensing and tariff-setting functions within six months of the Ordinance’s commencement. The accountability committee must be constituted and its terms of reference gazetted. The first cycle of mandatory public hearings must be scheduled and publicised. The transition protocols for dismantling NTMC and establishing CIS must be executed within the Ministry of Home Affairs, with appropriate data handling and asset transfer procedures. Training programmes for BTRC staff on the exercise of restored regulatory functions must be designed and deployed. Without these implementation steps, the Ordinance’s intent remains aspirational rather than operational.



Implementation KPIs: Bangladesh Telecommunication (Amendment) Ordinance 2025

Indicator

Timeline

Responsible Entity

BTRC licensing guidelines published

Within 6 months of commencement

BTRC

Accountability committee constituted

Within 3 months

PTD / Parliamentary Standing Committee

First public hearing cycle completed

Within 4 months of committee formation

BTRC

CIS operational; NTMC dissolved

Within 6 months

Ministry of Home Affairs

QoS Dashboard operational with real-time data

Within 12 months

BTRC

Zero internet shutdowns post-commencement

Continuous

BTRC / CIS

Staff training on restored functions completed

Within 9 months

BTRC

2.3 Telecommunications Network and Licensing Policy 2025

2.3.1 Background and Rationale

The Telecommunications Network and Licensing Policy 2025 was gazetted on 22 September 2025, replacing the International Long Distance Telecommunication Service (ILDTS) Policy 2010. The gazette notification simultaneously repealed the ILDTS Policy 2010 upon the new policy’s entry into force. The rationale for replacement was documented extensively in the White Paper: the ILDTS 2010 framework had created over twenty licence categories, imposed rigid segmentation between service layers, lacked demand-based calibration, and enabled cartel-like behaviour among intermediaries—particularly within the IGW Operators Forum (IOF), where operators coordinated on interconnection terms without effective regulatory oversight.

The policy’s design reflects a global shift toward converged, technology-neutral licensing. The European Union’s European Electronic Communications Code (2018) established the principle that service authorisation should not prescribe the technology through which services are delivered. India’s Unified Licence regime consolidated multiple service-specific licences into a single authorisation framework. Singapore’s Facilities-Based Operator (FBO) and Services-Based Operator (SBO) framework provides a simplified two-tier model. The Bangladesh policy adopts a comparable rationalisation, reducing complexity from over twenty categories to four core types while introducing provisions for infrastructure sharing, competitive safeguards, and quality of service transparency that were absent from the prior regime.

2.3.2 New Licensing Architecture

The policy establishes a three-tier network topology with four core licence categories, replacing the fragmented structure of IGWs, IIGs, ICXs, NIXs, and numerous specialised permits:

Licence Category

Scope of Operations

Key Conditions

Access Network Service Provider (ANSP)

Cellular mobile operators, fixed telecom operators, and telecom-enabled service providers. Delivers services directly to end users.

Minimum 15% domestic ownership for cellular operators (85% foreign cap). Must connect 50% of towers to fibre within 18 months; 80% within 3 years.

National Infrastructure and Connectivity Service Provider (NICSP)

National transmission, interconnection, and internet exchange functions. Backbone infrastructure layer between international connectivity and access networks.

45–55% foreign ownership cap. Mandatory infrastructure sharing obligations. Must provide non-discriminatory wholesale access to ANSPs.

International Connectivity Service Provider (ICSP)

International bandwidth, submarine cable landing, terrestrial cross-border links, and satellite earth station operations. Replaces IGW and IIG categories.

49% foreign ownership cap. Submarine cable operators and satellite gateway operators operate at this tier. Must ensure minimum 50% submarine bandwidth usage.

Non-Terrestrial Networks and Service Provider (NTNSP)

Satellite-based communications, including LEO, MEO, GEO, and HAPS services. First formal recognition in Bangladesh licensing.

Subject to specific terms per technology. Formal framework for Starlink-type satellite internet and future non-terrestrial entrants.



Existing licences issued under the ILDTS 2010 framework remain valid under their original terms and conditions, subject to the new policy’s provisions. IGW, IIG, ICX, and NIX licences will expire naturally, with most lapsing by 2027. This phased transition avoids abrupt disruption while ensuring that the market converges toward the simplified architecture within a defined timeframe.

2.3.3 Competitive Safeguards and Market Structure Reforms

The policy introduces several mechanisms to address the market concentration and anti-competitive behaviour documented in the White Paper:

Significant Market Power (SMP) Remedies. BTRC is empowered to designate operators with significant market power and impose ex-ante remedies, including mandatory wholesale access, price controls, and accounting separation. This provision mirrors the SMP framework employed by Ofcom in the United Kingdom and the European Commission under the European Electronic Communications Code. The absence of SMP remedies under the ILDTS 2010 framework allowed dominant operators to leverage market position without regulatory constraint.

Mandatory Infrastructure Sharing. All licensees are required to share passive and active infrastructure—including fibre networks, towers, radio access networks, and core network elements—under guidelines to be issued by BTRC. Infrastructure sharing reduces duplication, lowers barriers to entry, and accelerates coverage expansion. India’s infrastructure sharing regime, governed by TRAI’s IP-I and UASL guidelines, is credited with enabling the rapid expansion of mobile broadband coverage to over 700 million users. The Bangladesh policy mandates that BTRC issue comprehensive infrastructure sharing guidelines, encompassing both passive elements (ducts, poles, towers) and active elements (spectrum, radio networks).

MVNO Recognition. The policy formally recognises Mobile Virtual Network Operators (MVNOs) for the first time in Bangladesh’s licensing framework. MVNOs—which operate on host network infrastructure without owning spectrum—have been instrumental in increasing market competition and consumer choice globally. BTCL’s approved MVNO pilot is a direct product of this policy enablement. The MVNO framework is expected to create additional market entry opportunities, particularly for niche service providers targeting underserved segments.

National Quality of Service Dashboard. The policy mandates the creation of a publicly accessible dashboard displaying near-real-time quality of service data for all licensed operators. This transparency mechanism enables consumers, regulators, and policymakers to monitor network performance against published benchmarks. Brazil’s Anatel publishes quarterly QoS reports with operator-specific metrics; India’s TRAI MySpeed portal crowdsources speed data from millions of users. Bangladesh’s dashboard is intended to provide a comparable level of public accountability.

Foreign Ownership Caps with Tiered Structure. The policy establishes differentiated foreign ownership limits across the network tiers: 85 per cent for cellular mobile operators (requiring a minimum 15 per cent domestic holding), 49 per cent for international connectivity, and 45–55 per cent for national infrastructure. This tiered approach balances the need for foreign investment and technology transfer with the objective of ensuring domestic stakeholder participation in critical national infrastructure. The design draws on comparative frameworks: India permits 100 per cent FDI in telecommunications but maintains security conditions; the Philippines recently raised its foreign ownership cap from 40 per cent to 100 per cent for telecommunications carriers.

2.3.4 Fibre Connectivity Targets

The policy establishes binding fibre connectivity milestones for mobile operators: 50 per cent of towers connected to optical fibre within 18 months of policy commencement, and 80 per cent within three years. This is a critical provision. As documented in Chapter 1, only approximately 30 per cent of Bangladesh’s mobile towers are currently connected via fibre, with the majority relying on low-capacity microwave links that constrain data throughput. The National Digital Transformation Strategy’s target of 100 per cent fibre-to-tower connectivity by 2030 depends on the accelerated execution of these intermediate milestones. Operators that fail to meet the targets face enforcement actions under BTRC’s licensing conditions, including potential licence amendments.

2.3.5 Implementation Requirements

The policy’s effectiveness depends on the timely issuance of implementing guidelines by BTRC in several areas: detailed licensing guidelines for each of the four categories; infrastructure sharing guidelines encompassing both passive and active elements; SMP determination criteria and remedy procedures; MVNO operational guidelines; the National Quality of Service Dashboard technical specifications and data collection protocols; and cross-ownership restrictions to prevent concentration across network tiers. BTRC must also develop a migration roadmap for existing licensees, including timeline, fee structures, and transitional conditions. The policy explicitly reserves to the government the authority to grant exemptions for state-owned telecommunications entities, a provision that must be exercised transparently and subject to published criteria to avoid recreating the regulatory asymmetries of the prior regime.



Implementation KPIs: Telecommunications Network and Licensing Policy 2025

Indicator

Timeline

Responsible Entity

BTRC licensing guidelines for all 4 categories issued

Within 6 months of policy commencement

BTRC

Infrastructure sharing guidelines published

Within 9 months

BTRC

SMP determination framework operational

Within 12 months

BTRC

QoS Dashboard live with real-time operator data

Within 12 months

BTRC

50% fibre-to-tower connectivity achieved

Within 18 months

Licensed operators / BTRC oversight

MVNO operational guidelines issued

Within 6 months

BTRC

Legacy IGW/IIG/ICX/NIX licences expired or migrated

By 2027

BTRC / licensees

80% fibre-to-tower connectivity achieved

Within 3 years

Licensed operators / BTRC oversight


2.4 Postal Services Ordinance 2026

2.4.1 Context and Strategic Rationale

For over a century, the postal backbone of Bangladesh was governed by the Post Office Act, 1898—a colonial instrument designed primarily for the secure movement of royal mail and the maintenance of imperial communication lines. While effective in the pre-digital era, this archaic framework failed to address the complexities of the 21st-century digital economy, the explosion of e-commerce, the necessity of data sovereignty, and the imperative of financial inclusion.

The enactment of the Postal Services Ordinance, 2026 marks a definitive paradigm shift. It repeals the 1898 Act and establishes a unified, time-befitting legal structure aimed at the modernization, standardization, and digital transformation of the postal, courier, and parcel sectors. This Ordinance is not merely a regulatory update; it is a strategic instrument that redefines the Department of Post (Bangladesh Post) not just as a mail carrier, but as the "Designated Operator" responsible for the nation’s logistics sovereignty, a trustee of citizen wealth through postal banking, and the custodian of the National Address Infrastructure.

Crucially, the Ordinance recognizes the postal network as an "Essential Service". It mandates that in times of national crisis, disaster, or emergency, the postal network must remain operational to ensure the continuity of state communication and the delivery of essential goods, granting it priority status equivalent to other emergency services.

2.4.2 The Designated Operator: Redefining the National Post

The Ordinance formally designates the Department of Post as the country’s sole "Designated Operator" (DO). This aligns Bangladesh with the Universal Postal Union (UPU) convention, establishing a "Single Postal Territory" where the state guarantees uninterrupted communication to every citizen, regardless of geography or economic status.

1. Mandate for Universal Postal Service (UPS): Unlike private operators driven by profit, the Designated Operator is legally bound to provide "Universal Postal Service"—a guaranteed minimum level of service at an affordable, uniform price to every address in Bangladesh. This includes the delivery of letters, personal documents, judicial notices, public examination papers, and safety net allowances. The Ordinance ensures that this mandate is not an unfunded liability; it obligates the Government to provide necessary budget allocations and administrative support to maintain this critical network.

2. The Distinction of Services: To ensure market fairness while protecting state interests, the Ordinance categorizes postal services into distinct tiers:

  • Basic Postal Service: The core obligation of collecting, processing, and delivering mail under UPU quality standards.

  • Entitled Postal Service (Adhikari Seva): Historic services provided by the state for financial and social inclusion, such as Postal Life Insurance and Savings Bank operations.

  • Agency Services: The Post acting as a "proxy operator" for other government or private entities (e.g., passport delivery, allowance distribution).

  • Commercial & Premium Services: High-speed "Express" and specialized "Premium" services where the Post competes with private players based on market rates.

2.4.3 The Financial Powerhouse: From Savings to "Postal Bank"

A cornerstone of the 2026 Ordinance is the revitalization of the Post Office as a primary engine for financial inclusion. Recognizing that the postal network reaches deep into rural areas where commercial banks often fear to tread, the law empowers the Department to expand its financial horizon significantly.

1. Digital Financial Services & Interoperability: The Ordinance authorizes the Department to operate Money Transfer Services (MTS), digital wallets, and electronic money transfers. Crucially, it mandates the digital transformation of all financial services, moving away from manual ledgers to a centralized, technology-driven core banking environment. This allows for "Money Distribution" services using the postal network as a cash-out/cash-in mesh for other financial institutions.

2. Treasury and Investment Management: The law integrates postal financial operations with the national treasury system. All savings and financial service accumulations are to be managed through a Treasury Single Account (TSA) mechanism. However, it grants the Department the strategic flexibility to invest these funds in Treasury Bonds or other government instruments, ensuring that citizen deposits generate returns that can be shared as profit.

3. The Vision of a Postal Bank: Perhaps the most transformative provision is the enabling clause for the future. The Government is empowered to establish a full-fledged "Postal Bank" to institutionalize rural and small-scale savings, graduating the current Post Office Savings Bank into a formal banking entity subject to appropriate banking regulations. This provision provides the legal roadmap for separating the financial arm of the Post into a specialized corporate entity.

2.4.4 Postal Life Insurance (PLI): A Sovereign Guarantee

The Ordinance reinforces the unique position of Postal Life Insurance (PLI) as the oldest and most secure insurer in the country, backed by a sovereign state guarantee.

  • Inclusivity: PLI is mandated to be inclusive, offering policies not just to government employees but to any citizen with a regular income, including special provisions for risky professions like the defense forces.

  • Product Innovation: The Department is authorized to introduce modern insurance products such as education insurance, marriage insurance, and health insurance, moving beyond simple endowment policies.

  • Fund Management: A dedicated "Post Office Insurance Fund" will be maintained on a "no-profit, no-loss" basis, ensuring that surpluses are returned to policyholders as bonuses rather than siphoned off as corporate profit. The fund is to be valued regularly by a certified actuary to ensure solvency.

2.4.5 National Address Infrastructure: The Post as Data Fiduciary

In a digital economy, a physical address is as critical as a digital IP address. The Ordinance assigns the Department of Post the monumental task of establishing and maintaining the National Address and Data Infrastructure.

1. Data Fiduciary Role: The Department is designated as the "Data Fiduciary" for national address data. It is responsible for identifying every holding or structure in Bangladesh, assigning it a unique geolocation, and maintaining a dynamic database of these addresses. This is a strategic asset for the entire nation—vital for e-commerce delivery, emergency response, and urban planning.

2. Addressing the "Floating" Population: The law explicitly addresses the reality of climate change and migration. It mandates the preservation of address history for citizens displaced by river erosion or natural disasters ("Natural Migration"), ensuring that a citizen does not lose their identity simply because their land was washed away. The Post must develop methodologies to trace and serve floating populations who lack a fixed geographic abode.

3. Digital Codes and Interoperability: The Ordinance mandates the use of Postcodes and authorizes the introduction of innovative "Digital Location Codes". It also establishes a "Central Logistics Tracking Platform," a digital hub that ensures interoperability between the Post and private couriers. This platform will allow a user to track a package across different operators using a single portal, aiming to create a seamless national logistics grid.

2.4.6 Regulating the Private Sector: The Licensing Authority

The Ordinance brings discipline to the booming, yet chaotic, private courier and e-commerce delivery sector. It empowers the Mailing Operator and Courier Service Licensing Authority (hereinafter "The Authority") to regulate the market.

1. Licensing Regime: No person or entity can operate a courier or parcel service without a valid license. The Authority will categorize licenses based on service scope (e.g., international, domestic, city-wide) and service nature (e.g., document, heavy parcel). This ends the era of unregulated "grey market" operators. Existing licensees under the 2013 rules are grandfathered in but must transition to the new framework.

2. Level Playing Field & Anti-Monopoly: The Ordinance includes strong anti-trust provisions. It prohibits predatory pricing, dumping, and exclusivity agreements that stifle competition. If a dominant player emerges, the Authority has the power to impose "Price Caps" to prevent exploitation. Crucially, to ensure fair competition between the state and private sectors, the Department of Post is required to maintain "Account Separation," ensuring that subsidies meant for Universal Service are not used to cross-subsidize its commercial competitive services.

3. Consumer Protection: Operators are now legally liable for the safety and secrecy of the mail. The law mandates compensation for lost or damaged goods based on declared value and service terms. It also forces operators to implement effective grievance redressal mechanisms.

2.4.7 Security, Sovereignty, and Customs Integration

As the volume of cross-border e-commerce grows, so does the risk of illicit trade. The Ordinance integrates postal operations with national security and customs frameworks.

1. Electronic Advance Data (EAD): To align with global security standards (WCO and UPU), the Ordinance mandates Electronic Advance Data (EAD). Operators must transmit digital data about the sender, receiver, and contents of international parcels to customs and security agencies before the shipment physically arrives. This allows for risk-based screening and faster clearance of legitimate trade.

2. Know Your Customer (KYC): The law makes KYC mandatory for booking specific types of parcels, particularly international shipments or high-value domestic goods. Operators will be granted limited access to the National Identity (NID) server to verify sender identities in real-time, closing a major loophole used for trafficking contraband.

3. Prohibited Goods & Cyber Security: A strict schedule of prohibited items (explosives, narcotics, counterfeit currency) is established . Furthermore, the Ordinance mandates compliance with the Cyber Safety Ordinance, 2025 and National Data Management Ordinance, 2025, ensuring that the massive amounts of personal data handled by logistics providers are stored and processed securely.

2.4.8 Commercialization: Post Shops and Franchisees

Recognizing that the Department cannot do everything alone, the Ordinance creates frameworks for commercial partnerships and asset utilization.

  • Post Shops: The Department is authorized to convert its post offices into "Post Shops"—commercial hubs that sell not just stamps but also packaging materials, digital devices, and even third-party products, effectively becoming retail outlets for rural communities.

  • Franchise Post Offices: To expand the network without bloating the government payroll, the Designated Operator can appoint "Franchise Postmasters." These are private individuals or small businesses authorized to offer postal services in areas where a full-fledged post office is unviable. This fosters local entrepreneurship while ensuring service coverage.

  • Post E-Centers: These are designated as digital inclusion hubs, offering IT training, e-commerce support, and government e-services to the rural youth, acting as a bridge to the "Smart Bangladesh" vision.

2.4.9 Offenses, Penalties, and Enforcement

The Ordinance replaces the minor fines of 1898 with substantial deterrents suitable for the modern economy.

  • Illegal Operation: Operating a courier service without a license carries a penalty of up to 1 year imprisonment or 10 Lakh Taka fine.

  • Mail Tampering & Theft: Postal employees found guilty of stealing, opening, or destroying mail face up to 7 years imprisonment. This high penalty reflects the "high responsibility" status of postal work.

  • Data Privacy Violations: Unauthorized disclosure of mail contents or personal data is a criminal offense punishable by up to 2 years imprisonment.

  • Administrative Fines: For minor operational breaches, the Licensing Authority can impose administrative fines up to 10 Lakh Taka without needing to go to court, streamlining enforcement.

2.4.10 Governance: The Postal Council

To ensure high-level inter-ministerial coordination and oversight, the Ordinance establishes the Postal Council. Chaired by the Secretary of the Posts and Telecommunications Division, this high-powered body includes representatives from Finance, Commerce, Home Affairs, and Bangladesh Bank.

The Council serves as the ultimate appellate body for grievances against the Department or the Licensing Authority. It has the powers of a civil court to summon records and witnesses, ensuring that the postal sector remains accountable, transparent, and aligned with national development goals.

2.4.11 Comparative Analysis: Old Framework versus New

Dimension

Prior Framework (1898/2010)

Postal Services Ordinance 2026

Governing statute

Post Office Act 1898 (as amended 2010)

Postal Services Ordinance 2026 (complete replacement)

Digital services

No provisions for digital postage, logistics tracking, or e-commerce integration

Digital postage stamps (QR/barcode), central logistics tracking platform, interoperability mandates

Data protection

No data protection requirements

Mandatory PDPO compliance: encryption, data minimisation, breach notification

Licensing authority

Limited authority under Sections 4B/4C; 9 of 23 staff positions filled

Empowered licensing authority with expanded regulatory mandate and enforcement powers

Penalties for unlicensed operations

BDT 50,000–200,000

Up to BDT 10 lakh

Universal service

Designated under Section 4A (2010 amendment); no accounting separation

Universal service obligations with mandatory separate accounting to prevent cross-subsidisation

Financial services

General authorisation for banking/remittance (2010 amendment); no specific framework

Postal Bank establishment (subject to banking regulations)

Competition oversight

Minimal provisions

Non-discrimination enforcement, competition oversight, licensing transparency



2.4.12 Implementation Requirements

The Postal Services Ordinance is in draft stage and must be approved by the Advisory Council, gazetted, and brought into force. Upon commencement, the Directorate of Posts and the licensing authority must develop implementing rules covering digital postage standards, logistics tracking platform specifications, PDPO compliance protocols, universal service accounting methodology, and Postal Bank governance and capitalisation. The draft’s passage is a legislative priority: without it, the postal sector continues to operate under an 1898 statute that provides no legal basis for the digital services, data protection compliance, or institutional modernisation that this strategy requires.

2.4.13 Innovation and The Road Ahead

Finally, the Ordinance explicitly supports innovation through "Regulatory Sandboxes." It allows the Department and private operators to test new technologies (like drone delivery or blockchain stamps) in a controlled environment with temporary regulatory relaxation. It also legalizes "Digital Stamps" (QR codes, encrypted tokens), paving the way for a paperless postage system.

The Postal Services Ordinance, 2026 is a comprehensive "Root and Branch" reform. It sheds the colonial baggage of the 1898 Act to construct a legal framework where the Post is a sovereign logistics guarantor, a financial inclusion engine, and a digital data steward. For the PTD Transformation Strategy, this Ordinance provides the statutory bedrock upon which the "Smart Logistics" and "Sovereign Network" pillars will be built.


2.6 Integration with the National Legal Reform Programme

The sector-specific legal instruments examined in Sections 2.2 through 2.5 operate within a broader national legal reform programme enacted in 2025. Three cross-cutting ordinances create enforceable obligations for every PTD entity:

2.6.1 Cyber Safety Ordinance 2025 (CSO)

The CSO, which repeals the Digital Security Act 2018 and the Cyber Security Act 2023, establishes the legal framework for cybersecurity governance, criminal offences, and institutional coordination. For PTD entities, the CSO’s relevance is threefold. First, it recognises citizens’ right to continuous internet access, reinforcing the Telecommunication Ordinance’s shutdown ban with an additional statutory basis. Second, it establishes the National Cyber Emergency Response Team (N-CERT) and the National Security Operations Centre (NSOC), both of which require BTRC and licensed operators to participate in threat intelligence sharing and mandatory Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) incident reporting. Telecommunications networks constitute critical information infrastructure by any definition; the CSO’s incident reporting and response requirements apply directly to all PTD-regulated operators. Third, the CSO introduces procedural guardrails for the filing of cybercrime cases—restricting the filing to police, victims, or legal representatives—that eliminate the previous regime’s practice of using cybersecurity provisions for politically motivated prosecution of online speech.

2.6.2 Personal Data Protection Ordinance 2025 (PDPO)

The PDPO establishes a comprehensive data protection regime applicable to all entities that process personal data, including every PTD entity. BTRC processes subscriber registration data for all telecommunications operators. BTCL and Teletalk maintain extensive customer databases. The Directorate of Posts handles correspondence and is building address and financial service databases. BSCL manages satellite service user data. TSS and BCSL hold employee and contractor data. The PDPO’s requirements—including consent-based processing, data minimisation, security measures, data breach notification, and Data Protection Impact Assessments for sensitive data processing—apply to all of these entities without exception. The appointment of Chief Data Officers by significant data fiduciaries, as required under Section 23, applies to the larger PTD entities. The 2026 Amendment to Section 29 further requires that any cloud-based processing of Critical Information Infrastructure or Restricted Personal Data must maintain at least one synchronised real-time copy within Bangladesh—a provision with direct implications for any PTD entity considering cloud-based service delivery.

2.6.3 National Data Governance Ordinance 2025 (NDGO)

The NDGO establishes the National Data Governance Authority (NDGA) and creates the institutional framework for data interoperability across the government. For PTD entities, compliance with the NDGO requires alignment of all data systems with the Bangladesh National Data Governance and Interoperability Architecture (BNDIA). This means that BTRC’s licensing databases, BTCL’s subscriber records, the Directorate of Posts’ address management and logistics tracking systems, and MOCSLA’s operator registry must all be designed or retrofitted to meet BNDIA interoperability standards. The NDGO’s data classification framework—which categorises government data into open, restricted, confidential, and classified tiers—applies to the data held by every PTD entity. Compliance is not optional; the NDGO imposes penalties for non-compliance and establishes dispute resolution mechanisms for inter-agency data sharing disagreements.

2.6.4 Cross-Legislative Framework Integration

The following table maps the key compliance obligations that the national legal reform programme creates for PTD entities:

PTD Entity

Cross-Legislative Compliance Obligations (CSO, PDPO, NDGO)

BTRC

CSO: CII incident reporting, threat intelligence sharing. PDPO: subscriber data protection, consent management, breach notification. NDGO: licensing database interoperability with BNDIA standards.

BTCL

CSO: CII compliance for network infrastructure. PDPO: customer data protection, Chief Data Officer appointment. NDGO: subscriber records aligned with BNDIA, data classification applied.

Teletalk

CSO: CII compliance, NSOC coordination. PDPO: subscriber and usage data protection. NDGO: data interoperability and classification compliance.

BSCCPLC

CSO: submarine cable infrastructure as CII, mandatory incident reporting. PDPO: operational and commercial data protection. NDGO: bandwidth and capacity data sharing per interoperability standards.

BSCL

CSO: satellite infrastructure security, CII compliance. PDPO: user data protection for Starlink resale operations. NDGO: data reporting to national infrastructure databases.

Directorate of Posts

CSO: digital postal platform security. PDPO: correspondence data, address data, and financial transaction data protection; Data Protection Impact Assessment for Postal Bank. NDGO: address management and logistics data interoperability.

MOCSLA

CSO: operator registry and compliance platform security. PDPO: licensee data handling. NDGO: operator registry aligned with BNDIA for cross-agency verification.

TSS / BCSL

PDPO: employee and contractor data protection. NDGO: production and operational data aligned with national standards.


2.7 International Benchmarks for Legal Reform

Bangladesh’s 2026 legal reforms do not operate in isolation. Comparable legislative modernisations across the telecommunications and postal sectors provide reference points for implementation design and outcome expectations.

Legal Reform

Key Features

Relevance to Bangladesh

India: Telecommunications Act 2023

Replaced the Indian Telegraph Act 1885 and Indian Wireless Telegraphy Act 1933 with a unified framework. Introduced technology-neutral licensing, spectrum auctions, right of way provisions, regulatory sandbox, and enhanced consumer protection. Established TRAI’s consultative mandate with mandatory public consultation on all major decisions.

Direct parallel to Bangladesh’s replacement of the ILDTS 2010 regime. India’s transition from legacy legislation to a unified framework provides implementation lessons: phased migration of existing licences, clear timelines, and preservation of existing operator rights during transition.

EU: European Electronic Communications Code 2018

Consolidated four directives into a single Code. Introduced technology-neutral authorisation, co-investment incentives for very high capacity networks, strengthened consumer rights, and harmonised spectrum management across 27 member states.

The Code’s approach to SMP remedies, infrastructure sharing incentives, and co-investment frameworks directly informs BTRC’s implementation of SMP provisions and infrastructure sharing mandates under the Licensing Policy 2025.

Singapore: Telecommunications Act (revised 2024)

IMDA operates as a converged regulator for telecom, media, and infocomm. Technology-neutral licensing with FBO/SBO two-tier framework. Strong consumer protection, mandatory QoS reporting, and integrated industry development mandate.

Singapore’s converged regulatory model is relevant to PTD’s oversight of both telecom and postal sectors. IMDA’s integration of regulation with industry development informs DoT’s proposed evolution.

Denmark: Postal Act Amendment 2023

Abolished the designation of a single universal service provider effective 1 January 2024. Levelled the regulatory playing field by applying identical rules to all postal operators. Reflects a market that can deliver services without a designated monopoly provider.

While Bangladesh’s context differs (requiring continued universal service designation for the Directorate of Posts), Denmark’s approach to competitive neutrality and its market-based assessment methodology provide a model for evaluating Bangladesh’s postal market as it matures.

US: Postal Service Reform Act 2022

Addressed USPS’s structural financial challenges by reforming retiree benefit obligations, requiring Medicare integration, mandating six-day delivery, and establishing real-time package tracking requirements. Enabled the “Delivering for America” ten-year modernisation plan.

The Act’s approach to coupling legislative reform with a strategic modernisation plan parallels this strategy’s integration of the Postal Services Ordinance with the Directorate of Posts’ digital transformation programme.


2.8 Strategic Impact of Legal Reforms

The legal reforms of 2026, taken collectively, produce five structural shifts that redefine the operating environment for PTD’s digital transformation programme:

From Regulatory Capture to Independent Oversight. The Telecommunication Ordinance’s restoration of BTRC’s independence, combined with the accountability committee and public hearing requirements, creates an institutional architecture in which regulatory decisions are made on the basis of evidence and market analysis rather than political direction. This shift is the single most important precondition for investor confidence, competitive market structures, and consumer welfare. The lesson from fifteen years of compromised regulation is that no amount of infrastructure investment produces optimal outcomes when the regulator serves the interests of the Ministry rather than the market.

From Fragmented Licensing to Simplified, Technology-Neutral Authorisation. The Licensing Policy’s reduction from over twenty licence categories to four core types eliminates the intermediary structures that extracted value without contributing proportionate infrastructure investment. The technology-neutral approach ensures that the licensing framework does not constrain innovation by prescribing specific technologies. The phased transition preserves existing operator rights while converging the market toward a simplified structure.

From Colonial Postal Law to Digital-Ready Framework. The replacement of the 1898 Act with a comprehensive Postal Services Ordinance that includes digital postage, logistics tracking, data protection compliance, and Postal Bank provisions creates the legal basis for transforming the Directorate of Posts from a letter carrier into a digital logistics and financial inclusion platform. Without this legislative foundation, operational initiatives—however well designed—lack the legal authority required for sustainable deployment.

From Unconstrained Surveillance to Rule-of-Law Governance. The combined effect of the Telecommunication Ordinance’s internet shutdown ban and anti-surveillance provisions, the CSO’s judicial oversight requirements for lawful interception, and the PDPO’s data protection obligations creates a legal framework in which telecommunications infrastructure serves citizens rather than surveilling them. This shift is essential for rebuilding the domestic trust and international credibility that documented abuses had eroded.

From Siloed Entities to Integrated Data Governance. The NDGO’s interoperability requirements, the PDPO’s data protection standards, and the sector-specific provisions of the Postal Services Ordinance collectively mandate that PTD entities design their digital systems for integration rather than isolation. The era of each entity maintaining incompatible databases with no data sharing protocols must give way to a framework in which postal address data, telecommunications subscriber data, licensing records, and service quality metrics are governed by consistent standards and accessible through interoperable platforms—subject to appropriate privacy and security controls.

2.9 Key Highlights Summary

Legal Instrument

Status

Key Provisions

Bangladesh Telecommunication (Amendment) Ordinance 2025

Approved by Advisory Council, 24 December 2025

Restores BTRC independence; bans internet shutdowns permanently; abolishes NTMC; establishes CIS; creates parliamentary accountability committee; mandates quarterly public hearings; criminalises surveillance data abuse

Telecommunications Network and Licensing Policy 2025

Gazetted 22 September 2025

Reduces 20+ licence categories to 4 core types (ANSP, NICSP, ICSP, NTNSP); introduces SMP remedies, mandatory infrastructure sharing, MVNO recognition, QoS Dashboard, fibre-to-tower targets (50%/18 months, 80%/3 years); repeals ILDTS Policy 2010

Postal Services Ordinance 2026

Draft stage (pending Advisory Council approval)

Repeals Post Office Act 1898; introduces digital postage, central logistics tracking, PDPO compliance mandate, enhanced penalties (up to BDT 10 lakh), universal service with accounting separation, Postal Bank provisions, Establishes MOCSLA as statutory body with budgetary autonomy; expands regulatory mandate to digital commerce logistics; strengthens enforcement powers; creates merit-based leadership selection.

Cyber Safety Ordinance 2025

Enacted 2025 (cross-cutting)

Repeals DSA 2018 and CSA 2023; recognises right to continuous internet access; establishes N-CERT and NSOC; mandates CII incident reporting; restricts case filing to police/victims

Personal Data Protection Ordinance 2025

Enacted 2025 (cross-cutting)

Consent-based processing; data minimisation; breach notification; Chief Data Officer requirement for significant fiduciaries; cross-border data transfer conditions; cloud copy requirement for CII data (2026 amendment)

National Data Governance Ordinance 2025

Enacted 2025 (cross-cutting)

Establishes NDGA; data classification framework; BNDIA interoperability standards; mandatory compliance for all government entities; dispute resolution mechanism



The legal landscape of 2025 provides the Posts and Telecommunications Division with a comprehensive legislative foundation that, for the first time, aligns the sector’s legal framework with the ambitions of the National Digital Transformation Strategy. The implementation chapters that follow—covering infrastructure, institutional reform, project reform, and governance—operate within and are enabled by this reformed legal architecture. The critical determinant of success is not the quality of the legislation, which is structurally sound, but the discipline, speed, and transparency of its implementation.



— End of Chapter 2 —


3. Telecom Infrastructure and Regulatory Capture

3.1 The Infrastructure Imperative

Bangladesh’s telecommunications sector has, by several measures, achieved remarkable scale. As of mid-2025, the country has approximately 188 million mobile phone subscriptions across four mobile network operators, 133.6 million internet subscribers, and 4G coverage reaching virtually 100 per cent of the population. The communications services market generates an estimated USD 4.9 billion in annual revenue. Mobile financial services process transactions exceeding BDT 13 lakh crore annually, making Bangladesh one of the most active mobile money markets in Asia. The sector directly employs over 30,000 people within PTD entities alone, with hundreds of thousands more across private operators, tower companies, ISPs, and the broader digital ecosystem.

Yet these aggregate figures conceal structural deficiencies that, left unaddressed, will constrain the sector’s capacity to serve as the foundation of the national digital transformation. Internet penetration stands at only 44.5 per cent—significantly below regional peers such as India (52 per cent), Vietnam (79 per cent), and Malaysia (97 per cent). Between June 2024 and January 2025, the number of mobile internet users fell by 13.2 million, driven by a tax burden exceeding 39 per cent on mobile services (comprising supplementary duty, VAT, and surcharges), rising SIM card costs, and macroeconomic pressures that forced consumers to prioritise basic necessities over data packages. Only approximately 30 per cent of mobile towers are connected via fibre optic cables, with the remainder relying on low-capacity microwave links that constrain data throughput and network resilience. The median mobile download speed, at 28.3 Mbps (January 2025, Ookla data), places Bangladesh behind India (94.5 Mbps), Vietnam (50.7 Mbps), and significantly behind regional leaders.

The White Paper’s analysis reveals that these deficiencies are not merely the product of underinvestment or market immaturity. They are, in substantial part, the consequence of regulatory capture—a condition in which the regulatory authority, designed to serve the public interest, instead serves the interests of the entities it is meant to regulate, or of the political executive that controls it. The erosion of BTRC’s independence through the 2010 amendments, the issuance of licences without baseline demand analysis, the tolerance of ownership structures that breached BTRC’s own guidelines, and the maintenance of artificially high submarine cable bandwidth pricing all reflect a regulatory environment in which the structures of oversight were subverted to benefit concentrated interests at the expense of competitive markets and consumer welfare.

This chapter examines the state of Bangladesh’s telecommunications infrastructure and the regulatory architecture that governs it. It documents the governance failures that produced the current conditions, analyses the reform measures initiated in 2025, identifies the gaps that remain, and establishes the implementation imperatives and performance indicators that will determine whether the sector achieves the connectivity outcomes that the National Digital Transformation Strategy requires.

3.2 The Regulatory Commission: BTRC

3.2.1 Institutional Architecture

The Bangladesh Telecommunication Regulatory Commission (BTRC) was established under the Bangladesh Telecommunication Act 2001 (Act No. XVIII of 2001) as an independent statutory body responsible for licensing, tariff regulation, spectrum management, quality of service enforcement, and market oversight. The Commission structure comprises a Chairman, a Vice-Chairman, and three Commissioners, supported by eight functional divisions: Engineering and Operations, Administration and Human Resources, Spectrum Management, Systems and Services, Legal and Licensing, Enforcement and Inspection, Finance Accounts and Revenue, and Public Relations. Five divisions are headed by Director Generals, with two Directors and one Deputy Director reporting directly to the Commission.

BTRC’s revenue generation is substantial. Its licensing and regulatory fees constitute a major source of non-tax revenue for the national exchequer, with revenue peaking around 2017–2018. The development budget for fiscal year 2023–24 allocated over BDT 4,773 crore to BTRC, reflecting its central role in the sector’s development financing. The Commission oversees a market comprising four mobile network operators (Grameenphone, Robi Axiata, Banglalink, and Teletalk), two NTTN licensees, 24 IGW operators, 22 ICX operators, 36 IIG operators, approximately 2,500 ISPs, and multiple tower companies, satellite operators, and specialised service providers.

3.2.2 The Erosion of Independence: 2010–2024

The Bangladesh Telecommunication (Amendment) Act 2010 fundamentally altered BTRC’s institutional character. As documented in the White Paper, the 2010 amendments transferred final authority over licence issuance, renewal, service approval, and tariff-setting from BTRC to the Ministry of Posts and Telecommunications. This transfer converted BTRC from an independent regulator into, effectively, an implementing agency of the Ministry. Key decisions on spectrum management, licensing, and tariff approval required ministerial endorsement, weakening BTRC’s operational discretion. Regulatory decisions became subject to ad hoc instructions, ministerial intervention, and political influence rather than rule-based governance.

The consequences were systemic. The ILDTS Policy 2010, which followed shortly after the amendments, was issued under the Ministry’s assumed licensing authority and introduced a licensing regime that the White Paper characterises as having “enabled cross-layer ownership and expanded licence issuance” without baseline demand analysis. This created “fertile ground for collusion, rent-seeking, and monopolistic behavior.” The over-fragmented market that resulted—ICX (22 licensees), IGW (24), IIG (36), approximately 2,500 ISPs—was misaligned to actual demand. Legal international voice minutes collapsed tenfold since 2015. Chronic under-utilisation and arrears demonstrated that the economic model for the majority of licensees was unsustainable.

Internationally, the relationship between regulatory independence and sector performance is well-established. The ITU’s Global ICT Regulatory Tracker consistently demonstrates that countries with “fourth generation” regulatory frameworks—characterised by independent, multi-stakeholder regulatory bodies—achieve higher connectivity outcomes per unit of investment than those with first- or second-generation frameworks characterised by ministerial control. The OECD’s Principles for the Governance of Regulators (2019) underscore the need for clear separation between policymaking and regulation to prevent political or commercial interference. The European Electronic Communications Code prohibits external directives that could compromise objective decision-making by National Regulatory Authorities. The FCC in the United States and Ofcom in the United Kingdom both operate under statutory protections for institutional autonomy. TRAI in India, while advisory in function, conducts mandatory public consultations on all major regulatory decisions, creating procedural accountability even where formal independence is limited.

Bangladesh’s experience between 2010 and 2024 provides a case study in what the absence of these protections produces: a regulatory environment characterised by unpredictability, investor uncertainty, delayed spectrum auctions, slow deployment of next-generation technologies, and a licensing regime that served concentrated interests rather than competitive market development.

3.2.3 Governance Failures: Recruitment and Appointments

The White Paper documents extensive governance failures within BTRC’s own institutional processes. Since 2002, BTRC has had eleven Chairmen in twenty-one years, with many replaced when political administrations changed. Commissioner positions were filled by executive decision without transparent search processes or external scrutiny. Director Generals were commonly drawn from government cadres or promoted internally with Ministry clearance, reinforcing BTRC’s dependence on the parent Ministry.

The recruitment and promotion irregularities identified between 2009 and 2022 included: the reclassification of contract and project staff as “departmental candidates” to bypass competitive examination requirements; selective age relaxations for preferred candidates; cross-stream promotions that violated branch-specific criteria in the BTRC Service Regulations; appointment of a Commissioner in 2017 whose siblings retained telecom business licences, creating a conflict of interest inconsistent with OECD, Ofcom, and US federal impartiality standards; and the appointment in 2022 of a Commissioner found to lack a formal service contract. These irregularities stemmed from institutional weakness, administrative negligence, nepotism-linked preferential practices, abuse of discretionary authority, and alleged political interference.

The White Paper’s root-cause assessment identifies seven contributory factors: institutional weakness and incapacity (absence of a centralised HR oversight cell); negligence and procedural lapses (failure to maintain applicant lists, examination scripts, and result archives); nepotism and preferential favouritism; abuse of discretionary powers (Commission-level age relaxations and qualification waivers without documented justification); political and external influence on appointments at Director General, Commissioner, and Chairman levels; corruption-prone governance gaps (lack of transparent competitive examinations and weak conflict-of-interest safeguards); and regulatory and policy gaps (ambiguous definitions enabling procedural arbitrage).

3.2.4 Reform Agenda

The Bangladesh Telecommunication (Amendment) Ordinance 2025, analysed in Chapter 2, restores BTRC’s regulatory independence as a matter of law. However, institutional reform requires more than legislative restoration. The White Paper recommends a comprehensive reform programme comprising:

Competitive, rules-based recruitment: All vacancies publicly advertised with clear eligibility criteria, transparent PSC-style examinations, published mark weightings, and mandatory document retention for at least ten years. This curbs non-competitive entry and ensures equal opportunity consistent with Article 29 of the Constitution.

Merit- and ACR-based promotions with time-in-grade rules: All promotions linked to documented Annual Confidential Report/KPI performance, minimum time-in-grade, and prescribed qualifications, embedded in a central Human Resource Information System (HRIS) that auto-enforces rules and blocks promotions of officers under investigation or lacking required tenure.

Branch-locked career ladders with bridging pathways: Technical roles within technical streams, administrative within administrative, financial within finance, and legal within legal, with cross-stream promotions permitted only after completion of accredited bridging programmes and minimum qualifying tenure in the target stream.

Independent search-cum-selection committee for senior appointments: An amendment to the Telecommunication Act 2001 establishing an independent committee for the selection of Chairman, Commissioners, and Director Generals, replacing the current executive-driven model that has produced politicised, non-transparent appointments.

Strengthened conflict-of-interest framework: A mandatory declaration and recusal regime aligned with OECD and Ofcom standards, ensuring that no Commissioner or senior officer has personal or family financial interests in regulated entities.



Reform KPIs: BTRC Institutional Governance

Indicator

Timeline

Responsible

HRIS deployed with automated rule enforcement

Within 12 months

BTRC

Search-cum-selection committee constituted for senior appointments

Within 6 months of Ordinance commencement

PTD / BTRC

All vacancies advertised with published eligibility criteria

Immediate (continuous)

BTRC

Conflict-of-interest declarations filed by all Commissioners and DGs

Within 3 months

BTRC

First independent external audit of HR processes completed

Within 18 months

BTRC / external auditor

Zero cross-stream promotions without accredited bridging qualification

Continuous from HRIS deployment

BTRC


3.3 Market Structure and Competition

3.3.1 Mobile Network Operators

Bangladesh’s mobile market is served by four operators. Grameenphone (a subsidiary of Norway’s Telenor Group) leads with approximately 86.5 million subscribers, representing nearly 45 per cent of the market. Robi Axiata follows with approximately 57.4 million subscribers (30 per cent), having consolidated its position through its 2016 merger with Airtel Bangladesh. Banglalink (part of the VEON group) holds approximately 37.9 million subscribers, while Teletalk, the sole state-owned mobile operator, serves approximately 6.6 million subscribers. Collectively, these operators generate the majority of the sector’s revenue and drive the consumer experience of telecommunications in Bangladesh.

Operator

Ownership

Subscribers (Mid-2025)

Market Share

Key Characteristics

Grameenphone

Telenor (55.8%), Grameen Telecom (34.2%)

~86.5 million

~45%

Market leader; SMP designated; highest QoS scores (video experience 57.6, download 23.3 Mbps per OpenSignal Feb 2025)

Robi Axiata

Axiata Group (61.82%), Bharti Airtel (28.18%)

~57.4 million

~30%

Merged with Airtel 2016; parent Axiata also controls edotco tower company

Banglalink

VEON Group

~37.9 million

~20%

Strong youth demographic; digital lifestyle focus

Teletalk

100% Government of Bangladesh

~6.6 million

~3.5%

Persistent losses (Tk 196.97 crore FY23); declining subscriber base; MVNO pilot with BTCL approved



The market exhibits significant concentration. Grameenphone has been designated as an operator with Significant Market Power (SMP), a designation that in principle triggers ex-ante remedies including wholesale access obligations and price controls. In practice, as the White Paper documents, SMP remedies have existed “largely on paper but are rarely enforced.” The Telecommunications Network and Licensing Policy 2025 empowers BTRC to establish a functioning SMP framework; the critical implementation task is translating this authority into operational enforcement—including periodic market reviews, published determinations, and enforceable remedies with transparent compliance monitoring.

The decline in mobile subscribers between mid-2024 and early 2025—approximately 6 million mobile subscriptions and 13.2 million mobile internet users lost—warrants analysis. The decline is attributable to multiple reinforcing factors: a tax burden exceeding 39 per cent on mobile services following the 2024–25 budget’s increase of supplementary duty from 15 to 20 per cent; rising SIM card prices (from subsidised levels to BDT 100–300); macroeconomic pressures that reduced discretionary spending on data packages; and the deactivation of dormant multi-SIM subscriptions. While broadband internet subscriptions increased slightly to approximately 14 million during the same period (suggesting substitution toward Wi-Fi-based access), the net decline represents a significant setback to the digitisation trajectory and underscores the policy imperative of reducing the tax burden on mobile services to levels consistent with regional norms.

3.3.2 Tower Infrastructure Concentration

The tower infrastructure market in Bangladesh is dominated by two companies: edotco Bangladesh Company Limited (EBCL) and Summit Tower Limited, which together control approximately 90 per cent of the passive tower infrastructure. This duopoly raises significant competition and sovereignty concerns.

edotco Bangladesh Company Limited (EBCL): Established in 2013 as a subsidiary of edotco Group Sdn Bhd (itself a subsidiary of Malaysia’s Axiata Group Berhad), EBCL’s formation involved the extraction of Robi Axiata’s tower assets into a separate entity. The White Paper documents a troubling pattern of ownership changes. At formation, Robi Axiata held 51 per cent and edotco Group 49 per cent. In 2015, Robi sold 5,258 towers to EBCL for USD 250 million. In November 2016, BTRC approved edotco’s increase of its stake to 80 per cent—directly contravening BTRC’s own guideline limiting foreign partner shareholding to 70 per cent. In September 2018, Robi transferred its remaining 20 per cent to edotco Group. BRAC subsequently acquired 26 per cent and Greencon Tower/GETCO 4 per cent, bringing the current structure to edotco Group (70 per cent), BRAC (26 per cent), and Greencon/GETCO (4 per cent). The practical effect was that the Axiata Group re-rated tower assets to higher valuation multiples while depleting Robi’s asset base, with Malaysia’s sovereign wealth fund Khazanah Nasional Berhad holding significant stakes in both Axiata Group (36.7 per cent) and edotco Sdn Bhd (approximately 32 per cent).

The regulatory questions raised by the White Paper remain pertinent: how did BTRC approve an 80 per cent foreign stake that exceeded its own 70 per cent guideline? Why was the ILDTS Policy 2010’s prohibition on foreign ownership of new telecom entities not applied to the tower asset transfer? These questions illustrate the regulatory capture dynamic: ownership restrictions were enforced inconsistently, with the effect of enabling one corporate group to consolidate control over critical national infrastructure.

Reform Imperatives for Tower Infrastructure: The Licensing Policy 2025’s mandatory infrastructure sharing provisions, if enforced, create the framework to address tower market concentration. BTRC must establish open-access pricing guidelines for shared tower infrastructure, conduct transparent periodic SMP reviews of the tower market, enforce non-discrimination obligations on dominant tower providers, and ensure that the infrastructure sharing guidelines—which must cover both passive elements (towers, poles, ducts) and active elements (radio access networks)—create a genuinely competitive wholesale tower market. India’s tower sharing experience, where Indus Towers (a joint venture of Airtel and Vodafone Idea) and American Tower Corporation operate under a framework regulated by TRAI’s infrastructure provision (IP-I) guidelines, provides a comparative reference. Indonesia’s tower regulation, which mandates co-location and sharing with regulated pricing, offers another model for markets with concentrated tower ownership.

3.3.3 NTTN Duopoly and Vertical Integration

The Nationwide Telecommunications Transmission Network (NTTN) market in Bangladesh is served by two operators: Fiber@Home and Summit Communications. This duopoly controls the fibre backbone infrastructure that interconnects access networks, international gateways, and end users. The concentration of national transmission capacity in two entities creates the conditions for market power that the White Paper identifies as inadequately addressed: “The duopoly in NTTN provision by Fiber@Home and Summit… has not been counterbalanced by robust open-access price controls, audited non-discrimination, or transparent periodic SMP reviews.”

Compounding the duopoly concern is vertical integration across network layers. Cross-ownership arrangements combining NTTN, ITC (International Terrestrial Cable), and IIG (International Internet Gateway) licences have enabled integrated groups to influence upstream and downstream pricing and access. The White Paper observes that regulatory enforcement of accounting separation has been weak, allowing conflicts of interest to persist. Stand-alone operators—particularly smaller ISPs—pay more for the same wholesale services, creating structural disadvantage that undermines competitive entry and market diversity.

The Licensing Policy 2025’s rationalisation of licence categories addresses this concern by design: the National Infrastructure and Connectivity Service Provider (NICSP) licence integrates transmission, interconnection, and internet exchange functions under a single category with mandatory non-discriminatory wholesale access obligations. However, the transition from the current duopoly to a genuinely competitive NICSP market requires BTRC to implement SMP determinations, establish transparent wholesale pricing methodologies, mandate accounting separation with independent audits, and create monitoring mechanisms that detect discriminatory conduct. The European Commission’s regulatory treatment of national network operators under the European Electronic Communications Code—including the Significant Market Power framework, access and interconnection obligations, and cost-oriented pricing—provides a well-developed institutional model.

3.3.4 Licensing Over-Fragmentation and the ILDTS Legacy

The licensing regime established under the ILDTS Policies of 2007 and 2010 created over twenty distinct licence categories, producing a fragmented market structure that the White Paper characterises as the product of regulatory failure rather than market-driven evolution.

The ILDTS Policy 2007 introduced segmented licensing for International Gateways (IGW), Interconnection Exchanges (ICX), International Internet Gateways (IIG), and Internet Protocol Telephony Service Providers (IPTSP), with fixed licence caps established without any documented baseline demand analysis. The policy arbitrarily restricted the number of licensees: three IGWs (in addition to BTTB), and six ICXs (in addition to BTTB). The White Paper concludes that “allocating only 3 IGW licenses in 2008 was a flawed decision.”

The ILDTS Policy 2010 compounded the 2007 regime’s deficiencies by removing licence caps entirely without implementing competitive safeguards. The resulting flood of sub-scale operators stretched BTRC’s oversight capacity, diluted investment, and produced a market of “survival-mode firms, price wars, leakage, and weak QoS—without matching consumer benefit.” The multi-layered infrastructure stack—the rigid ITC→IIG/IGW→ICX→ANS chain—added cost and latency at each hop, forced additional audits and approvals, and imposed disproportionate costs on smaller ISPs.



Legacy Licence Categories and Market Oversupply

Category

Active Licensees

Key Issue

IGW

24

Severe oversupply; legal international voice minutes collapsed 10x since 2015; chronic under-utilisation; formation of IGW Operators Forum (IOF) enabling coordinated behaviour

ICX

22

Oversupply relative to declining voice interconnection demand; quality and monitoring challenges

IIG

36

Multiple IIG licences concentrated in vertically integrated groups; limited independent competition

ISP

~2,500

Vast majority sub-scale; many dormant or operating with expired licences; disproportionate compliance burden from multi-desk NOC processes

NTTN

2

Duopoly (Fiber@Home and Summit) controlling national fibre backbone; insufficient SMP enforcement

Tower Company

Multiple (duopoly)

edotco + Summit control ~90% of market; open-access pricing not enforced



The Licensing Policy 2025 addresses this legacy by consolidating the entire licence landscape into four categories (ANSP, NICSP, ICSP, NTNSP), with existing licences permitted to run to natural expiry—most lapsing by 2027. The transition period must be managed carefully: BTRC must publish a clear migration roadmap specifying timelines, fee structures, and transitional conditions for each legacy category; ensure that operators understand their obligations under the new framework; and provide a transparent appeal mechanism for disputes arising during transition.


3.4 International Connectivity: Submarine Cables and Bandwidth Pricing

3.4.1 BSCPLC and the Bandwidth Pricing Challenge

Bangladesh Submarine Cable Company PLC (BSCPLC)—the state-owned operator responsible for international submarine cable connectivity—has, in financial terms, been among the most consistently profitable PTD entities since its inception. Its 2012 IPO was successful, and its stock has performed steadily in the market. Yet this financial performance, as the White Paper documents in detail, came at a significant cost to the national interest: the price of submarine cable bandwidth was maintained at artificially high levels, keeping a large portion of available capacity unsold while Bangladesh’s internet users paid more than they should have for international connectivity.

The data is stark. In FY 2023–24, BSCPLC’s lit-up capacity stood at 6,850 Gbps, but actual utilisation was only 2,696 Gbps—39 per cent of available capacity. This consistent and remarkably large gap between capacity and utilisation is, as the White Paper notes, “a clear outcome of highly priced BSCPLC bandwidth” that has “had a negative impact on the overall expansion of the Internet in Bangladesh.” International bandwidth has zero shelf-life, and submarine cables have limited operational lifetimes of approximately twenty-five years; holding up unused bandwidth represents a direct loss of national economic value. While International Terrestrial Cable (ITC) operators may have benefited from BSCPLC’s pricing, the average internet user did not.

By 2023, the total downstream bandwidth brought in by ITC operators equaled BSCPLC’s bandwidth, and has since surpassed it. BSCPLC’s market share in international bandwidth is declining as operators seek lower-cost terrestrial alternatives. This trend will accelerate with the entry of the Bangladesh Private Cable Consortium (BPCS) and the commissioning of SEA-ME-WE 6.

Internationally, state-owned submarine cable operators face similar challenges. Brazil’s Telebras and India’s BSNL have both faced the tension between maintaining profitability as state enterprises and pricing bandwidth at levels that maximise national connectivity. The resolution, invariably, involves pricing reform that reduces wholesale bandwidth costs to competitive levels, coupled with commercial diversification into value-added services (managed wavelength services, data centre interconnection, co-location at cable landing stations) that generate margins from services rather than artificial scarcity. BSCPLC must undertake a comparable strategic pivot: price reductions to maximise utilisation, combined with new service offerings that generate sustainable revenue.

3.4.2 SEA-ME-WE 6, BPCS, and Redundancy

Bangladesh is investing in SEA-ME-WE 6 (SMW-6), a high-capacity international submarine cable expected to become operational in 2026. SMW-6 will significantly increase Bangladesh’s international bandwidth capacity and provide redundancy for the existing SEA-ME-WE 4 and SEA-ME-WE 5 connections. The cable’s design capacity of approximately 100 Tbps per fibre pair places it among the highest-capacity submarine cable systems globally.

The Bangladesh Private Cable Consortium (BPCS), licensed to invest in the SIGMAR cable (a 1,400 km stretch of UMO), introduces private sector participation in international submarine connectivity for the first time. BPCS offers dark fibre Indefeasible Right of Use (IRU) ownership, enabling operators to light their own capacity and bypass BSCPLC’s wholesale bandwidth model. This structural change may further divert traffic and revenue from BSCPLC unless it substantially revises its pricing and service models.

The White Paper raises legitimate concerns about BPCS’s licensing structure: three separate private bidders formed a consortium after the licensing process, raising questions about whether BTRC should have encouraged consortium formation earlier to direct investment into a redundancy cable rather than a parallel capacity addition. The timing of BPCS licensing during SMW-6’s rollout risks diluting national investment in BSCPLC and creating market fragmentation rather than strategically expanding Bangladesh’s cable landing diversity—a critical resilience concern given that Bangladesh currently has only two cable landing points (both in Cox’s Bazar), creating a single geographic point of failure.

Reform Imperatives: Bangladesh’s international connectivity strategy must address four priorities: pricing reform (BSCPLC bandwidth pricing reduced to competitive levels to maximise utilisation); redundancy (diversification of cable landing points beyond Cox’s Bazar, potentially through a Chittagong or Mongla alternative landing station); competitive framework (BTRC enforcement of fair-play rules ensuring BPCS entry stimulates competition rather than fragmentation); and strategic coordination (alignment of SMW-6 commissioning, BPCS operations, and Starlink satellite connectivity into a coherent national bandwidth strategy that optimises cost, capacity, and resilience).

3.4.3 Satellite Internet: The Starlink Entry

The arrival of Starlink in Bangladesh added a new dimension to the connectivity landscape in 2025. SpaceX’s LEO satellite constellation, offering broadband internet with low latency, received regulatory approval to operate in Bangladesh, with Bangladesh Satellite Company Limited (BSCL) designated as the national resale partner. The entry of satellite internet—formalised through the Non-Terrestrial Networks and Service Provider (NTNSP) licence category in the Licensing Policy 2025—is the first time Bangladesh’s licensing framework has provided explicit provisions for satellite broadband.

Starlink’s potential impact is significant in three dimensions: geographic coverage (LEO satellites can provide broadband connectivity to the approximately 55 per cent of Bangladesh’s population in rural areas, the Chittagong Hill Tracts, and coastal regions where terrestrial infrastructure is limited or absent); disaster resilience (satellite connectivity is independent of terrestrial infrastructure and can maintain communications during cyclones, floods, and other natural disasters that regularly affect Bangladesh); and competitive pressure (satellite broadband creates an alternative pathway to international connectivity that bypasses the entire terrestrial value chain, potentially disciplining pricing by ITC operators, BSCPLC, and the NTTN providers).

However, challenges remain: Starlink’s consumer pricing must be affordable for the Bangladeshi market (current US pricing of approximately USD 120 per month for equipment lease plus USD 120 per month for service is well beyond the reach of most Bangladeshi consumers); regulatory conditions must ensure that Starlink’s operations comply with PDPO data protection requirements and the CSO’s CII provisions; and BSCL’s operational capacity to serve as an effective resale partner must be strengthened.


3.5 Spectrum Management and 5G Readiness

3.5.1 Current Holdings and Allocation History

Spectrum management in Bangladesh has been characterised by the White Paper as “price-heavy and flexibility-light.” Auctions have carried rigid rules and inflated reserve prices, while the absence of secondary spectrum trading, dynamic spectrum management, spectrum sharing, refarming, and technology-neutral rights has constrained efficient utilisation and delayed the rollout of next-generation services.

The 2022 spectrum auction allocated 220 MHz across the 2.3 GHz and 2.6 GHz bands: Grameenphone and Robi each secured 60 MHz from the 2.6 GHz band for approximately BDT 3,360 crore each; Banglalink acquired 40 MHz from the 2.3 GHz band for BDT 2,241 crore; and Teletalk obtained 30 MHz for BDT 1,680 crore. Notably, 30 MHz from the 2.3 GHz band remained unsold in a second phase, with neither Teletalk nor Banglalink showing interest—an indication that reserve pricing exceeded operators’ willingness to pay at the margin. In August 2024, BTRC allocated an additional 20 MHz in the 2.6 GHz band to Grameenphone and Robi (10 MHz each) at a negotiated price of BDT 580 million per MHz, with payment terms structured over ten annual instalments.

3.5.2 The 700 MHz Band and Rural Broadband

BTRC planned to auction the 700 MHz band in 2025, a frequency band that is critical for mobile broadband expansion in rural areas due to its superior propagation characteristics (wider coverage and better building penetration compared to higher frequencies). However, a legal dispute with an ISP over a portion of this band has delayed the auction. The proposed base price of BDT 263 crore per MHz was identified by the White Paper as being far above international benchmarks. At this price level, spectrum costs would reach 14–16 per cent of operator revenue (compared to the current 9–12 per cent range), discouraging investment and potentially leaving the band unsold.

Internationally, spectrum pricing reform has been a critical enabler of rural broadband expansion. India’s approach to the 700 MHz band is instructive: after initial auctions in 2016 saw the band go unsold at reserve prices that exceeded operators’ valuations, TRAI recommended substantial reductions. The 2024 Indian auction attracted bids at significantly lower reserve prices, enabling operators to deploy rural broadband infrastructure. The United Kingdom’s Ofcom set 700 MHz reserve prices at levels designed to ensure allocation rather than maximise auction revenue, recognising that the economic value of spectrum is generated through its deployment, not its auction price. Bangladesh must resolve the legal dispute and reset the 700 MHz reserve price to levels that ensure allocation and deployment, particularly in the approximately 2,600 unions identified by the National Digital Transformation Strategy as requiring enhanced connectivity.

3.5.3 5G Readiness and Roadmap

Bangladesh has not yet commercially deployed 5G services, placing it behind regional peers including India (launched June 2022), Vietnam (commercial trials ongoing since 2024), Thailand (launched March 2020), and the Philippines (launched March 2021). The prerequisites for 5G deployment include: adequate spectrum allocation in mid-band (3.3–3.8 GHz) and high-band (millimetre wave) frequencies; fibre backhaul to at least 80 per cent of tower sites (current estimate: approximately 30 per cent); a regulatory framework for small cell deployment (including rights of way, permitting, and municipal coordination); and an investment environment that provides operators with confidence in the return on 5G capital expenditure.

The Licensing Policy 2025’s provisions for technology-neutral licensing, infrastructure sharing, and NTNSP licences create the regulatory preconditions. The fibre-to-tower targets (50 per cent within 18 months, 80 per cent within three years) directly address the backhaul constraint. However, 5G spectrum allocation—particularly in the 3.5 GHz band that is the global anchor for 5G mid-band deployment—must be scheduled, priced competitively, and allocated through a transparent process. BTRC must develop and publish a 5G spectrum roadmap within twelve months, identifying candidate bands, indicative timelines, and the technical conditions (including electromagnetic compatibility assessments and satellite coordination) required for allocation.

The RAN (Radio Access Network) and spectrum sharing rules that the White Paper identifies as “years late”—MORAN (Multi-Operator Radio Access Network) and MOCN (Multi-Operator Core Network) frameworks—must be finalised and published by BTRC. Active infrastructure sharing reduces duplicative capital expenditure, enables more efficient spectrum utilisation, and accelerates rural coverage. Without these rules, operators continue to invest in parallel infrastructure that increases costs and slows deployment—a pattern that is particularly damaging for Teletalk, whose limited capital base makes shared infrastructure essential to its viability.



Reform KPIs: Spectrum Management and 5G Readiness
