BTRC



3.6 Consumer Protection and Quality of Service

3.6.1 The QoS Gap

Consumer experience of telecommunications in Bangladesh falls substantially short of both the standards that regulation prescribes and the outcomes that peer countries achieve. Bangladesh ranks 93rd among 154 countries in Ookla’s global index of mobile internet quality, behind Vietnam (13th), India (28th), Indonesia (82nd), and Sri Lanka (85th). The median mobile download speed of 28.3 Mbps, while improved from prior years, is less than a third of India’s 94.5 Mbps and roughly half of Vietnam’s 50.7 Mbps. Fixed broadband performance, at a median of 48.9 Mbps, similarly trails regional comparators.

BTRC has established QoS regulations and conducts periodic drive tests, but the White Paper identifies critical weaknesses in the enforcement framework. Drive tests in 2024 revealed non-compliance with standards: excessive call drops, poor 4G coverage in areas nominally covered, and high latency. Despite these findings, penalties have been rare. Methodologies lack transparency and standardisation; disputes between operators and BTRC regarding test procedures remain unresolved; and public QoS reports are irregularly published and lack detail on test criteria and compliance levels. The new QoS benchmarks proposed in early 2025—requiring ISPs to deliver at least 75 per cent of subscribed download speed and 50 per cent of upload speed—represent an improvement, but their effectiveness depends entirely on the enforcement machinery.

Certain regulatory decisions have, counterintuitively, degraded the consumer experience. In 2021, BTRC directed the removal of cache servers from small and medium-sized ISPs, which increased operational costs and reduced service quality. Although this restriction was lifted for most ISPs in December 2024, the intervening three years imposed significant financial and technical strain on smaller operators and their subscribers. Similarly, restrictions on ISPs sharing physical network resources (equipment and fibre-optic infrastructure) have contributed to higher transmission costs and consumer prices, with the impact most pronounced in rural and semi-urban areas. A recent study found that rural users pay higher rates than urban users while receiving comparatively lower internet speeds—a regressive outcome that deepens the digital divide.

3.6.2 Consumer Rights and Complaint Resolution

There is currently no dedicated consumer protection unit within BTRC and no centralised complaint portal. The existing complaint mechanisms are fragmented: BTRC maintains a call centre (100), individual operators have customer service desks, but escalation paths are unclear and independent oversight is absent. Public hearings reveal that fewer than 2 per cent of complaints receive formal responses, reflecting BTRC’s limited grievance-handling capacity.

The absence of a unified national consumer protection code for telecommunications is a significant institutional gap. Global peers provide instructive models: India’s TRAI has established an Ombudsman for telecom complaints with binding dispute resolution powers; the United Kingdom’s Ofcom operates the Communications and Internet Services Adjudication Scheme (CISAS) and Ombudsman Services: Communications as independent Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) bodies; and the US FCC’s Consumer Complaint Center provides an online portal for filing, tracking, and resolving complaints with mandatory operator response timelines. These models share common features: an online portal for complaint submission and tracking, mandatory response timelines for operators, escalation to an independent adjudication body, and periodic publication of complaint resolution data.

Reform Imperatives: The Licensing Policy 2025’s QoS Dashboard requirement provides the foundation for transparent quality monitoring. BTRC must implement: a national digital complaint management system (a single online and mobile platform for submitting, tracking, and escalating grievances with monthly public reporting); a modernised customer code requiring plain-language tariff communication, explicit consent for value-added services (VAS), standardised billing formats, and transparent fair-usage policies; district-level independent performance scorecards combining drive-test and crowdsourced data on speed, latency, and complaint resolution; and an independent telecom ombudsman or ADR mechanism for disputes that operators’ internal processes cannot resolve within prescribed timelines.

3.6.3 Affordability and Taxation

The total tax burden on mobile services in Bangladesh exceeds 39 per cent—comprising supplementary duty (increased from 15 to 20 per cent in the 2024–25 budget), VAT, and surcharges. This is among the highest effective tax rates on telecommunications in Asia and substantially above the levels that international evidence suggests are consistent with maximising digital inclusion. The GSMA’s annual Mobile Tax Gap analysis estimates that sector-specific taxes above approximately 25–30 per cent of the total cost of mobile ownership begin to reduce adoption among lower-income consumers, with each additional percentage point of taxation producing a disproportionate reduction in mobile internet penetration among the bottom two income quintiles.

The impact is evident in Bangladesh’s subscriber data: the 13.2 million decline in mobile internet users between June 2024 and January 2025 coincides with the supplementary duty increase and the consequent rise in retail prices. The decline is concentrated among pre-paid users, who represent the most price-sensitive segment of the market. Internationally, countries that have reduced telecommunications taxation to stimulate adoption—including India (which reduced its GST rate on mobile services from 18 to 12 per cent in certain categories) and Kenya (which reversed a proposed excise duty increase after evidence of subscriber decline)—have observed demonstrable recovery in subscriber growth and data consumption.

The strategy must advocate for: a phased reduction of the supplementary duty on mobile services toward 15 per cent; alignment of total effective taxation on telecommunications with the GSMA’s recommended ceiling; and demand-side affordability interventions including data and device vouchers for low-income populations, zero-interest handset financing programmes, and targeted subsidies for educational and healthcare connectivity—reforms that fall within the broader fiscal policy domain but must be articulated as essential preconditions for the digital transformation agenda.



Reform KPIs: Consumer Protection and Quality of Service

Within 12 months

Within 12 months

Within 18 months

Within 6 months

BTRC


3.7 Social Obligation Fund: Governance Failures and Reform

3.7.1 Background and Scale

The Social Obligation Fund (SOF) was established in November 2011 under the Telecommunication Act (Amendment) 2010, mandating telecom operators to contribute 1 per cent of their audited gross annual revenue to finance connectivity in underserved areas. The fund is intended to universalise internet access by supporting broadband infrastructure in remote coastal zones, river island (char) areas, hilly regions, and areas affected by river erosion—locations where low commercial returns inhibit private investment.

Over fourteen years, the SOF accumulated BDT 3,558.98 crore in total contributions and interest. Seven SOF-funded projects were approved between 2018 and 2021, implemented by five different agencies, with a combined estimated cost exceeding BDT 2,000 crore. These projects encompassed school digitisation, rural broadband rollout, mobile internet deployment in haor and island areas, and infrastructure installation in underserved regions.

3.7.2 Governance Failures

The White Paper’s analysis of the SOF identifies three categories of systemic failure that have rendered the fund substantially less effective than its potential:

Non-compliance and revenue shortfalls: The statutory 1 per cent levy was unevenly enforced. While major mobile operators contributed with reasonable consistency, the majority of ISPs, divisional and district-level service providers, and value-added service licensees either remitted irregularly or not at all. Only recently, in 2023, were approximately 3,000 additional licensees (ISPs, IIGs, VAS providers) considered for inclusion—raising questions about the reasons for their prior exclusion. Discretionary waivers further eroded the contribution base: for certain internet services, BTRC selectively waived the 1 per cent SOF alongside a 5.5 per cent revenue-sharing requirement.

Idle funds and delayed deployment: Despite accumulating substantial sums, approximately 35 per cent of total funds raised (approximately BDT 1,500 crore) remained idle due to regulatory and institutional bottlenecks. From its establishment in 2011, the SOF saw no project activity until early 2018—seven years of institutional inertia during which collected public monies were not deployed for their intended purpose. The prolonged non-deployment represents a significant opportunity cost: backbone and last-mile investments that could have been financed when costs were lower were foregone, and subsequent spending was hurriedly approved under political pressure, creating conditions for poor procurement and weak project design.

Governance failures in project implementation: The White Paper documents fund mismanagement, multiple cases of Public Procurement Rules (PPR) violation in contract and tender awards, instances of political interference in project site selection and installation placement, lack of transparency in project selection, and deviations from SOF guideline principles in terms of impact and sustainability plans. SOF projects were excluded from the Annual Development Programme (ADP), bypassing standard national planning scrutiny. The SOF Management Committee’s governance structure—with the Minister serving simultaneously as Committee Chairperson and the power to withdraw funds on the Minister’s signature alone—created vulnerability to political direction that the White Paper characterises as a “key reason for their vulnerability in terms of conception, implementation and impact framework.”

Sustainability risks were pervasive. The “Connected Bangladesh” project was to be handed to entities that either lacked necessary licences or had unclear operational budgets. BTCL hotspot installations lacked ring-fenced operation and maintenance funding. VSAT terminals, solar systems, and classroom tablets rarely budgeted for spare parts, batteries, or refresh cycles beyond limited warranties. Backbone deployments without guaranteed last-mile service provision risked underutilisation. The White Paper notes that some SOF project approvals appeared “designed to generate demand for TSS or other politically connected vendors rather than being grounded in competitive need assessment.”

3.7.3 International Comparators

SOF-related governance challenges are not unique to Bangladesh. India’s Universal Service Obligation Fund (USOF) has faced allegations of misallocation, incomplete work, and manufactured cost inflation; some projects were abandoned post-funding. Malaysia’s Universal Service Provision (USP) has been criticised for misaligned priorities and procurement bias. However, both countries have developed stronger institutional frameworks: India’s USOF operates under the Department of Telecommunications with published project evaluation criteria, competitive bidding, and independent completion audits; Malaysia restructured its USP through the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) with transparent annual reporting and outcome-based disbursement. Bangladesh’s SOF lacks these institutional safeguards.

3.7.4 Reform Imperatives

The SOF must be restructured to operate on a results-based financing model. Disbursements should be awarded only for verified delivery of measurable outcomes—broadband speeds, latency, active user adoption—confirmed by independent audits. Specific reforms include:

Institutional restructuring: Separate the SOF governance body from the ministerial chain by establishing an independent SOF Board with representation from BTRC, PTD, civil society, industry, and the Planning Commission. Remove the single-signatory withdrawal authority.

Harmonisation with national planning: Integrate SOF project appraisal with the ADP process, requiring Planning Commission review for projects above a defined threshold (recommended: BDT 50 crore).

Competitive and transparent procurement: Mandate open competitive bidding for all SOF projects with published evaluation criteria, and include private operators as eligible implementing entities.

Outcome-based disbursement: Tie funding tranches to independently verified milestones (connectivity delivered, active users registered, speed benchmarks met). Withhold final payment until post-completion audit.

Sustainability requirements: Require every SOF project proposal to include a credible exit plan with identified successor operator, ring-fenced O&M funding for at least five years, and total cost of ownership (TCO) budgeting that includes equipment refresh cycles.

Impact evaluation: Establish a centre of excellence for impact evaluation (as originally proposed in the inaugural SOF meeting of March 2018 but never followed through), independent of BTRC, to conduct third-party assessments of SOF project outcomes.

Reduced NTTN dependency: Avoid dependence on private NTTN entities for future SOF project awards. Where upstream connectivity flows through private NTTN licensees, PTD should finance expansion of government-owned optical fibre as an alternative, or BTRC should revisit NTTN licence conditions to empower government NTTN operators.



Reform KPIs: Social Obligation Fund

Within 6 months

Within 12 months

Target: 90% by 2027

Within 12 months

3.8 State-Owned Telecom Entities: Institutional Challenges and Strategic Direction

3.8.1 BTCL: From Legacy Operator to National Infrastructure Provider

Bangladesh Telecommunications Company Limited (BTCL) was created through the 2008 corporatisation of the legacy fixed-line operations of the Bangladesh Telegraph and Telephone Board (BTTB). BTCL inherited an extensive but ageing copper network, a bureaucratic management culture, high maintenance costs, and a workforce structure designed for a monopoly era. Its revenue contribution to PTD stands at over BDT 300 crore annually, but this figure is misleading: in FY 2023–24, BTCL reported a small profit driven primarily by non-operating income (fixed deposit interest) rather than core telephony and data operations, which continue to decline. The fixed-line subscriber base has contracted sharply as mobile and broadband services have substituted for traditional telephony.

The White Paper identifies BTCL’s challenges as structural and financial. Without regulatory incentives or modernisation policies following the 2008 separation, BTCL struggled to transition toward fibre-based broadband and digital services. Policy gaps in performance monitoring and public-sector reform meant that inefficiencies persisted, reducing BTCL’s ability to compete with private ISPs and mobile operators. The 2008 separation resulted in institutional fragmentation rather than functional specialisation—the lack of a unified governance model and performance-linked regulatory oversight undermined the goal of creating a dynamic, competitive public telecom ecosystem.

Strategic Repositioning: The White Paper identifies a transformative opportunity for BTCL to redefine its role from a legacy fixed-line utility into a strategic national infrastructure provider. With one of the largest and most valuable infrastructure portfolios in the country—including extensive duct networks, exchange buildings, and right-of-way assets—BTCL is uniquely positioned to lead the national infrastructure-sharing framework mandated by the Licensing Policy 2025. By offering wholesale backhaul and interconnect capacity to both public and private operators, it can become the backbone of Bangladesh’s digital ecosystem. Key strategic directions include: leading fibre-to-tower deployment (BTCL’s duct network provides the most cost-effective pathway to meet the 80 per cent fibre-to-tower target within three years); becoming the primary wholesale broadband provider to ISPs in underserved areas; and underpinning smart grid, smart city, and digital public goods infrastructure.

An MVNO initiative with Teletalk has been approved, enabling BTCL to offer mobile services using Teletalk’s network capacity. This represents an important step toward diversifying BTCL’s revenue base beyond declining fixed-line operations. However, the initiative’s success depends on BTCL developing commercial capabilities (marketing, customer acquisition, digital service delivery) that its current institutional culture has not prioritised.

3.8.2 Teletalk: The State-Owned Mobile Operator

Teletalk Bangladesh Limited, the only state-owned mobile network operator, has struggled with persistent financial losses throughout most of its operational history. Total losses reached BDT 196.97 crore in FY 2022–23, driven by a declining subscriber base (approximately 6.6 million, representing just 3.5 per cent of the market), deteriorating service quality, and substantial debts owed to regulatory authorities. The White Paper notes that “Teletalk’s losses totaled Tk 196.97 crore in FY23, driven by a declining subscriber base, revenue, and deteriorating service quality, along with substantial debts owed to regulatory authorities. Its revival prospects remain uncertain due to weak financial controls and operational inefficiencies.”

Recent operational initiatives include: negotiating tower sharing agreements with tower companies for 2,300 sites (with agreements for 1,720 sites completed by late 2025 and 15 new sites activated from shared towers); expanding 4G coverage by adding 1,699 new 4G BTS sites (increasing daily data consumption from 354 TB to 464 TB, a 20 per cent increase); upgrading battery capacity at 1,420 sites to improve network availability; and planning further 4G expansion to 3,600 additional sites by June 2027, targeting 85 per cent 4G coverage of existing Teletalk sites.

Strategic Assessment: Teletalk’s viability as an independent mobile network operator must be honestly assessed. With 3.5 per cent market share in a four-operator market, persistent losses, and a capital base insufficient to fund 5G deployment, Teletalk cannot sustain competitive operations on its current trajectory. The strategy must consider three options: first, restructuring Teletalk as a government network services provider (maintaining its spectrum assets and network for government communications, emergency services, and rural connectivity while partnering with private operators through MVNO and infrastructure-sharing arrangements); second, merging Teletalk’s operations with BTCL to create a single state-owned telecommunications entity with sufficient scale to compete in wholesale infrastructure and government services; third, strategic partnership with a private operator (either through a joint venture or management contract) that brings commercial capabilities while preserving government’s strategic interests. Each option involves trade-offs between financial sustainability, employment, sovereign control over spectrum, and the political economy of public-sector reform. What is not sustainable is the status quo of continuous losses funded by public resources.

3.8.3 BSCPLC: The Submarine Cable Company

Bangladesh Submarine Cable Company PLC (BSCPLC) has been discussed in Section 3.4 in the context of international connectivity. From a state-owned entity management perspective, BSCPLC presents a paradox: it is the most consistently profitable PTD entity, yet its profitability has been achieved through pricing practices that the White Paper documents as having constrained national internet adoption. Revenue from BSCPLC stands at approximately BDT 60 crore annually, and it is one of the few PTD entities listed on the Dhaka Stock Exchange (with general public investors holding 7.75 per cent).

With BPCS entry, SMW-6 commissioning, and ITC operators now collectively exceeding BSCPLC’s bandwidth, the company’s business model requires fundamental restructuring. The strategic direction must shift from high-margin, low-volume bandwidth sales to competitive pricing that maximises capacity utilisation, complemented by value-added services (managed wavelength, co-location, data centre interconnection at cable landing stations). BSCPLC must also lead the diversification of cable landing infrastructure beyond Cox’s Bazar to ensure national resilience.

3.8.4 Department of Telecommunications (DoT)

The Department of Telecommunications (DoT) faces several structural and operational challenges. The White Paper identifies its authority as narrow and its coordination with both BTRC and the Ministry as weak, leading to duplication of responsibilities and slow, unpredictable decision-making. Many policy and oversight functions originally assigned to DoT have drifted toward BTRC, while BTRC has expanded beyond its traditional compliance role into policy agenda-setting—a role inversion that has left DoT without a clearly defined operational mandate.

The White Paper recommends reintroducing a dedicated Telecom Cadre to institutionalise technical specialisation within DoT. This would create a permanent pool of technical expertise capable of formulating evidence-based telecommunications policy, monitoring sector performance, and providing the Ministry with informed advice on spectrum, infrastructure, and technology evolution—functions that require domain expertise distinct from the generalist administrative cadre that currently staffs the department. Revenue collection functions should be transferred from BTRC to appropriate government financial authorities, clarifying the boundary between regulatory and fiscal functions.

3.8.5 TSS, BSCL, and Other PTD Entities

Telephone Shilpa Sangstha (TSS), the state-owned telecom equipment manufacturer, and Bangladesh Satellite Company Limited (BSCL) both face existential questions about their relevance and sustainability. TSS was identified in the White Paper’s analysis of SOF projects as a vendor whose demand was, in certain instances, artificially generated through project approvals. BSCL’s role has become more significant with the Starlink partnership and the NTNSP licensing framework, but its operational capacity to manage satellite resale operations must be strengthened substantially. Bangladesh Cable Shilpa Limited (BCSL), the state-owned cable manufacturer, occupies a niche in the domestic cable supply chain but faces competition from imports.

The common thread across these entities is the absence of performance-linked governance. The comprehensive reform of state-owned telecom entities—bringing BTCL, Teletalk, BSCPLC, TSS, BSCL, BCSL, and DoT under a coordinated portfolio with shared performance targets (network coverage, service reliability, customer satisfaction, financial sustainability)—would create synergy among public operators and instill a culture of accountability. Formal service contracts for public mandates (rural connectivity, emergency communications, disaster-ready networks), backed by clear funding frameworks, measurable service levels, and independent audits, would redefine each entity’s role from an unfunded obligation model to a transparent, performance-based public service delivery model.



Summary: State-Owned Telecom Entities – Status and Strategic Direction

Entity

Current Status

Key Challenge

Strategic Direction

BTCL

~BDT 300 cr revenue; small profit from non-operating income; declining fixed-line base

Legacy copper infrastructure; bureaucratic culture; lack of commercial orientation

Reposition as national wholesale infrastructure provider; lead fibre-to-tower; MVNO with Teletalk

Teletalk

~6.6M subscribers (3.5%); persistent losses (BDT 197 cr FY23); declining base

Insufficient scale; weak financials; cannot self-fund 5G; regulatory debts

Three-option assessment: government network provider, merge with BTCL, or strategic partnership

BSCPLC

~BDT 60 cr revenue; consistently profitable; 39% capacity utilisation

Artificially high bandwidth pricing; declining market share vs. ITCs and BPCS

Price reduction to maximise utilisation; diversify into value-added services; diversify cable landing points

DoT

Narrow mandate; weak coordination with BTRC; drifted functions

No defined operational role; staffed by generalist cadre; policy vacuum

Reintroduce Telecom Cadre; define policy formulation mandate; transfer revenue collection from BTRC

TSS / BSCL / BCSL

Marginal revenue; sustainability questions; TSS linked to SOF procurement concerns

Relevance; operational capacity; competitive imports; vendor capture risk

Performance audit; role redefinition; BSCL to lead satellite resale partnership; BCSL to supply fibre rollout


3.9 Chapter Summary: The Regulatory Capture Diagnosis and the Reform Response

The analysis presented across both parts of this chapter establishes a central finding: Bangladesh’s telecommunications infrastructure—despite achieving impressive aggregate scale—has been shaped by regulatory capture that has produced outcomes misaligned with the public interest. The erosion of BTRC’s independence through the 2010 amendments, the issuance of licences without demand analysis, the tolerance of ownership structures that breached regulatory guidelines, the maintenance of artificially high bandwidth pricing, the mismanagement of the Social Obligation Fund, and the failure to enforce consumer protection standards collectively constitute a pattern in which the structures of oversight served concentrated interests rather than competitive markets and consumer welfare.

The reform programme initiated in 2025—comprising the Telecommunication (Amendment) Ordinance, the Licensing Policy, and the institutional measures recommended by the White Paper—addresses the structural roots of these failures. However, legislative and policy reform creates possibility, not certainty. The transformation of possibility into outcome depends on implementation: the capacity, will, and accountability of the institutions responsible for executing the reform agenda.

3.9.1 Five Structural Shifts

The reforms documented in this chapter, if implemented, will produce five structural shifts in Bangladesh’s telecommunications landscape:

From regulatory capture to independent oversight. The restoration of BTRC’s independence, the establishment of a search-cum-selection committee for senior appointments, and the HRIS-enforced merit-based HR processes create the institutional preconditions for regulatory decisions that serve the public interest rather than political or commercial interests.

From fragmented licensing to simplified, technology-neutral authorisation. The consolidation of 20+ licence categories into four (ANSP, NICSP, ICSP, NTNSP) reduces regulatory complexity, lowers barriers to entry, and eliminates the multi-layered value chain that inflated costs and created opportunities for cartel behaviour.

From concentrated infrastructure control to mandated sharing and open access. The infrastructure sharing mandates, SMP framework, and mandatory non-discrimination obligations address the tower duopoly, the NTTN duopoly, and the vertical integration that have constrained competition. Implementation requires BTRC to develop and enforce open-access pricing, conduct transparent SMP reviews, and mandate accounting separation with independent audits.

From high-price, low-utilisation bandwidth to competitive international connectivity. BSCPLC’s pricing reform, the entry of BPCS, SMW-6 commissioning, and Starlink’s satellite broadband collectively diversify Bangladesh’s international connectivity options and create competitive pressure that should drive bandwidth prices toward cost-reflective levels.

From opaque, politically directed universal access spending to results-based digital inclusion financing. The SOF’s restructuring—independent governance, ADP integration, outcome-based disbursement, competitive procurement, and independent impact evaluation—transforms a fund that has been characterised by idle balances, governance failures, and sustainability risks into an instrument of verifiable digital inclusion.

3.9.2 Consolidated Reform Timeline

Timeframe

Priority Actions

0–6 Months

BTRC accountability committee constituted; search-cum-selection committee for senior appointments established; SOF Board constituted; conflict-of-interest declarations filed; licensing migration roadmap published; new QoS benchmarks enforcement begins; customer code gazetted

6–12 Months

HRIS deployed at BTRC; QoS Dashboard operational; national digital complaint management system launched; BTRC guidelines for all 4 licence categories published; MORAN/MOCN sharing rules published; SOF contribution tracking system operational; 5G spectrum roadmap published; 700 MHz auction completed; BSCPLC pricing reform initiated; impact evaluation centre established

12–18 Months

Infrastructure sharing guidelines published; SMP framework operational with first market review; first independent HR audit of BTRC completed; telecom ombudsman established; spectrum trading framework established; independent SOF project completion audits

18–36 Months

50% fibre-to-tower target achieved; legacy licences migrated to new framework; BSCPLC capacity utilisation above 60%; first 5G commercial pilot; Teletalk strategic options assessment completed and implemented; cable landing diversification study initiated; SOF fund deployment exceeds 85%

By 2030

80% fibre-to-tower achieved; 5G commercially available in metropolitan areas; internet penetration exceeds 60%; Bangladesh in top 70 Ookla mobile quality ranking; SOF-financed connectivity in all 2,600 underserved unions; state-owned entity portfolio operating under performance contracts with independent audit



3.9.3 Interdependencies with Other Strategy Chapters

The telecommunications infrastructure and regulatory reforms analysed in this chapter are foundational to—but not sufficient for—the broader digital transformation. Chapter 4 (Sectoral Impacts Outside PTD) depends on the connectivity outcomes produced here: citizen-centric digital service delivery requires reliable broadband reach; digital commerce requires trusted, affordable connectivity; and educational and health applications require quality-of-service standards that are enforced rather than aspirational. Chapter 5 (Strategic Policies for Sovereignty and Innovation) builds on the spectrum management and satellite connectivity frameworks established here. Chapter 6 (Institutional Reform) addresses the organisational restructuring of PTD entities whose current conditions are documented in Section 3.8. Chapter 7 (Project Reform) directly addresses the SOF project governance failures analysed in Section 3.7. And the implementation roadmap in Chapter 9 integrates the timelines established in Section 3.9.2 with the reform sequences of all other chapters.

The central argument of this chapter is that connectivity outcomes are shaped not by technology alone, but by the quality of governance. When licensing frameworks reflect network realities, spectrum flows to its highest-value use, essential facilities are accessible on fair terms, consumers enjoy enforceable rights, and public funds drive measurable inclusion—and under these conditions, even modest investment produces substantial connectivity gains. Conversely, when regulatory processes are fragmented, captured, or slow, even the most advanced technologies underperform, and costs cascade across the entire value chain. The reforms articulated here are designed to shift Bangladesh’s telecommunications sector decisively from the latter condition to the former.

— End of Chapter 3—

4. Sectoral Impacts Beyond Posts and Telecommunications Division

4.1 Introduction: PTD as a Cross-Sectoral Enabler

The preceding chapters have examined the legislative framework and infrastructure architecture of the Posts and Telecommunications Division. This chapter shifts focus from supply-side reforms to demand-side outcomes: how the infrastructure, institutions, and regulatory frameworks under PTD translate into tangible improvements for citizens, enterprises, and sectors that fall outside the Division’s direct mandate. The central proposition is that postal and telecommunications assets are not ends in themselves but enabling platforms whose value is realised through the services they make possible in health, education, agriculture, financial inclusion, e-commerce, emergency response, and citizen-centric governance.

Bangladesh’s 188 million mobile subscriptions, 133 million internet connections, and 9,886 post offices constitute the country’s most extensive physical and digital service delivery network. No other government institution matches this combined geographic reach. Yet the sectoral potential of this network remains substantially unrealised. The Post e-Centre project, which invested BDT 541 crore to convert 8,500 rural post offices into digital centres, stands as a cautionary example: a Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) audit found that the project had comprehensively failed to achieve its objectives, with government funds effectively wasted. Mobile Financial Services, while transformative in their reach, have developed largely outside the institutional control of the Postal Directorate, as demonstrated by the Nagad experience documented in the PTD White Paper. Telemedicine platforms depend on reliable broadband that the telecom infrastructure does not yet consistently deliver to rural health facilities.

The National Digital Transformation Strategy 2025 to 2030, adopted by the ICT Division, establishes a comprehensive citizen-centric service design framework anchored in nine strategic actions: a national definition of digital public services, annual digital maturity assessments, common design and portal standards, the once-only principle for data reuse, procurement reform for outcomes, digital-first legislative reform, a digital service workforce framework, reusable BanglaGovStack building blocks, and public digital literacy programmes. PTD’s contribution to this whole-of-government agenda is not to replicate these strategic actions but to ensure that its infrastructure and institutions provide the reliable, affordable, and inclusive connectivity without which the broader digital transformation cannot reach the populations that need it most.

This chapter therefore examines five domains where PTD’s reform agenda intersects with sectoral outcomes: the transformation of the postal network into a citizen service delivery platform; financial inclusion through postal and mobile channels; e-commerce and logistics modernisation; the enabling role of telecom infrastructure across health, education, agriculture, and social protection; and emergency communications and disaster resilience. For each domain, the analysis identifies the current state, the specific reforms required from PTD entities, the interdependencies with other government agencies, and the measurable outcomes that should be achieved by 2030.



4.2 The Postal Network as a Citizen Service Delivery Platform

4.2.1 The 9,886-Office Opportunity

The Bangladesh Post Office operates 9,886 offices across the country, supported by approximately 40,000 personnel, making it one of the largest public service networks in the country. This physical infrastructure reaches communities that no other government agency consistently serves, particularly in remote char lands, haor areas, and hill districts where Union Parishad offices may be the only other institutional presence. The Universal Postal Union’s Integrated Index for Postal Development (2IPD) assessed Bangladesh at a composite score of approximately 54 in early 2025, ranking the country 68th among 174 nations, with a reliability sub-score of 72 in the 2024 assessment. While these scores represent improvement from a reliability score of 16.3 in 2021 and an overall IPD of 13.9, they continue to lag behind higher-performing operators and confirm that substantial operational gaps persist.

The operational reality is that the Bangladesh Post Office functions primarily as a traditional mail carrier, even as traditional mail volumes have declined precipitously worldwide. The general delivery standard of two to three days for domestic mail is frequently not met due to inadequate sorting infrastructure, a transport fleet without modern route optimisation, and a workforce whose skills have not kept pace with the shift from paper mail to parcels and digital services. The 14 Automated Mail Processing Centres established across districts at a cost of approximately BDT 365 crore have remained substantially dormant, with sophisticated sorting equipment accumulating dust due to inadequate training, maintenance failures, and integration gaps. Meanwhile, private courier and logistics firms, including Sundarban, eCourier, Paperfly, Pathao, and RedX, have captured the e-commerce logistics market, offering real-time tracking, same-day delivery in urban areas, and digital payment integration that the postal network cannot match.

Internationally, postal operators that have successfully transformed their operations share a common trajectory: India Post’s IT 2.0 programme, backed by an investment of INR 5,800 crore (approximately USD 700 million), deployed the Advanced Postal Technology platform across 1.70 lakh post offices by August 2025, introducing QR-code payments, OTP-based delivery authentication, real-time tracking, and integration with the Open Network for Digital Commerce. India Post now processes 3.2 million bookings and 3.7 million deliveries daily through a microservices-based architecture hosted on government cloud. It delivered 43 lakh passport applications, processed 6.94 lakh export shipments worth INR 148.19 crore through Dak Ghar Niryat Kendras, and opened 3.5 crore India Post Payments Bank accounts, 99 per cent at the doorstep and 59 per cent for women. Singapore’s SingPost and South Korea’s Korea Post have similarly repositioned themselves as logistics-technology platforms integrated with national e-commerce ecosystems.

The lesson for Bangladesh is clear: without a comparable technology investment and institutional transformation, the postal network will continue to lose relevance. The Postal Services Ordinance 2026, approved by the Council of Advisers on 5 February 2026, provides the legislative foundation by replacing the 1898 Post Office Act, introducing digital postage stamps and e-stamping, establishing e-commerce compliance requirements, mandating a modern address management system, and increasing the maximum administrative fine for unlicensed postal, courier, or parcel operations from BDT 50,000 to BDT 10 lakh. What remains is execution: converting legal authority into operational capability.

4.2.2 Post Offices as Nagorik Sheba Hubs

The National Digital Transformation Strategy identifies Nagorik Sheba Bangladesh as its flagship last-mile service delivery initiative: a nationwide programme to transform unused government premises, including post offices and union facilities, into community-based digital service centres operated by trained local entrepreneurs delivering over 500 government services through a human-assisted digital service model. The programme was inaugurated on 26 May 2025 by the Chief Advisor, with three pilot centres in Gulshan, Uttara, and Nilkhet, 60 trained entrepreneurs (42 per cent women), and a target to scale to 8,000 centres serving 80 million active users by 2030.

Post offices are natural host sites for Nagorik Sheba centres. They provide physical premises in locations where citizens already visit for postal, savings, and insurance services. Their nationwide distribution ensures geographic coverage that no purpose-built network could replicate at comparable cost. The Nagorik Sheba model operates as a zero-cost interoperability hub: centres are managed by licensed entrepreneurs under a sustainable social business framework, with private financing replacing direct government subsidies. For the Postal Directorate, hosting Nagorik Sheba centres converts underutilised postal premises into multi-service hubs that generate footfall, introduce new revenue streams, and create local employment without burdening the postal budget.

The integration pathway requires specific actions from PTD. First, the Postal Directorate must conduct a nationwide premises audit to identify post offices with adequate space, power supply, and internet connectivity to host Nagorik Sheba operations. The BD Post progress report indicates that six Nagorik Sheba centres have been established in postal premises at Gulshan, Uttara, and four additional locations, with a target of transforming 1,000 post offices into such centres. Second, BTCL broadband connectivity must be provisioned to all designated postal Nagorik Sheba sites, ensuring minimum speeds of 10 Mbps for service delivery operations. Third, the Postal Directorate must establish formal service-level agreements with the Nagorik Sheba programme authority to define premises standards, revenue sharing, operational boundaries, and dispute resolution mechanisms.

4.2.3 Lessons from the Post e-Centre Project

Any strategy for transforming the postal network into a digital service platform must reckon honestly with the failure of the Post e-Centre for Rural Community project, which invested BDT 541 crore between 2012 and 2017 to equip 8,500 rural post offices with computers, internet connections, and point-of-sale machines for delivering e-governance, e-commerce, e-education, and financial services. The CAG audit, conducted through visits to 900 centres over five and a half months, found that the project had comprehensively failed. Services listed in the Development Project Proposal, including internet access, e-governance, e-commerce, e-agriculture, and accelerated remittance disbursement, were included, in the CAG’s words, only to secure project approval. Government funds had been wasted.

The Implementation Monitoring and Evaluation Division (IMED) corroborated these findings: IT services were not fully activated in many offices; essential equipment such as photocopiers, POS machines, webcams, and computers was missing or non-functional; operators lacked ICT training; and electricity problems in rural areas went unaddressed. The infrastructure underutilisation was staggering: of 17,000 POS machines procured, only 704 were in use; of 10,000 finger vein machines, only 643 were operational; of 500 servers purchased, only 33 served their intended purpose while 467 were misapplied to unrelated functions. Equipment purchased under the Mail Processing and Logistics Services project was non-functional. The Postal Headquarters building itself remained unused for two years after completion.

The post-mortem reveals systemic failures that any successor initiative must explicitly address. The project lacked a viable operating model: equipment was procured without corresponding revenue streams, maintenance budgets, or trained operators. There was no demand-side assessment to determine which digital services rural populations actually needed and would use. No exit strategy ensured sustainability beyond the project period. Procurement was supply-driven, prioritising hardware acquisition over service design. And oversight was absent: the Project Completion Report was submitted a year late, and implementing agency responses to IMED observations were never reported.

Structural Safeguards for Future Initiatives: Any postal digitalisation programme must incorporate the following non-negotiable design principles. First, demand-validated service portfolios: before any technology procurement, the Postal Directorate must survey actual citizen demand at the upazila level and design service offerings accordingly. Second, sustainable operating models: each digital post office must have a business plan demonstrating revenue sufficiency for at least five years of operations, maintenance, and equipment refresh. Third, outcome-based procurement: technology contracts must be structured around service delivery milestones, not hardware delivery, with payments linked to verified operational status. Fourth, independent monitoring: quarterly audits by IMED or an independent evaluator, with findings published and mandatory management responses. Fifth, mandatory connectivity prerequisites: no equipment should be installed until broadband connectivity of at least 5 Mbps is confirmed and operational.

4.3 Financial Inclusion through the Postal and Telecom Network

4.3.1 Postal Financial Services

The Bangladesh Post Office has historically been a significant channel for financial inclusion in underserved communities. The Post Office Savings Bank and Postal Life Insurance provide savings and insurance products to populations that have limited access to commercial banking. The Electronic Money Transfer Service (EMTS), launched on 26 March 2010 and expanded to over 2,750 post offices, enables inter-office fund transfers using mobile phones, providing a basic remittance channel for rural households. These legacy financial services remain important: in a country where only 53 per cent of adults hold a bank account (Global Findex 2021), the post office network’s physical presence in every upazila offers a distribution advantage that no private financial institution can replicate.

However, the existing postal financial infrastructure operates on manual or semi-digital processes. Savings bank transactions are recorded in paper passbooks with limited electronic reconciliation. Insurance claim processing is paper-intensive with extended settlement periods. The EMTS, while functional, lacks the speed, convenience, and feature set of modern Mobile Financial Service platforms. The contrast with India Post Payments Bank is instructive: India’s postal banking arm, backed by its IT 2.0 digital platform and a Core Banking Solution linking 25,022 departmental post offices, opened 3.5 crore accounts with biometric authentication and doorstep service, of which 59 per cent were opened for women. Bangladesh’s postal financial services have no comparable digital backbone.

Reform Direction: The Postal Services Ordinance 2026 explicitly incorporates provisions for postal life insurance, savings banks, and expanded financial services. The Postal Directorate should digitise all savings bank transactions through a core banking platform integrated with the national payment switch (Bangladesh Electronic Fund Transfer Network). Postal Life Insurance should be modernised with digital policy issuance, premium collection through MFS channels, and electronic claims processing. The target should be to bring all postal financial transactions onto a digital platform by 2028, with interoperability with the national financial system ensured through Bangladesh Bank coordination.

4.3.2 The Nagad Experience: Governance Lessons

The most consequential financial service to emerge from the postal ecosystem is Nagad, the digital financial service launched by the Bangladesh Post Office on 26 March 2019 under the authority of the Post Office (Amendment) Act 2010, which permits the Postal Directorate to appoint agents for financial service delivery and to partner with institutions for remittance and banking services. Nagad recorded its highest monthly transaction value of BDT 34,000 crore in March 2025, surpassing private competitors in certain categories. Its Digital Know Your Customer (DKYC) system, which combines Bangla OCR, automated identity verification, and localised data to reduce customer onboarding time to one-tenth of market practice, represents a genuine technical innovation.

Yet the PTD White Paper’s Task Force findings reveal profound governance failures surrounding Nagad that must be documented for institutional learning. The Memorandum of Understanding signed between the Postal Directorate and the operating company on 26 April 2017 was for a pilot programme to test agency operations of the Postal Cash Card on a profit-sharing basis, raising the question of whether the Postal Directorate was legally permitted to launch a full-scale financial service through an MoU rather than a formal licensing process. The Postal Directorate’s institutional control over Nagad’s operations progressively eroded: the operating company changed its name to Nagad Limited based on a no-objection letter whose authenticity the Task Force found questionable, as the referenced source documents were missing from official files. Bangladesh Bank’s role was ambiguous, with the institution applying Mobile Financial Services Regulations 2018 to what the Postal Directorate consistently termed a Digital Financial Service, without amending the underlying legislation. Excess e-money creation raised serious accountability concerns.

The governance lessons are threefold. First, public digital financial services operated through the postal network must be subject to dual regulatory oversight by both Bangladesh Bank and the PTD, with clear delineation of prudential supervision (Bangladesh Bank) and institutional governance (PTD). Second, any agency arrangement for financial service delivery must be established through formal contracts approved by the relevant ministry, not through MoUs or pilot agreements that lack the transparency and accountability mechanisms of standard public procurement. Third, the Postal Directorate must retain demonstrable operational control over financial services bearing its institutional name, including control over customer data, transaction records, brand usage, and agent network management. The new Postal Services Ordinance provides the legal framework; the challenge is ensuring that implementing regulations enforce these governance standards.

4.3.3 Mobile Financial Services and Telecom’s Enabling Role

Mobile Financial Services have become the primary channel for digital financial inclusion in Bangladesh, with active accounts exceeding 60 million and monthly transaction volumes surpassing BDT 1 lakh crore. This ecosystem operates entirely on the telecommunications infrastructure regulated by BTRC and provided through mobile network operators. The MFS sector’s continued growth depends on three telecom-related conditions: universal mobile network coverage, including in rural areas where financial exclusion is most acute; affordable mobile data pricing that does not deter low-income users from transacting digitally; and network reliability that ensures transactions complete without failure, a critical trust factor for populations transitioning from cash.

PTD’s contribution to financial inclusion through MFS is therefore primarily infrastructural. The reforms outlined in Chapter 3, particularly the reduction of the telecommunications tax burden from 39 per cent, the expansion of 4G coverage through the Social Obligation Fund, the introduction of Teletalk’s strengthened rural network (targeting 85 per cent 4G coverage by June 2027 through 3,600 additional BTS sites), and BTCL’s broadband reach, all directly affect MFS penetration. Additionally, BTRC’s regulatory authority over USSD access pricing and short code allocation directly influences MFS operational costs and accessibility. BTRC should ensure that USSD access pricing for financial services remains regulated at levels that do not create barriers for low-income users, and that mobile operators provide non-discriminatory network access to all licensed MFS providers.





4.4 E-Commerce and Logistics Modernisation

4.4.1 Bangladesh’s E-Commerce Landscape

Bangladesh’s e-commerce sector has emerged as a significant economic force, with market revenue projected at approximately USD 7 billion in 2025, growing at a compound annual growth rate of 8 to 13 per cent through 2029 when the market is expected to reach USD 9.6 to 12.4 billion. Despite this growth, online retail accounts for only 3 to 5 per cent of total retail transactions, indicating that the market remains substantially under-penetrated compared to regional peers. The sector is led by Daraz (owned by Alibaba Group), with niche platforms including Chaldal (grocery), Pickaboo (electronics), and a growing ecosystem of social commerce operators leveraging Facebook and Instagram. Private logistics providers, including Pathao, RedX, Paperfly, eCourier, and Sundarban Courier, dominate last-mile delivery, with the Courier, Express, and Parcel segment projected to grow at a CAGR of 3.6 per cent through 2030.

The e-commerce sector’s growth faces two structural constraints that fall within PTD’s sphere of influence. First, the absence of a standardised national address system renders last-mile delivery inefficient and expensive, particularly outside major metropolitan areas. Delivery riders rely on phone calls to locate recipients, adding time and cost to each delivery. Over 90 per cent of e-commerce transactions use cash-on-delivery, reflecting both consumer distrust of digital payments and the logistical complexity of payment collection at unstandardised addresses. Second, the logistics infrastructure outside Dhaka and Chittagong is fragmented and underdeveloped, with limited cold chain capacity, inconsistent delivery timelines, and no integrated parcel tracking standards.

4.4.2 Postal Logistics Transformation

The Bangladesh Post Office has the physical infrastructure to participate meaningfully in the e-commerce logistics market, but it must fundamentally redefine its operating model from a mail carrier to a logistics platform. The BD Post progress report documents several promising but early-stage initiatives: a Central Logistics Tracking Platform (CLTP) is being developed; 85 mail vans have been equipped with GPS trackers for route transparency; 14 chiller chambers and 84 deep freezers have been installed for perishable goods; a reverse logistics middleware system for e-commerce returns is in the planning stage; and the domestic mail monitoring software (DMS) is being integrated through APIs with e-commerce and courier platforms.

Perhaps the most compelling proof of concept is the postal department’s mango delivery service, which connects farmers in Rajshahi and Chapainawabganj directly with consumers in Dhaka, cutting out intermediaries and securing fairer prices. In its most recent season, the service transported 65 tonnes of mango through the postal network. This agricultural market linkage model demonstrates the postal network’s potential as a rural logistics backbone connecting producers to urban and digital markets. If systematised and expanded to other seasonal products, it could establish the Post Office as a key node in the agricultural supply chain.

Strategic Direction: The postal logistics transformation should prioritise three capabilities. First, parcel and e-commerce fulfilment: the postal network must offer real-time tracking, digital proof of delivery, OTP-based authentication for high-value parcels, and integration with major e-commerce platforms through open APIs, replicating the capabilities that India Post’s IT 2.0 platform has demonstrated. Second, agricultural logistics: the mango delivery model should be expanded into a year-round agricultural market linkage service connecting rural producers with urban and export markets, utilising cold chain infrastructure and GPS-tracked transport. Third, reverse logistics: the planned middleware system for processing e-commerce returns should be developed to enable the postal network to compete for the growing reverse logistics market, which private operators find commercially challenging in semi-urban and rural areas.

Data-Driven Management: Implement a centralized platform for customer complaint management and data-analytics-based performance analysis.

Digital Tracking & Security: Deploy a National Address Management System (NAMS) and domestic mail tracking integrated with a secure source code repository to ensure data sovereignty.

Logistics Team Reform: Form specialized teams for effective mailing and courier service reforms to ensure KPI-based monitoring and the prevention of illegal/prohibited item transport.

4.4.3 National Address Management System

The National Address Management System (NAMS) represents one of the most consequential infrastructure initiatives for e-commerce, logistics, and citizen service delivery. The BD Post progress report indicates that the Project Evaluation Committee has provided conditional approval for NAMS, which will divide Bangladesh into structured micro-regions with unique identifiers, introduce GPS-based geofencing for accurate deliveries, and align with international postal standards to facilitate cross-border trade.

The absence of standardised addresses imposes costs across the economy. E-commerce firms report that 10 to 15 per cent of delivery attempts fail on the first try due to address ambiguity, adding approximately BDT 50 to 100 per delivery in re-attempt costs. Emergency services cannot geocode incident locations for efficient dispatch. Social protection payments cannot be reliably delivered to verified addresses. Land records, utility connections, and tax administration all suffer from the lack of a universal address register. Countries that have invested in postal code and address standardisation, including India (PIN code system with six-digit codes covering all 1.4 billion citizens), the United Kingdom (Royal Mail’s Postcode Address File covering 30 million addresses), and Rwanda (which implemented a comprehensive addressing system in partnership with the UPU in under three years), have demonstrated measurable improvements in logistics efficiency, tax collection, and emergency response times.

Implementation Pathway: NAMS should be implemented in three phases. Phase 1 (2026 to 2027): establish the addressing framework, including the coding structure, database architecture, and institutional governance, in partnership with the Survey of Bangladesh and the Local Government Division, covering all 64 districts and 495 upazilas with unique geo-referenced codes. Phase 2 (2027 to 2028): integrate NAMS with the National Portal, e-commerce platforms, and logistics providers through standardised APIs, and digitise all post office delivery zones using the new addressing system. Phase 3 (2028 to 2030): achieve 70 per cent GPS-geotag address coverage nationwide, as targeted by the National Digital Transformation Strategy, and establish cross-border address interoperability aligned with UPU S42 standards for international e-commerce fulfilment.

4.4.4 MOCSLA and Courier Sector Regulation

The Mailing Operator and Courier Services Licensing Authority (MOCSLA), established in 2014, is responsible for licensing, monitoring, and enforcing compliance among private courier and mailing operators. It maintains a public registry of licensed operators and oversees reforms to modernise postal logistics. The Postal Services Ordinance 2026 significantly strengthens MOCSLA’s enforcement capacity by increasing penalties for unlicensed operations and establishing a modern regulatory framework for the courier and parcel sector.

The courier sector in Bangladesh is characterised by a small number of established operators alongside a large informal segment of unregistered delivery services, particularly those serving social commerce transactions on Facebook and Instagram. MOCSLA’s regulatory challenge is to bring this informal segment into a licensed framework that ensures consumer protection, including parcel tracking, insurance for high-value items, and complaint resolution, without imposing compliance costs that stifle the sector’s growth. The regulatory model should be risk-proportionate: lighter registration requirements for micro-operators handling fewer than 100 parcels per day, with progressive compliance obligations as operators scale.

All major





4.5 Telecom Infrastructure as Enabler of Sectoral Transformation

The telecommunications network regulated by BTRC and operated by both private and state-owned providers serves as the enabling layer for digital transformation across every sector of the economy. The National Digital Transformation Strategy sets ambitious targets: 50 to 75 per cent of health facilities digitised by 2027 to 2028, 40 to 60 per cent of schools with digital classrooms, 5 to 10 million farmers using agricultural applications, and 50 to 75 million active e-service users. None of these targets can be met without reliable, affordable, and geographically comprehensive telecommunications. This section examines four sectors where the connection between PTD’s reforms and citizen outcomes is most direct.

4.5.1 Health: Telemedicine and Health Information Systems

Telemedicine has emerged as a critical healthcare delivery channel in Bangladesh, with over 1 million annual teleconsultations delivered through platforms supported by the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare. The National Digital Transformation Strategy targets 8 to 15 million teleconsultations annually by 2027 to 2028, rising to 25 to 40 million by 2030, alongside 25 to 35 million citizens with unique Health IDs by 2028 and 50 million by 2030. These targets require broadband connectivity at every community health centre, upazila health complex, and district hospital, with minimum bandwidth sufficient to support video consultations, medical image transmission, and electronic health record synchronisation.

The infrastructure gap is significant. Many rural health facilities rely on mobile data connections with inconsistent speeds, making video-based teleconsultation unreliable. The Social Obligation Fund’s Connected Bangladesh project, examined in Chapter 3, has deployed internet connectivity in selected community clinics, but coverage remains incomplete and the sustainability of these connections beyond the project period is uncertain. BTCL’s fibre network, currently underutilised, could provide dedicated broadband links to health facilities in areas where it passes, but no systematic health connectivity programme exists to convert this infrastructure potential into actual service.

PTD Action Required: BTRC should collaborate with the Ministry of Health to establish a Health Connectivity Programme that maps all public health facilities against existing fibre and mobile coverage, identifies connectivity gaps, and prioritises infrastructure investment accordingly. BTCL should be mandated to provide wholesale broadband to health facilities within 2 kilometres of its fibre route at concessional rates funded through the Social Obligation Fund. For facilities beyond fibre reach, Teletalk’s 4G network expansion and the proposed satellite connectivity through BSCL’s Starlink resale partnership should be designated as alternative delivery channels. The target should be broadband connectivity at 100 per cent of upazila health complexes by 2027 and 75 per cent of community clinics by 2029.

4.5.2 Education: Digital Classrooms and E-Learning

The education sector’s digital transformation depends fundamentally on school connectivity. The National Digital Transformation Strategy targets 40 to 60 per cent of schools with digital classrooms by 2027 to 2028, 10 to 15 million students on e-learning platforms, and 200,000 to 300,000 teachers trained in ICT. By 2030, the targets rise to 80 to 100 per cent of schools with digital classrooms and 20 to 25 million students on digital learning platforms. Bangladesh has approximately 150,000 primary and secondary educational institutions, of which a substantial proportion, particularly government primary schools in rural areas, currently lack internet connectivity.

The ITU’s Giga initiative, which aims to connect every school to the internet globally, estimates that school connectivity in low and lower-middle-income countries requires a combination of fibre, fixed wireless, and satellite technologies, with the choice depending on school location, population density, and existing infrastructure. For Bangladesh, BTCL’s fibre network and the NTTN infrastructure of Fiber@Home and Summit can serve schools in urban and peri-urban areas. Teletalk’s expanded 4G network can provide connectivity to schools in areas with mobile coverage. Satellite solutions, including the proposed NTNSP licensing framework for providers like Starlink, will be necessary for schools in remote haor, char, and hill-tract areas.

PTD Action Required: BTRC should work with the Ministry of Education to develop a School Connectivity Plan following the Giga methodology, mapping all schools against available infrastructure and producing a cost deployment roadmap. The Social Obligation Fund should allocate a dedicated window for school connectivity, with clear per-school cost benchmarks and outcome-based disbursement linked to verified operational connectivity. Spectrum allocation decisions, particularly the assignment of 700 MHz band for rural broadband, should incorporate school connectivity targets as a condition of spectrum licence obligations for successful bidders.

4.5.3 Agriculture: Connectivity for Rural Enterprise

Agriculture employs approximately 38 per cent of Bangladesh’s workforce and contributes around 12 per cent of GDP, but the sector’s productivity remains constrained by information asymmetries: farmers lack timely access to market prices, weather forecasts, pest management guidance, and input supply information. The National Digital Transformation Strategy targets 5 to 10 million farmers using agricultural applications by 2028 and 15 to 20 million by 2030, alongside 30 to 50 per cent of villages with agricultural information hubs and 2,000 to 5,000 drone-assisted crop surveys by 2028.

The postal network’s role in agricultural transformation extends beyond connectivity. The mango delivery service demonstrates that post offices can function as aggregation and distribution points for agricultural produce. The BD Post progress report notes agricultural market linkage activity connecting farmers directly with consumers. The potential to expand this model into a systematic rural e-commerce logistics service is significant: post offices could serve as collection centres where farmers deposit produce for onward distribution, receive payments through postal financial services or MFS, and access price information through digital kiosks. This model is particularly relevant for perishable products where the cold chain infrastructure (chiller chambers and deep freezers) that the Postal Directorate has begun installing could ensure product quality.

PTD Action Required: The agricultural logistics model requires coordination between the Postal Directorate, the Department of Agricultural Extension, and e-commerce platforms. PTD should establish an Agricultural Postal Logistics Unit within the Postal Directorate to manage collection, cold chain, and distribution operations for agricultural products. BTRC should ensure that mobile network quality-of-service standards cover agricultural zones, including char and haor areas, where connectivity gaps directly constrain agricultural advisory services. Teletalk, as a state-owned operator, should be given specific coverage obligations for agricultural production zones identified by the Department of Agricultural Extension.

4.5.4 Social Protection: Digital Payment Delivery

Bangladesh’s social safety net programmes serve over 9 million beneficiaries through allowances for the elderly, persons with disabilities, widows, and other marginalised groups. The shift from manual cash disbursement to digital payment, primarily through Mobile Financial Services, has reduced leakage and improved payment timeliness, but significant coverage gaps persist, particularly in areas where MFS agent networks are thin. The post office network offers an alternative and complementary disbursement channel: its presence in every upazila provides a physical payment point where beneficiaries who cannot use MFS (due to age, disability, or lack of a mobile phone) can collect payments.

The post office’s role in social protection payment delivery was recognised as early as 2020, when the Prime Minister directed that Nagad be used for social safety net allowance disbursement. However, the Nagad governance concerns documented in the White Paper have complicated this channel. The reform pathway is to decouple the payment delivery function from any single MFS provider and instead establish the postal network as a multi-channel payment disbursement platform that can work with any licensed MFS provider, bank, or payment system to deliver social protection payments to beneficiaries at post office counters using biometric authentication.

PTD Action Required: The Postal Directorate should develop a Social Protection Payment Gateway that integrates with the Ministry of Social Welfare’s beneficiary database and supports disbursement through multiple channels: direct post office counter payment with biometric verification, MFS credit to beneficiary mobile wallets, and bank account transfer. The platform should be channel-agnostic, ensuring that the choice of payment method rests with the beneficiary, not the provider. The target should be to make post office payment disbursement available in all 64 district headquarters by 2027 and all 495 upazilas by 2029.

4.6 Disaster Resilience and Emergency Communications

Bangladesh’s exposure to climate-related disasters, including cyclones, floods, riverbank erosion, and sea-level rise, makes telecommunications resilience a matter of life safety. The National Digital Transformation Strategy identifies disaster recovery and smart national infrastructure safety as a strategic priority, proposing AI-enabled surveillance, satellite and drone coverage for disaster zones, IoT-based sensors for critical infrastructure monitoring, and smart highway systems. The telecommunications network’s role in disaster resilience operates at three levels: early warning dissemination, emergency response coordination, and post-disaster service restoration.

At the first level, cell broadcast technology enables mass dissemination of disaster warnings to all mobile phones within affected areas without requiring recipients to have data connections or specific applications. BTRC should mandate the deployment of cell broadcast capability across all mobile networks by 2027, following the model of the EU’s Public Warning Systems Directive and Japan’s Earthquake Early Warning System, which broadcasts alerts within seconds of seismic detection. At the second level, the integration of National Helplines 333 (citizen services) and 999 (emergency services) through the technical and operational framework proposed in the National Digital Transformation Strategy requires telecommunications backhaul capacity and call routing infrastructure that BTRC must ensure operators provide. At the third level, network resilience requires physical infrastructure hardening, including tower reinforcement in cyclone-prone zones, battery backup extending beyond current four-hour standards, and geographic redundancy in core network routing to ensure that the loss of a single node does not isolate an entire region.

The submarine cable landing station at Cox’s Bazar represents a specific vulnerability: as documented in Chapter 3, all of Bangladesh’s international fibre connectivity lands at a single coastal location highly exposed to cyclone damage. The diversification of cable landing points, whether through the proposed Khulna or Payra landing station or through satellite backup via BSCL’s Starlink partnership and the proposed NTNSP licensing framework, is a disaster resilience imperative that extends well beyond the telecommunications sector to every digital service that depends on international connectivity.

PTD Action Required: BTRC should develop a Telecom Disaster Resilience Framework, in coordination with the Ministry of Disaster Management and Relief, that establishes minimum infrastructure resilience standards for telecommunications facilities in each disaster zone category; mandates cell broadcast deployment on all mobile networks by 2027; requires operators to maintain pre-positioned mobile cell-on-wheels (COWs) for rapid post-disaster coverage restoration; and ensures that the 333/999 integration has geographically redundant call processing centres. The Social Obligation Fund should include disaster-resilient connectivity as an eligible expenditure category, funding tower hardening, backup power, and emergency satellite terminals in the most vulnerable districts.





4.7 Chapter Summary: From Infrastructure to Impact

This chapter has demonstrated that the value of PTD’s infrastructure and institutions is ultimately measured by the sectoral outcomes they enable. The postal network’s 9,886 offices can become citizen service delivery platforms, financial inclusion channels, agricultural logistics hubs, and social protection payment points, but only if the institutional failures exemplified by the Post e-Centre project and the Nagad governance lapses are addressed through the structural safeguards identified in this chapter. The telecommunications network’s 188 million mobile connections can enable telemedicine, digital classrooms, agricultural advisory services, and disaster early warning, but only if the quality-of-service, affordability, and coverage reforms outlined in Chapter 3 are implemented.

The cross-cutting theme is that PTD’s sectoral impact requires sustained coordination with line ministries and agencies that it has historically not maintained. The Health Connectivity Programme requires joint planning with the Ministry of Health. The School Connectivity Plan requires coordination with the Ministry of Education. The National Address Management System requires partnership with the Local Government Division and the Survey of Bangladesh. The Social Protection Payment Gateway requires integration with the Ministry of Social Welfare’s beneficiary database. Agricultural logistics require collaboration with the Department of Agricultural Extension. Disaster resilience requires coordination with the Ministry of Disaster Management and Relief.

The institutional mechanism for this coordination is addressed in Chapter 6 (Institutional Reform). The project governance framework is addressed in Chapter 7 (Project Reform). The implementation timeline integrating all sectoral targets is consolidated in Chapter 9 (Roadmap). The immediate priority for PTD is to recognise that its performance will increasingly be judged not by the volume of spectrum auctioned or licences issued, but by the measurable improvement in citizens’ access to health, education, financial, and government services that its infrastructure makes possible.
