6.3 Teletalk Bangladesh Limited

6.3.1 Rationale for Reform

Teletalk Bangladesh Limited occupies a unique but precarious position as the sole state owned mobile operator in a market dominated by four private operators (Grameenphone, Robi, Banglalink, and newly licensed entrants). With a subscriber base representing a modest fraction of the total mobile market, Teletalk's commercial viability has long been questioned. The PTD Whitepaper documents persistent challenges including delayed 4G rollout, inadequate network coverage, dependence on government and foreign funded projects for capital expenditure, and governance weaknesses in procurement and human resource management.

Nevertheless, Teletalk serves critical sovereign functions that private operators cannot be relied upon to fulfil. These include secure government communications, biometric SIM verification infrastructure (the National Identification Database interface), coverage obligations in commercially unviable areas such as the Chittagong Hill Tracts and border regions, and the provision of mobile connectivity for government employees and defence forces. The reform agenda for Teletalk must therefore balance two objectives: improving commercial competitiveness to reduce fiscal dependence, while strengthening Teletalk's role as a sovereign communications backbone. Norway's Telenor (prior to full privatization) and India's BSNL restructuring (2019 revival package) offer relevant precedents for reforming state owned mobile operators.

6.3.2 Reform Initiatives and Current Status

Initiative

Current Status

Timeline

Expected Outcome

VoLTE Service Launch

Limited rollout completed; preparation for full subscriber base rollout underway

February 2026

Improved voice call quality for LTE enabled devices; enhanced customer satisfaction

VoWiFi Service Launch

Limited rollout completed; full deployment preparation ongoing

February 2026

Extended indoor coverage; reduced call drops in weak signal areas

Tower Sharing (2,300 Sites)

Lease agreement signed November 2025; 1,720 site surveys ongoing; 95 sites under equipment installation; 15 sites live

December 2026

Reduced CAPEX for new site deployment; accelerated network expansion; operational cost reduction

Battery Capacity Expansion

1,420 sites modernized under various projects; additional 350 sites under modernization

March 2026

Improved network availability; reduced downtime from power disruptions

4G Coverage Expansion

1,699 new 4G BTS sites activated; daily data usage increased from 354 TB to 464 TB (20% increase); 3,600 additional sites planned

June 2027

BTCL MVNO SIM

Billing and provisioning links established; three packages designed; 500 SIM pilot supply prepared

Awaiting launch

5G BTS Launch

Completed

Technology readiness demonstration; regulatory and technical validation for 5G

New ADP Projects (Remote Areas)

Three project DPPs prepared for ADP green list: remote area coverage, public university connectivity, and highway corridor 4G

April 2026

Digital inclusion in underserved areas; educational connectivity; highway safety communications

Sales and Marketing Acceleration

Cloud Phone service launched December 2025; MFS recharge partnerships with bKash and others; SIM distribution via Post Offices launched

Completed

Revenue growth; diversified distribution channels; improved customer accessibility

6.3.3 Strategic Improvement Agenda

Teletalk's most critical near term challenge is the tower sharing programme. The agreement to lease 2,300 sites from a tower company represents a fundamental shift from BTCL-era asset ownership models toward the shared infrastructure approach that has proven cost effective across emerging markets in Africa and Southeast Asia. The conversion of lease agreements into operational sites must be accelerated, with clear monthly targets, a dedicated project management unit, and penalty clauses for delayed handover. edotco Group's tower sharing operations in Malaysia and Myanmar provide a relevant regional model for the operational framework Teletalk should adopt.

The 5G demonstration at four sites is symbolically important but must be followed by a realistic 5G deployment strategy that does not repeat the delays of 4G rollout. Given Teletalk's financial constraints, 5G deployment should initially target high value use cases (enterprise customers, industrial IoT zones, university campuses) rather than competing for mass consumer 5G coverage, which private operators with larger spectrum holdings and deeper capital reserves will inevitably provide first. India's BSNL adopted a similar phased approach after its 2019 revival package, prioritizing 4G network completion before selective 5G deployment.

The distribution partnership with Bangladesh Post for SIM sales through post offices is an innovative cross entity synergy that should be expanded. With over 8,500 post offices nationwide, this channel provides unmatched rural reach. However, success depends on training postal staff in SIM activation procedures, maintaining adequate SIM inventory at post offices, and integrating the distribution data into Teletalk's customer management systems. Japan Post's partnership with mobile operators for device and plan distribution at post offices offers a proven model for this approach.

6.4 Bangladesh Submarine Cable Company PLC (BSCPLC)

6.4.1 Rationale for Reform

BSCPLC operates Bangladesh's submarine cable landing stations and manages the country's international internet bandwidth connectivity. As the sole state owned international connectivity service provider, BSCPLC's pricing, service quality, and capacity planning directly affect the cost and reliability of internet access for every citizen and enterprise in Bangladesh. The company manages connections through the SEA ME WE 4 and SEA ME WE 5 submarine cable systems and provides IPLC, IP transit, co location, port services, and NIX (National Internet Exchange) services to domestic operators and international carriers.

The reform programme has focused on two immediate objectives: reducing bandwidth prices to lower end user internet costs, and strengthening financial discipline through aggressive dues recovery. Between March 2025 and January 2026, BSCPLC has reduced bandwidth prices by 30 to 50 percent effective 1 April 2025, introduced customer friendly packages for IPLC and IP transit clients, reduced port charges by at least 10 percent, and launched a systematic dues recovery programme targeting major defaulters. These price reductions, while commercially significant, bring Bangladesh closer to regional parity with India and Vietnam, though further reductions remain necessary to achieve the national target of affordable broadband for all.

6.4.2 Reform Initiatives and Current Status

Initiative

Current Status

Timeline

Expected Outcome

Tariff and Package Rationalization

New tariff effective 1 April 2025; bandwidth price reduced 30 to 50%; promotional packages for IPLC customers including three free 10G circuits per 100G purchase on SMW4

Completed

Consumer level internet cost reduction; increased competitiveness with ITC providers

Service Simplification and Client Engagement

Responsibility matrix developed; customer consultation held 27 February 2025; 24 hour upgrade commitment on demand note payment; 7 day wet segment activation promise

Ongoing

Faster service delivery; improved client relationships; reduced service complaints

Dues Recovery and Financial Discipline

Major defaulter list prepared (including Amra Technologies, Mango Teleservices); BTRC notified; active pursuit of outstanding payments

Ongoing

Strengthened balance sheet; improved cash flow; reduced bad debt exposure

Bandwidth Usage Ratio Enforcement

BTRC directed 50:50 submarine to ITC bandwidth ratio; Windstream Communications Ltd obtained High Court stay on 31 July 2025; BSCPLC pursuing ratio reinstatement

Under legal review

Level playing field between submarine and ITC bandwidth; market discipline

Port Charge Reduction

Port charges reduced minimum 10% effective 1 April 2025

Completed

Lower interconnection costs for operators; downstream savings for consumers

ITC Monitoring and Licensing Compliance

Recommendations submitted for accurate ITC bandwidth import measurement and strict telecom licensing policy enforcement

Pending regulatory action

Market transparency; fair competition; revenue protection for licensed operators

6.4.3 Strategic Improvement Agenda

BSCPLC's most pressing institutional challenge is the bandwidth usage ratio dispute. The principle that a balanced ratio between submarine cable and International Terrestrial Cable (ITC) bandwidth protects the viability of the state owned submarine cable investment is strategically sound, but the legal challenge from Windstream Communications demonstrates the need for a regulatory framework that can withstand judicial scrutiny. BTRC should formalize the ratio policy through a transparent regulatory determination process, supported by published cost studies and public consultation, rather than relying solely on administrative directives. International precedents from the European Union's Significant Market Power (SMP) framework and India's interconnection usage charge regime demonstrate how such access obligations can be legally robust when grounded in transparent cost modelling.

Second, BSCPLC must prepare for the anticipated growth in bandwidth demand driven by 5G deployment, expanding OTT consumption, cloud migration, and enterprise digitization. Capacity planning for SEA ME WE 6 participation and potential alternative routing through additional cable systems (such as proposed Bay of Bengal cable projects) should be initiated to ensure that Bangladesh is not constrained by single point of failure submarine cable dependencies. Japan's NICT and Singapore's IMDA have both invested in submarine cable diversity as a matter of national digital resilience.

Third, the tax and duty burden on telecommunications, including supplementary duty, VAT, and surcharges, is creating downstream pricing pressure that undermines the government's own objective of affordable internet. BSCPLC, as the primary wholesale bandwidth provider, is well positioned to provide evidence-based analysis to the Ministry of Finance and National Board of Revenue on the impact of fiscal policies on bandwidth pricing. A sector specific tax rationalization study, benchmarked against regional competitors, should be commissioned jointly by BSCPLC and BTRC.

Additionally, the following reforms must be introduced:

Landing Station Modernization: Introduce data centers, cloud hosting, cache storage, and edge routers at cable landing stations to increase domestic digital services.

Infrastructure Redundancy: Ensure land connectivity redundancy between Kuakata and Cox's Bazar through BTCL/PGCB fiber to divert capacity if a landing station fails.

Operational Governance: Conduct biannual tariff coordination and stakeholder meetings. Ensure 100% payment security and zero-default protocols for all customers.

Vendor Independence: Implement a technology transfer protocol with vendors/OEMs to reduce long-term dependency and enhance local technical handover.

6.5 Bangladesh Post Office

6.5.1 Rationale for Reform

Bangladesh Post Office is arguably the PTD entity with the greatest untapped potential for transformative impact on citizen services. With a network of over 8,500 post offices reaching into the most remote villages, a workforce with deep community trust, and physical infrastructure in every upazila, the postal network represents a pre existing national service delivery platform that no private logistics company can replicate at equivalent scale. Globally, the postal reform movement initiated by the Universal Postal Union (UPU) in the early 2000s has demonstrated that modernized postal services can serve as platforms for financial inclusion, e commerce logistics, government service delivery, and rural entrepreneurship. Countries as diverse as Brazil (Correios), India (India Post), Kenya (Posta Kenya), and China (China Post) have successfully repositioned their postal systems from letter delivery monopolies into diversified logistics and financial service platforms.

The reform programme underway since May 2025 has been remarkably multifaceted, addressing logistics modernization, digital service delivery, agricultural market linkages, cold chain development, legal reform, and the ambitious programme to convert 1,000 Digital Centres into full service Nagorik Sheba (Citizen Service) Centres. The draft Postal Services Ordinance, currently awaiting Advisory Council approval, represents the most significant legal reform in the postal sector in decades, providing the statutory foundation for a commercially oriented, digitally enabled postal service.

6.5.2 Reform Initiatives and Current Status

Initiative

Current Status

Timeline

Expected Outcome

Central Logistics Tracking Platform (CLTP)

Onboarding phase active since March 2025; stakeholder meetings completed with e commerce, courier, payment gateway, and regulatory bodies; integrated digital logistics platform foundation established

Ongoing

Unified logistics tracking; interoperability with private couriers and e commerce platforms

API sharing initiative with courier and e commerce platforms under development

Vehicle monitoring; route optimization; transportation transparency and security

Ongoing

Perishable goods transport capability; agricultural value chain strengthening

Alternative mechanism established for courier mail delivery through postal system due to courier representative access restrictions

In progress

Resolution of secretariat courier delivery bottleneck

E Commerce Service Hour Extension

Committee formed to evaluate office hour extension for entrepreneurs and field officers

Planning stage

Improved accessibility for e commerce entrepreneurs; extended service availability

Financial Transaction Modernization (EMS and EMTS)

EMS and EMTS service modernization and expansion ongoing

In progress

Faster merchant payments; improved e commerce money order processing

National Address Management System (NAMS)

Project entitled Expanding and Facilitating Existing Postal Services at Citizen Level; PEC meeting completed; conditional approval provided

Conditional approval

Digital delivery capability; geocoded addressing; last mile delivery accuracy

Agricultural Market Linkage (Mango Transport)

65 tonnes of mangoes transported from Rajshahi, Chapainawabganj, Naogaon, and Satkhira to Dhaka during May to July 2025 season; direct farmer to consumer delivery

Completed (seasonal)

Profitable market access for small farmers; intermediary bypass; rural income enhancement

Commemorative Stamp Programme

Five commemorative stamps released: Independence Day (26 March), World Telecom Day (17 May), July Uprising Day (5 August), World Post Day (9 October), and Victory Day (15 December 2025)

Completed

National identity promotion; philatelic revenue; cultural and institutional branding

Nagorik Sheba (Citizen Service) Centres

Three pilot centres launched 26 May 2025 at Gulshan, Uttara, Nilkhet by Chief Adviser; three additional centres at Banasree, Mohammadpur, Gulistan opened 22 October 2025; phased conversion of 1,000 Digital Centres ongoing

Ongoing (target December 2026)

End to end government service delivery at single location; one stop citizen access

Postal Services Ordinance

Draft finalized by PTD; awaiting Advisory Council approval for policy endorsement

Pending

Legal foundation for modernized postal services; regulatory reform enabling commercial operations

Postal Ballot Modernization

SOP development in coordination with Election Commission; digital tracking system integration for ballot paper security and confidentiality

In progress

Democratic participation for election duty officials, overseas citizens, and remote area residents

ED Employee Management Reform

ED employee policy prepared and submitted to Ministry for approval; ED allowance increase proposal also submitted

Pending approval

Workforce motivation; socioeconomic security for Extra Departmental employees

6.5.3 Strategic Improvement Agenda

The most transformative initiative in the postal reform programme is the conversion of 1,000 Digital Centres into Nagorik Sheba Centres. This programme has the potential to create the largest citizen service delivery network in Bangladesh, offering government services, financial transactions, e commerce facilitation, and digital literacy training at a single physical location in every upazila. The model draws inspiration from India Post's transformation under the India Post Payments Bank (IPPB) initiative, which converted post offices into banking access points serving over 60 million accounts in underserved areas, and Brazil's Correios, which provides over 200 government services through its post office network.

However, the success of this programme depends critically on three factors. First, the digital infrastructure at each centre must be standardized and maintained, including reliable internet connectivity, biometric verification devices, secure payment terminals, and trained staff. Second, the service catalogue must be progressively expanded through interoperability agreements with other government agencies, moving beyond basic services toward passport applications, land records, health insurance enrolment, and social safety net disbursements. Third, the Nagorik Sheba Centre model must be financially sustainable through a combination of government service delivery fees, commission on financial transactions, and e commerce facilitation charges that generate sufficient revenue to cover operational costs.

The National Address Management System (NAMS) represents a foundational infrastructure investment that will benefit not only the postal sector but the entire logistics and service delivery ecosystem. Without a standardized, geocoded addressing system, last mile delivery remains inefficient, emergency response is hampered, and digital service delivery (including drone based delivery, which the National Digital Transformation Strategy envisions) cannot be reliably scaled. The United Kingdom's Postcode Address File and India's PIN Code system are long established examples; more recent initiatives such as what3words and Estonia's address data infrastructure demonstrate how digital addressing can be implemented rapidly in emerging markets.

The cold chain logistics initiative, while modest in current scale (14 chiller chambers and 84 deep freezers), addresses a critical gap in Bangladesh's agricultural value chain. The loss of perishable produce between farm gate and consumer is estimated at 30 to 40 percent in Bangladesh, representing both a food security challenge and an economic waste. Expanding the postal cold chain, particularly during seasonal harvests of mangoes, lychees, and vegetables, can position Bangladesh Post as a trusted agricultural logistics partner. Thailand Post's farm to consumer cold chain service and China Post's rural e commerce logistics network provide relevant models.

6.6 Bangladesh Satellite Company Limited (BSCL)

6.6.1 Rationale for Reform

Bangladesh Satellite Company Limited (BSCL) operates the nation's first geostationary communications satellite, Bangabandhu Satellite 1 (BS 1), and is positioning itself as the institutional anchor for Bangladesh's space economy ambitions. BS 1, launched in May 2018, provides C band and Ku band transponder capacity for broadcasting, telecommunications backhaul, and VSAT connectivity. However, capacity utilization has remained below commercial expectations, partly due to limited domestic demand development and constrained international marketing capability. BSCL's mandate has expanded significantly in 2025 to encompass Starlink reselling, maritime satellite services, cable television digitalization, and earth observation satellite planning, representing a strategic pivot from single satellite operation toward a diversified space services portfolio.

6.6.2 Reform Initiatives and Current Status

Initiative

Current Status

Timeline

Expected Outcome

BS 1 Bandwidth Sales (Domestic)

Contracts signed with Bangladesh Navy, DGFI, and Police Special Branch for BS 1 bandwidth utilization; security sector adoption progressing

Ongoing

Internal security communications strengthening; sovereign satellite utilization increase

BS 1 Bandwidth Sales (International)

Preliminary discussions completed with Nepal; proof of concept preparation ongoing; discussions with Philippines and Indonesia underway

Ongoing

Foreign exchange earnings; regional market presence; satellite capacity monetization

Starlink Reselling in Bangladesh

BSCL operating as Starlink reseller; kits and data plans being sold; services deployed to BGB, WFP, Chittagong Hill Tracts schools (approximately 10 institutions); Grameenphone partnership for service expansion underway

Operational

High speed internet in remote and underserved areas; institutional connectivity in difficult terrain

Starlink at State Guest House Jamuna

Starlink internet deployed at all locations used by Chief Adviser at Jamuna state guest house

Completed

Secure, high bandwidth connectivity for state leadership; technical capability demonstration

Maritime AIS 2.0 System

Initial specifications prepared; stakeholder consultations completed; funding proposal submitted to BTRC SOF Committee

In progress (target June 2026)

Maritime security; vessel tracking in territorial waters; blue economy enablement

Cable Television Network Digitalization

Concept paper presented to Information and Broadcasting Ministry; verbal policy approval received; BSCL to develop central platform, set top box specifications, and TRP measurement framework

In progress (target June 2026)

Broadcasting transparency; TRP based advertising market discipline; content distribution modernization

Earth Observation (EO) Satellite Planning

Draft writeup on environmental impact of EO satellites under review by Managing Director

In progress (target December 2026)

Climate monitoring; disaster preparedness; agricultural planning; sovereign earth observation capability

TRP Device Deployment

Awaiting minutes of Information and Broadcasting Ministry meeting for next steps

Pending

Transparent television viewership data; advertising market rationalization

6.6.3 Strategic Improvement Agenda

BSCL's institutional transformation must be evaluated against the backdrop of a rapidly evolving global satellite industry. The emergence of low earth orbit (LEO) constellation providers, primarily Starlink but also OneWeb, Amazon Kuiper, and Telesat Lightspeed, has fundamentally altered the economics of satellite connectivity. Rather than viewing LEO as a competitive threat to BS 1, BSCL's strategy of becoming a Starlink reseller while continuing to market BS 1's geostationary capacity for specific use cases (broadcasting, maritime VSAT, government communications) represents a pragmatic adaptation. The reseller model ensures that Bangladesh participates in the LEO connectivity revolution while maintaining sovereign oversight and local distribution control.

The cable television digitalization initiative presents a major institutional opportunity. Bangladesh's cable TV market, estimated at over 20 million households, currently operates largely through analogue distribution by local cable operators with minimal transparency in subscriber counts, content licensing, or viewership measurement. BSCL's proposal to serve as the central digital platform, supported by standardized set top boxes and TRP measurement, mirrors the digital migration mandated by India's Telecom Regulatory Authority (TRAI) beginning in 2012, which transformed India's cable industry from an opaque, tax evading sector into a digitized, auditable industry. The implementation must, however, be carefully sequenced to avoid the disruptions India experienced during its initial digitalization phases.

BS 2 planning must commence immediately given BS 1's finite orbital life. The procurement and launch cycle for a geostationary communications satellite typically requires four to six years from initial planning to orbit. BSCL should engage with potential satellite manufacturers, evaluate the relative merits of traditional GEO capacity versus hybrid GEO MEO constellations, and develop a business case that reflects realistic demand forecasts rather than the aspirational projections that characterized the BS 1 procurement process.

6.7 Bangladesh Telephone Shilpa Sangstha (TSS)

6.7.1 Rationale for Reform

Bangladesh Telephone Shilpa Sangstha (TSS), the state owned telecom equipment manufacturing entity, has experienced the most dramatic institutional pivot among PTD entities. Historically focused on telephone handset and exchange equipment production, TSS found its traditional product lines obsolete as digital switching, mobile communications, and IP based networks replaced analogue telephony. Rather than allowing the entity to continue a slow decline, the reform programme has reimagined TSS as an innovation enabling state owned platform, leveraging its manufacturing infrastructure, Hi Tech Park designation, and strategic partnerships to produce a diversified portfolio of digital products.

On 6 April 2025, TSS was designated a Hi Tech Park by the ICT Division of the Ministry of Posts, Telecommunications and Information Technology. This designation provides regulatory benefits including tax incentives, simplified licensing, and access to the Hi Tech Park ecosystem of investors, technology transfer partners, and skilled workforce pipelines. The designation is being operationalized through multiple strategic partnership agreements and manufacturing pilot programmes that aim to reduce Bangladesh's dependence on imported digital equipment.

6.7.2 Reform Initiatives and Current Status

Initiative

Current Status

Timeline

Expected Outcome

Software Incubation Centre

Established with strategic partner support; ICT related innovative ideas being converted into practical products; operations ongoing

Operational

Local software entrepreneurship; product development pipeline for government digital services

Edotco MoU for Battery Refurbishment

MoU signed 4 December 2025 between TSS and edotco Bangladesh Ltd; technician training commenced for lithium ion battery and rectifier repair and refurbishment centre

December 2026 (pilot year)

Circular economy revenue; reduced battery import dependency; skilled workforce development

Strategic Partnership Agreements (IT and IoT)

Two strategic partnership agreements signed 15 September 2025 with technology firms; production planning for computers, accessories, network devices, telecom devices, and IoT equipment underway

Ongoing (8 to 12 months)

Local assembly and production of low to mid tech digital products; import substitution

CCTV and Surveillance Equipment Manufacturing

Korean OEM partner (Ticon Systems Ltd) technical and investment support secured; project proposal approved by TSS board

December 2026

Domestic surveillance equipment production; reduced import dependency for security infrastructure

4G and 5G Dual Band WiFi Router Production

SKD and CKD assembly from ZTE and Huawei components; supply to PTD affiliated entities planned

December 2026

Local router assembly; supply chain security for government telecom entities

Low Tech Product Assembly (Set Top Boxes, Chargers, Electric Poles)

Strategic partners engaged for set top box, single charging port, mobile charger, and multipurpose electric pole production

8 to 12 months

Diversified product portfolio; employment generation; domestic market supply

Smart Electric Meter Production

Expatriate Bangladeshi scientists developing local IoT and LTE SIM based smart meter technology; production at existing TSS meter plant

December 2026

Import substitution for smart meters; Made in Bangladesh IoT product; energy sector modernization support

Hi Tech Park and Electric Bike Manufacturing

Hi Tech Park designation received 6 April 2025; Liban Industries Ltd application approved by HTPA for electric bike production at TSS premises

December 2026

Green mobility manufacturing; Hi Tech Park ecosystem activation; new revenue from facility leasing

6.7.3 Strategic Improvement Agenda

TSS's transformation from a defunct telephone manufacturing unit into an innovation enabling platform is conceptually compelling but faces significant execution risks. The primary risk is overextension: attempting to simultaneously launch software incubation, battery refurbishment, CCTV manufacturing, router assembly, smart meter production, and electric bike manufacturing may exceed the entity's management capacity and financial resources. A phased approach, prioritizing initiatives with the clearest revenue potential and lowest technology risk, is advisable.

TSS is pivoting toward high-demand digital accessories to capture the retail market and support tower infrastructure. This includes the domestic manufacturing of high-quality charging cables and power adapters. Furthermore, through strategic partnerships with infrastructure providers like edotco, TSS will manufacture and supply specialized hardware such as multipurpose electric poles and power management units for telecom towers, ensuring a "Made in Bangladesh" supply chain for critical network sites.

The battery refurbishment centre, developed through the edotco partnership, deserves particular emphasis. The telecom tower industry in Bangladesh and across South Asia faces an escalating challenge of lithium ion battery lifecycle management. With tens of thousands of tower sites requiring periodic battery replacement, a certified refurbishment facility reduces costs, addresses environmental disposal concerns, and generates recurring revenue. South Korea's battery recycling industry and Japan's circular economy policies for electronics provide frameworks for how this can evolve into a significant business line.

The smart electric meter initiative, leveraging IoT technology developed by expatriate Bangladeshi scientists, aligns directly with the national energy sector's modernization needs. The Bangladesh Energy Regulatory Commission (BERC) and electricity distribution companies have identified smart metering as a priority for reducing system losses and improving billing accuracy. If TSS can produce smart meters at competitive prices with adequate quality certification, it could capture a meaningful share of the domestic smart meter procurement market, currently dominated by imports from China and India. However, this requires rigorous testing, IEC standard compliance, and pilot deployments with distribution utilities before large scale procurement orders can be secured.

6.8 Bangladesh Cable Shilpa Limited (BCSL)

6.8.1 Rationale for Reform

Bangladesh Cable Shilpa Limited (BCSL, commonly referred to as BAKESHI) is the state owned cable manufacturing entity producing optical fiber cables, power cables, and related products. BCSL occupies a strategic position in the national infrastructure supply chain, providing the physical medium through which digital connectivity and power distribution are realized. In a context where fiber optic deployment is accelerating under national broadband expansion plans and power grid modernization is a priority under Bangladesh's energy strategy, BCSL's relevance has arguably never been greater.

The reform programme has focused on production capacity enhancement, product diversification, and market expansion beyond the traditional government procurement channel. The inter ministerial initiative to prioritize BCSL manufactured products in government infrastructure projects has already generated visible results in order volumes.

6.8.2 Reform Initiatives and Current Status

Initiative

Current Status

Timeline

Expected Outcome

Super Enamelled Copper Wire Plant

300 metric tonne annual capacity plant established; commercial production commenced

Operational

New product line; reduced import dependency for transformer and motor winding wire

Power Cable Capacity Expansion

International tender for 5 new technology machines opened 29 October 2025; technical evaluation completed; capacity to increase from 600 to 1,500 metric tonnes

October 2026

2.5 times capacity increase; enhanced ability to serve power distribution companies

LAN and Network Cable (CAT6 and CAT6A) Production

October 2026

Copper and Aluminium Rod Production Plant

June 2027

June 2026

Green Certification and Solar Panel Installation

July 2026

11 and 33 kV Underground Power Cable Production

Recycling Plant Activation

Scrap duct recycling plant fully activated; maximum waste reuse being ensured

Operational

Production cost reduction; waste minimization; circular economy contribution

6.8.3 Strategic Improvement Agenda

BCSL's production capacity expansion, particularly the planned increase from 600 to 1,500 metric tonnes in power cable output, positions the entity to capture a larger share of domestic infrastructure procurement. However, three strategic gaps merit attention. First, quality certification to international standards (IEC, ISO 9001, and product specific certifications) is essential to compete not only in domestic government procurement but also in potential export markets. Vietnam's cable manufacturing industry, which has successfully entered regional export markets through aggressive quality certification and competitive pricing, offers a relevant benchmark.

Second, the Green Certificate initiative and solar panel installation represent progressive environmental positioning, but should be accompanied by a comprehensive sustainability strategy including energy efficiency improvements in manufacturing processes, waste heat recovery, and water recycling. European cable manufacturers such as Prysmian Group and Nexans have established ESG reporting and sustainability targets that increasingly influence procurement decisions by multilateral development banks and international buyers.

Third, the 11 and 33 kV underground power cable production capability, once operational, will represent a qualitative leap in BCSL's product sophistication. Underground power distribution is expanding in Dhaka, Chattogram, and other major cities as urban density increases and overhead line hazards become less acceptable. BCSL should engage with DPDC, DESCO, BPDB, and other distribution utilities early to understand technical specifications and secure advance procurement commitments before the production facility is completed.

6.9 Cross Cutting Institutional Reform Priorities

Beyond the entity specific reforms detailed above, several cross cutting institutional priorities apply across all PTD entities and require coordinated action at the divisional level.

6.9.1 Board Governance and Appointment Reform

The PTD Whitepaper Task Force findings documented a pervasive pattern of governance capture, where political patronage influenced board appointments, executive selection, and procurement decisions across multiple entities. Restoring governance integrity requires that Boards of Directors across all SOEs be reconstituted to include a majority of independent members with demonstrated sectoral expertise. The PTD Secretary should not serve as Board Chair of any SOE, separating policy oversight from operational control. Selection committees for executive positions should include external experts and observers from the Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) or independent professional bodies. These measures align with the OECD Guidelines on Corporate Governance of State Owned Enterprises (2015) and the World Bank's Corporate Governance of State Owned Enterprises Toolkit.

6.9.2 Human Resource Digitization and Merit Based Progression

All PTD entities should implement centralized, digital Human Resource Management Information Systems (HRMIS) that ensure recruitment, promotion, and transfer decisions are transparently documented, rule based, and auditable. The post BTTB fragmentation of HR functions across BTCL, Teletalk, DoT, and other entities created overlapping mandates and enabled irregular appointments, ad hoc hiring, and post retirement reappointments without competitive processes. A unified digital HR framework, accessible to both internal management and external auditors, will institutionalize fairness and remove procedural ambiguity. Estonia's public sector HRMIS and Singapore's Whole of Government HR portal provide models for digital HR governance in the public sector.

6.9.3 Accountability, Oversight, and Audit Compliance

Comprehensive HR compliance audits by the CAG should be integrated into the regular audit cycle for all PTD entities, with findings submitted to Parliament to reinforce public accountability. An ex post regularization programme must review and rectify past irregular appointments through transparent procedures. Legal and disciplinary mechanisms should be fast tracked, with accused officers barred from retirement or transfer to evade proceedings. Secure whistleblower channels guaranteeing anonymity and protection against retaliation should be introduced across all entities. The completion of pending legal instruments, including the MOCSLA recruitment regulations and the clear definition of DoT BTRC functional boundaries, will eliminate structural ambiguity and prevent overlapping mandates.

6.9.4 Rebuilding the Telecommunication Cadre

The corporatization of BTTB dismantled the dedicated Telecommunication Cadre without creating an adequate replacement mechanism. The Department of Telecommunications (DoT), originally envisioned as the strategic anchor for sectoral leadership, has been structurally marginalized and rendered practically nonfunctional. Rebuilding a dedicated Telecommunication Cadre under DoT through competitive civil service recruitment will restore technical capacity and institutional continuity essential for managing emerging technologies including 5G, IoT, cybersecurity, and AI governance. This cadre will also ensure that PTD retains in-house technical expertise for policy formulation, project evaluation, and regulatory oversight, reducing dependence on external consultants and deputation based staffing.

6.9.5 Inter Entity Synergy and Resource Sharing

Several reform initiatives described in this chapter involve deliberate inter entity cooperation: BTCL's MVNO partnership with Teletalk, Teletalk's SIM distribution through Bangladesh Post, BCSL's cable supply to BTCL and power utilities, TSS's router production for PTD entities, and BSCL's Starlink reselling in partnership with telecom operators. These synergies should be formalized through structured inter entity service agreements with defined service levels, pricing mechanisms, dispute resolution procedures, and performance monitoring. A PTD level coordination body, meeting quarterly and reporting to the Secretary, should be established to oversee inter entity cooperation, resolve bottlenecks, and ensure that the collective reform agenda advances coherently rather than in institutional silos.

6.10 Chapter Summary

Chapter 6 presents a structured, entity by entity institutional reform programme for the ten organizations under the Posts and Telecommunications Division. The chapter diagnoses the institutional legacy of governance fragmentation, political patronage in appointments, and the disconnect between policy ambition and operational execution that the PTD Whitepaper Task Force documented in April 2025. Each entity section follows a consistent analytical framework: rationale for reform, current status of initiatives (drawn from official progress reports through January 2026), a strategic improvement agenda with international benchmarks, and measurable KPIs with defined timelines. BTRC's reform centres on restoring regulatory autonomy, operationalizing the Telecom Ordinance 2025, and transforming the Social Obligation Fund into an outcomes driven vehicle. BTCL's programme repositions the company from a legacy landline operator into a Digital Infrastructure Hub through TowerCo licensing, GPON outsourcing, the Hathazari Green Data Centre PPP, and the MVNO partnership with Teletalk. Teletalk's reform balances commercial competitiveness with sovereign communications obligations, anchored by the 2,300 site tower sharing agreement and phased 5G readiness. BSCPLC has reduced bandwidth prices by 30 to 50 percent and is pursuing dues recovery and capacity planning for next generation submarine cable systems. Bangladesh Post is undergoing the most multifaceted transformation, converting 1,000 Digital Centres into Nagorik Sheba Centres, building cold chain logistics capability, developing the National Address Management System, and modernizing postal ballot procedures. BSCL is pivoting from single satellite operation toward a diversified space services portfolio encompassing Starlink reselling, maritime AIS, cable television digitalization, and earth observation satellite planning. TSS has been redesignated as a Hi Tech Park and is pursuing battery refurbishment, router assembly, CCTV manufacturing, and smart meter production through strategic partnerships. BCSL is expanding power cable production capacity from 600 to 1,500 metric tonnes and diversifying into network cables and underground power cables. Five cross cutting priorities apply across all entities: board governance reconstitution with independent majorities, unified digital HRMIS implementation, CAG integrated accountability mechanisms, rebuilding the Telecommunication Cadre under the Department of Telecommunications, and formalizing inter entity synergies through structured service agreements overseen by a PTD level coordination body. The overarching institutional reform philosophy rests on three pillars: commercial viability to justify continued public ownership, governance integrity to eliminate patronage and restore meritocratic progression, and strategic complementarity to position state entities as essential infrastructure partners rather than ineffective competitors to private operators.



— End of Chapter 6 —

7. Project Reform

7.0 Introduction: The Project Governance Crisis

The Posts and Telecommunications Division (PTD) has, between January 2009 and August 2024, implemented or initiated 42 development projects across its affiliated entities, with a combined estimated value exceeding BDT 20,000 crore. These projects span fiber optic network expansion, submarine cable connectivity, satellite infrastructure, postal modernization, equipment manufacturing, and mobile network development. They have been funded through the Annual Development Programme (ADP), entity self financing, and the Social Obligation Fund (SOF). Yet the PTD Whitepaper Task Force, upon reviewing 18 priority projects representing 83 percent of total project value, found pervasive patterns of procurement irregularity, specification manipulation, cost inflation, inter project dependency failures, delayed completion, and in several cases, active Anti Corruption Commission (ACC) investigations.

These findings are not isolated incidents of individual misconduct. They reveal systemic weaknesses in the project governance architecture: the absence of mandatory feasibility studies for projects below BDT 50 crore, the lack of entity level master plans or strategic frameworks to guide project selection, chronic non submission or delayed submission of Project Completion Reports (PCRs), inadequate IMED (Implementation Monitoring and Evaluation Division) evaluation, and most critically, the structural exemption of SOF funded projects from ADP inclusion, IMED monitoring, and CAG audit scrutiny. The consequence has been a project pipeline that serves procurement cycles rather than strategic outcomes, where success is measured by expenditure rates rather than service delivery impact.

This chapter presents a comprehensive project reform framework designed to transform the PTD's project governance from its current state of fragmented accountability and opportunistic procurement into a disciplined, outcomes oriented, and strategically coherent system. The reform framework addresses five interconnected dimensions: the legacy project portfolio and its remediation, SOF governance reform, the new generation of strategically aligned projects initiated in 2025, the introduction of project governance safeguards, and the establishment of a project monitoring and evaluation architecture that ensures continuous accountability. International best practices from the United Kingdom's Infrastructure and Projects Authority (IPA), South Korea's Public Investment Management System (PIMAC), and Australia's Infrastructure Australia assessment framework inform the reform design.





7.1 Legacy Project Portfolio: Diagnosis and Remediation

7.1.1 Systemic Findings from the Task Force Review

The PTD Whitepaper Task Force's examination of 18 priority projects identified recurring governance failures that transcend individual entities. These systemic patterns must be understood as structural vulnerabilities requiring institutional remedies rather than merely disciplinary action against individual officers. The principal findings, drawn from the Task Force's documented analysis, are as follows.

Absence of Strategic Framework and Master Plans. Multiple implementing entities, including BTCL, lacked articulated vision statements, mission definitions, or master plans at the time projects were conceived. Projects were therefore designed in a strategic vacuum, without reference to long term institutional objectives or sectoral coordination. The consequence was that projects addressed immediate procurement opportunities rather than coherent infrastructure development sequences. The Task Force recommended that all entities formulate clearly articulated visions and missions accompanied by comprehensive master plans spanning short, medium, and long term horizons, grounded in an understanding of the evolving global telecom landscape.

Feasibility Study Failures. The Ministry of Planning's June 2022 guideline mandates comprehensive feasibility studies for all investment projects exceeding BDT 50 crore. Despite this requirement, the Task Force found instances where feasibility studies were either not conducted, conducted superficially, or their findings disregarded during project implementation. In the case of BTCL's Optical Fiber Transmission Network Development for 5G Readiness Project (approved at BDT 1,059.10 crore by ECNEC in February 2022), the tender specifications for the GD 1 package were altered to require 400G/100G equipment, deviating from the 100G/10G specifications recommended in the approved feasibility study and Development Project Proposal (DPP). This unauthorized modification constituted a violation of Public Procurement Rules (PPR) and was subsequently flagged by the ACC for investigation.

Inter Project Dependency Failures. The 5G Readiness Project and BTCL's Internet Protocol (IP) Network Development and Expansion Project were technically interdependent: the IP network's servers required the DWDM transmission network to operate. Yet neither project's DPP formally documented this dependency, and the GD 1 specification alteration in one project effectively handicapped the other. The IP network project was reported to be 70 to 80 percent complete but rendered non functional because the DWDM network it depended upon was stalled by procurement disputes and ACC investigation. This situation, where substantial public expenditure on one project is rendered valueless by the failure of a linked project, exemplifies the absence of a centralized coordination mechanism under the Head of the Procuring Entity (HOPE).

Procurement Irregularity and Political Interference. The Task Force documented instances of political interference in procurement processes across multiple entities. In the 5G Readiness Project, documentary evidence indicated that both a former Minister and a former Secretary sought to influence the tender process in favour of different bidding companies (ZTE Corporation and Huawei Technologies respectively). The BTCL board's 213th meeting revealed that the proposed 126 terabyte infrastructure capacity far exceeded projected demand of 26 terabytes by 2030, with only half the capacity expected to be utilized even by 2040. Industry experts described this capacity over specification as indicative of deliberate cost inflation. The project, valued at BDT 463 crore for the GD 1 tender package alone, is now under ACC investigation.

Delayed Project Completion Reports and IMED Evaluation. The Task Force found that Project Completion Reports were frequently submitted late (in one case, 18 months after completion) or not submitted at all for projects still nominally in implementation phase years after their original completion dates. IMED impact evaluations were either pending or, in the case of self financed projects, not required under existing rules. This evaluation gap means that the government has no systematic evidence base for assessing whether project expenditure achieved its intended outcomes.

7.1.2 Remediation Framework for Legacy Projects

Legacy projects under active ACC investigation must be allowed to proceed through the investigative and, where applicable, judicial process without executive interference. The ACC's June 2025 communication explicitly warned that proceeding with procurement activities in projects where preliminary evidence of PPA 2006 and PPR 2008 violations has been found may constitute a legal violation and that related expenditure would likely be deemed unlawful. Regardless of pressures to advance projects citing national interest or technological urgency, the integrity of the anti corruption process must be safeguarded.

For legacy projects not under investigation but suffering from delays or scope problems, a structured Project Health Assessment should be conducted by an independent technical team, reviewing each project against its original DPP, feasibility study recommendations, procurement compliance, physical progress, and financial expenditure patterns. Projects that are salvageable should receive revised DPPs with realistic timelines, updated cost estimates reflecting currency depreciation and technology evolution, and clearly defined completion milestones. Projects that have been rendered technically obsolete by the passage of time or overtaken by alternative solutions should be formally cancelled, with Project Completion Reports submitted and lessons learned documented.

The United Kingdom's Infrastructure and Projects Authority conducts annual reviews of major government projects, assigning a Delivery Confidence Assessment (rated green, amber, or red) that is published publicly. Bangladesh should adopt a similar mechanism for PTD projects, with the PTD Secretary commissioning quarterly project health assessments and publishing summary findings to restore public confidence in project governance.





7.2 Social Obligation Fund (SOF) Governance Reform

7.2.1 Diagnosis of SOF Governance Failures

The Social Obligation Fund, established in November 2011 under the Telecommunication Act (Amendment) 2010, requires telecom operators to contribute 1 percent of their audited gross annual revenue. By January 2025, cumulative contributions and interest had reached approximately BDT 3,559 crore. The SOF's intended purpose, financing telecommunications and ICT development in underserved areas including remote coastal zones, river islands, hilly regions, and communities with limited commercial viability, is critically important for digital inclusion. However, the Task Force's review revealed that the SOF has been undermined by five structural governance failures.

First, non compliance with mandatory contributions. While major mobile operators contributed more consistently, the majority of ISPs, district level operators, and telecom value added service (TVAS) licensees either remitted irregularly or did not remit at all. BTRC's weak enforcement and ad hoc waiver practices eroded compliance norms, reducing the effective funding base and creating an uneven distribution of the contribution burden.

Second, large idle balances and delayed deployment. Approximately 35 percent of total funds raised remained idle due to regulatory and institutional bottlenecks. Large tranches of money were left undeployed for years, violating the fiduciary expectation that collected public monies be actively applied for their intended purpose. When spending was eventually hurried under political pressure, it created space for poor procurement and weak project design.

Third, exclusion from national oversight frameworks. SOF projects are not included in the Annual Development Programme (ADP). Consequently, their progress is not discussed in ADP coordination meetings, they are not monitored by IMED, and they are not audited by the Audit Directorate. The approval powers of the SOF Council, chaired by the Minister/State Minister in charge, are not harmonized with ECNEC's authority, and no upper cost limit is set for ministerial approvals. This structural exemption created an accountability vacuum that facilitated procurement shortcuts and ad hoc decisions.

Fourth, fragmented accountability architecture. The SOF design separates revenue collection (BTRC) from project selection and implementation (SOF Management Committee and diverse PTD agencies). No single entity carries the full chain of accountability for impact. BTRC can collect but not compel performance, while implementing agencies execute projects without sustained BTRC oversight. Ministerial approval signatures bypass routine cross checks, creating a fragmented accountability trail.

Fifth, absence of central monitoring and evaluation. BTRC maintains no dedicated M&E unit for SOF nor a unified asset and performance dashboard. Field inspections reported routers and hotspots as installed but not operational, with no central mechanism to capture status changes in real time. The institutional culture privileged procurement milestones over sustained operations and actual service delivery.

7.2.2 SOF Reform Architecture

The reform of SOF governance requires structural changes across five dimensions, drawing on international best practices from India's Universal Service Obligation Fund (USOF), Malaysia's Universal Service Provision (USP) Fund, and Colombia's Fondo de Tecnologias de la Informacion y las Comunicaciones (FONTIC).

Inclusion in National Oversight Frameworks. All SOF funded projects must be included in a dedicated annex to the ADP, subjecting them to the same IMED monitoring, ADP coordination review, and CAG audit requirements as government funded development projects. The SOF Council's approval powers must be harmonized with ECNEC's authority, with projects exceeding BDT 50 crore requiring ECNEC clearance regardless of funding source. India's USOF, while administered by the Department of Telecommunications, operates within the framework of the national budget process and is subject to CAG audit, providing a precedent for this integration.

Independent Project Selection and Prioritization. A formal project invitation and evaluation process must replace the current ad hoc system where neither a dedicated proposal invitation committee nor clear prioritization criteria exist. SOF project proposals should be evaluated by an independent technical committee against published criteria including: geographic targeting accuracy (underserved area verification), cost effectiveness analysis, sustainability planning (maintenance, operational continuity), and alignment with PTD strategic priorities. Colombia's FONTIC employs a competitive project selection process with published evaluation criteria and independent technical assessment, which has significantly improved fund deployment effectiveness.

Unified Digital SOF Management Platform. BTRC must establish a dedicated M&E unit for SOF and commission a digital SOF management platform that provides real time asset tracking, functionality monitoring, usage analytics, and public reporting. This platform should integrate with BTRC's existing Automated Spectrum Management System (ASMS) and Telecom Monitoring System (TMS) to verify that SOF funded connectivity infrastructure is actually operational. Asset registers, functionality reports, and usage metrics must be consolidated into a single dashboard accessible to auditors, policymakers, and the public.

Enforcement of Operator Contributions. BTRC must implement automated revenue verification linked to operator audit reports, with graduated penalties for late or incomplete remittance. Licensees in persistent default should face licensing consequences, including suspension of spectrum assignment or license renewal conditionality. Malaysia's USP Fund, governed under Section 204 of the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998, imposes a mandatory 6 percent levy on licensees with revenue exceeding RM 2 million, with enforcement backed by licensing sanctions.

Performance Based Project Monitoring. SOF project contracts must include service level agreements (SLAs) with measurable performance indicators: connectivity uptime, user registration rates, training completion rates, and operational sustainability over a defined maintenance period. Payment tranches should be linked to verified performance milestones rather than procurement completion alone. Projects that fail to meet SLA benchmarks within a grace period should trigger remedial action, including contract termination with recovery provisions.

7.2.3 SOF Reform KPI Framework

Reform Area

KPI

7.3 New Generation Projects: Strategically Aligned Initiatives (2025 Onwards)

The reform programme initiated in 2025 has introduced a new generation of projects that, unlike many of their predecessors, are embedded within a coherent strategic framework defined by the PTD's digital transformation agenda. These projects are distinguished by their alignment with entity transformation plans, emphasis on commercial sustainability, cross entity coordination, and citizen facing service delivery outcomes. This section catalogues the principal new projects across PTD entities, assessing their strategic coherence and implementation readiness.

7.3.1 Nagorik Sheba Bangladesh: The Flagship Citizen Service Platform

Nagorik Sheba Bangladesh represents the most ambitious cross institutional project in the PTD reform portfolio. Launched on 26 May 2025 by Chief Adviser Professor Muhammad Yunus at the State Guest House Jamuna, the initiative transforms unused government facilities, including post offices and exchange buildings, into modern citizen service centres operated by trained local entrepreneurs. The programme operates on a zero cost to public money model, with centres managed under a sustainable social business framework through public private community partnership, where licensed entrepreneurs provide services for a fee while paying rent for government space.

The pilot phase established centres at Gulshan, Uttara, and Nilkhet in Dhaka, with subsequent expansion to Banasree, Mohammadpur, and Gulistan by October 2025. By November 2025, a total of 1,122 Nagorik Sheba Centres were reported operational, integrating over 41 services with 453 services available through the MyGov platform. More than 2,000 entrepreneurs nationwide were trained, with a rigorous selection process initially certifying 100 entrepreneurs (50 women and 50 men) from an initial pool of 200 applicants. A daylong training workshop held on 13 November 2025 at Dak Bhaban brought together representative entrepreneurs from all 64 districts for standardized training on the platform, digital security, cyber security, and data protection.

Nagorik Sheba is structurally significant for PTD because it operationalizes three simultaneous transformation objectives. For Bangladesh Post, it converts the postal network from a declining mail delivery service into a citizen service delivery platform, generating revenue from service facilitation fees and revitalizing physical infrastructure. For BTCL, the Nagorik Sheba centres established in former telephone exchange buildings (Gulshan, Uttara, Nilkhet) demonstrate the commercial repurposing of underutilized telecom real estate. For the Digital Transformation Strategy as a whole, Nagorik Sheba serves as the last mile physical manifestation of the Bangladesh National Digital Architecture (BNDIA), acting as a decentralized interoperability hub that bridges the digital divide through trained human intermediaries who navigate complex digital services on behalf of citizens with limited literacy or technical skills.

Milestone

Status (January 2026)

Target

KPI
