7.3.2 BTCL Infrastructure Modernization Projects

BTCL's new project pipeline reflects the entity's strategic repositioning from a legacy landline operator to a digital infrastructure hub. Three major projects are in various stages of approval and implementation.

Mohakhali Telecom and Software Technology Complex (BTSTC)

The BTSTC project proposes to develop BTCL's prime Mohakhali real estate into a technology innovation hub and software industry cluster. The Pre Feasibility Study (PFS) was restructured following Planning Commission review on 28 October 2025, with inputs provided for RADP inclusion in FY 2025 to 2026. A verification committee meeting was held on 10 November 2025 to advance the PFS toward full DPP preparation. The project represents a significant departure from BTCL's historical project approach: rather than procuring telecommunications equipment, it seeks to develop BTCL's most valuable urban asset into a revenue generating technology complex. The strategic logic mirrors the Nairobi Innovation Hub development in Kenya and Cyberjaya in Malaysia, where state owned land was leveraged for technology cluster development.

District Telecom Infrastructure Modernization (34 Districts, Phase 1)

This project aims to modernize BTCL infrastructure across 34 districts to provide co location services and updated connectivity infrastructure. The project was included in the ADP green list for FY 2025 to 2026, with a Finance Division letter dispatched on 3 November 2025 seeking clarification on the GOB financing modality. The Tejgaon Telecom Tower and Innovation Complex, a related initiative, received BTCL Board approval at the 240th meeting on 25 November 2025 for BTCL's own financing. The feasibility study for the 34 district Phase 1 programme is progressing, and the project is designed to complement the Nagorik Sheba programme by ensuring that district level facilities have the infrastructure required to serve as technology service hubs.

Green Data Centre (Hathazari, Chattogram)

The Hathazari Green Data Centre project, anchored by an MoU signed on 26 January 2025 among BTCL, PTD, the PPP Authority, and the Asian Development Bank, represents BTCL's entry into the data centre market. The project is structured as a public private partnership, with ADB providing technical advisory support for a Tier III specification facility. ERP consultant procurement is ongoing to support BTCL's internal readiness for data centre operations. Given Bangladesh's rapidly growing data hosting demand, driven by financial sector compliance requirements, e government expansion, and private cloud adoption, the timing is strategically sound. However, execution risk remains elevated given BTCL's limited experience with PPP structures and data centre operations, necessitating the operational partnerships and capacity building measures identified in Chapter 6.

7.3.3 Teletalk Network Expansion Projects

Teletalk has three new Development Project Proposals (DPPs) prepared and submitted for ADP green list consideration, targeting April 2026 Planning Commission approval. These projects address three strategic gaps in Teletalk's coverage: remote area connectivity in the Chittagong Hill Tracts and river island communities, 4G coverage extension at public universities, and highway corridor 4G deployment for safety communications. The projects are complemented by the ongoing 4G expansion programme, which has already activated 1,699 new 4G BTS sites and increased daily data consumption by approximately 20 percent (from 354 TB to 464 TB). The tower sharing agreement for 2,300 sites, signed in November 2025, provides the infrastructure foundation for accelerated network expansion without the prohibitive capital expenditure of own tower construction.

Critically, these projects reflect lessons learned from Teletalk's previous ADP project history. The DPPs include detailed demand justification, phased implementation schedules, and defined handover criteria that address the Task Force's concerns about project completion reporting and impact evaluation. The integration of SIM distribution through post offices and the MVNO partnership with BTCL provides cross entity revenue sharing models that strengthen the business case for continued public investment in Teletalk's infrastructure.

7.3.4 Bangladesh Post Modernization Projects

Bangladesh Post's project pipeline encompasses three transformative initiatives, each grounded in the broader postal reform strategy.

National Address Management System (NAMS)

The NAMS project, conditionally approved following a Project Evaluation Committee (PEC) meeting in 2025, establishes a standardized, geocoded addressing system for Bangladesh. Under the proposed design, NAMS introduces a National Address Identifier (NAID), a 12 character alphanumeric code that serves as a permanent digital key for any location in Bangladesh. The system operates as a foundational layer of Digital Public Infrastructure, with Bangladesh Post serving as the primary custodian of the address registry, leveraging its nationwide field force for verification and data maintenance. The expected outcomes by 2030 include 100 percent household and business NAID assignment, elimination of addressing ambiguity in emergency response routing, and a projected 30 percent reduction in e commerce logistics costs through elimination of failed deliveries. NAMS directly operationalizes the National Digital Transformation Strategy's vision of a standardized delivery infrastructure for digital commerce and government service delivery.

Central Logistics Tracking Platform (CLTP)

The CLTP, in its onboarding phase since March 2025, creates an integrated digital logistics platform connecting Bangladesh Post with private courier companies, e commerce platforms, payment gateways, and regulatory bodies. Stakeholder consultations have been completed, and the platform's architecture is being finalized to support real time parcel tracking, interoperability with commercial logistics networks, and API based data sharing. The CLTP, combined with the Domestic Mail Monitoring Software (DMS) API sharing initiative and the planned Reverse Logistics Middleware System, positions Bangladesh Post as a national logistics backbone capable of serving the growing e commerce market while leveraging its unmatched physical reach into rural areas.

Cold Chain Logistics Expansion

The installation of 14 chiller chambers and 84 deep freezers under the ongoing postal development project (2024 to 2025) establishes Bangladesh Post's entry into cold chain logistics. The successful pilot transport of 65 tonnes of mangoes from Rajshahi, Chapainawabganj, Naogaon, and Satkhira to Dhaka during the May to July 2025 season demonstrates commercial viability and social impact. The expansion of cold chain capacity to additional post offices, particularly during seasonal fruit and vegetable harvests, addresses both the 30 to 40 percent post harvest loss problem in Bangladesh's agricultural sector and creates a sustainable revenue stream for the postal service. Thailand Post's farm to consumer cold chain model and India Post's Kisan Rin Portal (connecting farmers directly to urban markets) offer operational benchmarks for scaling this initiative.

7.3.5 BSCL Space and Satellite Projects

BSCL's project pipeline encompasses the Maritime Automatic Identification System (AIS 2.0) project, with preliminary specifications prepared and a financing proposal submitted to BTRC's SOF Committee (target completion June 2026), the cable television network digitalization initiative approved in principle by the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting, and the Earth Observation (EO) satellite planning initiative currently under Managing Director review. The AIS 2.0 project, designed to provide satellite based vessel tracking in Bangladesh's territorial waters, has direct implications for maritime safety, Blue Economy development, and border security. Its financing through the SOF represents an appropriate use of the fund's mandate for infrastructure development in underserved or strategically critical domains.

The cable television digitalization project, proposing BSCL as the central digital platform with standardized set top box specifications and transparent TRP (Television Rating Points) measurement, carries implications beyond BSCL's institutional boundaries. If implemented effectively, it will modernize a cable industry currently estimated at over 20 million households, establish auditable subscriber and viewership data, and create a transparent advertising market. India's TRAI mandated digital migration, implemented in phases from 2012 onwards, provides both positive lessons (dramatic increase in revenue transparency and tax compliance) and cautionary examples (consumer disruption during rapid forced migration) that BSCL should study carefully.

7.3.6 BCSL and TSS Manufacturing Projects

The manufacturing entities' project pipelines, while smaller in individual scale, collectively address a critical strategic gap: Bangladesh's near total dependence on imported telecommunications and digital equipment. BCSL's power cable capacity expansion (from 600 to 1,500 metric tonnes), LAN/Network cable (CAT6/CAT6A) production facility, and 11/33 kV underground power cable feasibility study represent progressive capacity building in physical infrastructure manufacturing. TSS's diversification into CCTV and surveillance equipment (with Korean OEM support), 4G/5G WiFi router assembly (from ZTE/Huawei components), and smart electric meter production (using IoT technology from expatriate Bangladeshi scientists) represents a technology transfer model that, if successfully implemented, reduces import dependency while building domestic manufacturing capability.

These manufacturing projects are strategically important precisely because they are modest in ambition. Unlike the legacy mega projects that suffered from over specification and procurement capture, the manufacturing initiatives are structured as phased expansions with identifiable revenue streams, specific customer bases (power utilities, government procurement, telecom operators), and manageable technology risk. The critical success factor is quality certification: without IEC, ISO, and product specific certifications, domestically manufactured products will face resistance in both government procurement and commercial markets. Vietnam's experience in building a cable and electronics manufacturing export industry through systematic quality certification and competitive pricing provides a roadmap that BCSL and TSS should follow.





7.4 Project Governance Safeguards: Preventing Recurrence

The lessons from legacy project failures must be institutionalized through binding project governance safeguards that apply to all future PTD projects, regardless of funding source. The following safeguards are proposed for adoption through PTD administrative order and, where necessary, through amendments to the SOF Guidelines and entity procurement manuals.

7.4.1 Mandatory Feasibility Study and Independent Review

All PTD projects exceeding BDT 25 crore must undergo a comprehensive third party feasibility study by a qualified, independent team selected through competitive procurement. The current BDT 50 crore threshold established by the Ministry of Planning is insufficient for the telecom sector, where technology cycles are shorter and cost inflation risk is higher. Feasibility studies must include demand analysis validated by independent market data, technology options appraisal with life cycle costing, risk assessment (including technology obsolescence, currency fluctuation, and procurement timeline risks), and a clear statement of assumptions that can be verified during implementation. South Korea's PIMAC (Public Investment Management Center), which conducts independent feasibility studies for all public projects exceeding 50 billion won (approximately BDT 340 crore), provides a rigorous model. PIMAC's studies include benefit cost analysis, policy relevance assessment, and regional balance considerations, with a rejection rate of approximately 50 percent, demonstrating genuine gatekeeping rather than rubber stamping.

7.4.2 DPP Integrity and Change Control

Once a Development Project Proposal (DPP) is approved, any material change in project scope, specifications, cost, or timeline must undergo formal revision through the same approval channel that authorized the original DPP. The Task Force's finding that tender specifications were altered without DPP correction, as occurred in the 5G Readiness Project's GD 1 package, constitutes a fundamental governance breach. A formal Change Control Board, chaired by the HOPE and including representation from the Ministry of Planning and the relevant sector division of the Planning Commission, must authorize any deviation from approved specifications. Unauthorized specification changes should be treated as prima facie procurement rule violations, triggering automatic referral to the CAG and, where warranted, to the ACC.

7.4.3 Inter Project Coordination Mechanism

All projects within a single entity or with technical dependencies across entities must be mapped in a Project Dependency Register maintained by the PTD's project monitoring cell. The IP Network and 5G Readiness fiasco demonstrates the consequences of implementing interdependent projects without formal coordination. Before any project tender is floated, the HOPE must certify that all technical dependencies have been identified, that linked projects are on track, and that specifications are mutually compatible. Where dependencies involve different entities (for example, BTCL fiber expansion feeding Teletalk coverage deployment), formal inter entity coordination protocols must be established, including shared milestone reporting and escalation mechanisms for dependency failures.

7.4.4 Procurement Transparency and Anti Interference Protocols

The documented political interference in BTCL's 5G Readiness tender, where both a Minister and a Secretary sought to influence the process in favour of different bidders, represents a governance failure that must be addressed through institutional design rather than merely individual accountability. The following protocols are proposed. All communications between political office holders (Ministers, State Ministers, Special Assistants) and procuring entities regarding specific tender processes must be formally documented and disclosed to the relevant review authority. The Head of the Procuring Entity (HOPE) must submit a sworn declaration at each procurement milestone confirming that no external direction has been received that conflicts with PPR requirements. Tender Evaluation Committee (TEC) membership must include at least one external expert nominated from a panel maintained by the Institution of Engineers, Bangladesh or an equivalent professional body, ensuring independent technical judgment. Technical evaluation reports must be made available to all Board members in advance of any board meeting where procurement decisions are discussed, addressing the practice identified by the Task Force where external board members were denied access to evaluation reports.

7.4.5 Mandatory Project Completion Reporting and Impact Evaluation

Project Completion Reports (PCRs) must be submitted within 90 days of project completion or termination, with the responsible Project Director personally accountable for timely submission. Late PCR submission should be reflected in the Project Director's Annual Performance Assessment, with delays exceeding six months triggering administrative inquiry. IMED impact evaluations must be commissioned within 12 months of project completion, regardless of funding source. For self financed projects that currently fall outside IMED's mandate, the entity's own internal audit function must conduct an equivalent evaluation, with findings submitted to the PTD Secretary and the CAG. Australia's Infrastructure Australia mandates post completion reviews for all nationally significant infrastructure projects, with published assessment reports that inform future project design. This practice of transparent, published evaluation should be adopted for all PTD projects exceeding BDT 100 crore.





7.5 Project Monitoring and Evaluation Architecture

7.5.1 PTD Project Monitoring Cell

A dedicated PTD Project Monitoring Cell should be established within the Division, staffed by officers with project management qualifications and reporting directly to the PTD Secretary. The Cell's mandate would encompass maintaining a consolidated project portfolio dashboard covering all ADP, self financed, and SOF funded projects; conducting quarterly project health assessments using a standardized delivery confidence methodology; maintaining the Project Dependency Register for inter project coordination; tracking PCR submission compliance and IMED evaluation scheduling; and preparing semi annual project portfolio reports for submission to the Secretary and, through the Secretary, to the Chief Adviser's Office. The United Kingdom's Infrastructure and Projects Authority's Government Major Projects Portfolio (GMPP) reporting system provides a proven template for this function. Singapore's Ministry of Finance also maintains a centralized project monitoring function through its Centre for Strategic Futures, which conducts prospective evaluations of major public investments.

7.5.2 Digital Project Management Information System

The project monitoring function must be supported by a digital Project Management Information System (PMIS) that integrates project planning, procurement tracking, financial management, milestone monitoring, and evaluation data in a single platform. The PMIS should be accessible to implementing entities, PTD, the Ministry of Planning, IMED, and the CAG, with role based access controls ensuring appropriate information sharing while maintaining procurement confidentiality where legally required. India's Pragati (Pro Active Governance and Timely Implementation) platform, which enables the Prime Minister's Office to monitor infrastructure project progress across all central government ministries through a unified digital dashboard, demonstrates the transformative potential of centralized digital project monitoring.

7.5.3 Public Accountability and Transparency

Project information, including DPP summaries, procurement timelines, physical and financial progress, and completed evaluation reports, should be published on the PTD website in a machine readable format. This transparency measure serves multiple purposes: it enables parliamentary oversight, facilitates civil society monitoring (including by organizations such as Transparency International Bangladesh that have previously flagged PTD project irregularities), provides data for academic research on project governance, and creates reputational incentives for implementing entities to maintain performance standards. The Philippines' Department of Budget and Management publishes project level ADP data through its Open Data Portal, and Rwanda's RPPA (Rwanda Public Procurement Authority) publishes procurement data in real time through its e Procurement system. Bangladesh should adopt similar practices, beginning with PTD as a pilot division.

7.6 Chapter Summary

Chapter 7 addresses the systemic project governance crisis within the Posts and Telecommunications Division, where 42 development projects valued at over BDT 20,000 crore were implemented between 2009 and 2024 with pervasive patterns of procurement irregularity, specification manipulation, cost inflation, and delayed completion. The chapter is organized across five reform dimensions. First, the legacy project portfolio diagnosis documents the Task Force's principal findings: absence of entity level master plans, feasibility study failures (including the unauthorized specification alteration in BTCL's 5G Readiness Project from 100G/10G to 400G/100G equipment), inter project dependency breakdowns that rendered substantial expenditure valueless, documented political interference in procurement by both a former Minister and a former Secretary, and chronic non submission of Project Completion Reports. Second, the Social Obligation Fund governance reform addresses five structural failures including non compliance with mandatory contributions, large idle balances, exclusion from ADP and IMED oversight, fragmented accountability, and absence of monitoring and evaluation, proposing ADP annex integration, independent project selection, a unified digital SOF management platform, automated contribution enforcement, and performance based project monitoring. Third, the new generation of strategically aligned projects initiated in 2025 is catalogued, led by the Nagorik Sheba Bangladesh programme (1,122 centres operational by November 2025, targeting 8,000 by 2030), BTCL's Mohakhali Technology Complex and Hathazari Green Data Centre, Teletalk's remote area and highway corridor coverage projects, Bangladesh Post's NAMS and CLTP platforms, BSCL's maritime AIS and cable TV digitalization initiatives, and BCSL and TSS manufacturing expansions. Fourth, binding project governance safeguards are proposed: mandatory independent feasibility studies for all projects above BDT 25 crore, formal DPP change control boards, a Project Dependency Register for inter project coordination, procurement anti interference protocols requiring sworn HOPE declarations and external TEC membership, and mandatory PCR submission within 90 days with IMED impact evaluation within 12 months. Fifth, the project monitoring and evaluation architecture establishes a dedicated PTD Project Monitoring Cell, a digital Project Management Information System accessible to all oversight bodies, and public transparency through machine readable project data publication. International frameworks from the United Kingdom's Infrastructure and Projects Authority, South Korea's PIMAC, Australia's Infrastructure Australia, India's Pragati platform, and the Philippines' Open Data Portal inform the reform design throughout.



— End of Chapter 7 —

8. Strategic and Governance Reforms

8.0 Introduction

The preceding chapters have diagnosed the specific institutional, project, and infrastructure failures that have constrained the posts and telecommunications sector. This chapter turns from diagnosis to architecture. It sets out the strategic and governance reforms necessary to create a regulatory and policy environment capable of sustaining the transformation agenda across electoral cycles, leadership transitions, and technological shifts. While Chapter 6 addressed the internal restructuring of PTD entities and Chapter 7 reformed the project management ecosystem, this chapter operates at a higher level of abstraction: the rules, institutions, and accountability frameworks that govern the entire sector.

The reform imperatives are substantial. The Task Force review documented an ecosystem in which regulatory independence existed in statute but not in practice, where spectrum policy functioned as a revenue extraction mechanism rather than an enabler of connectivity, where over 3,000 licences across 23 categories created artificial barriers to competition and innovation, and where consumer protection, data governance, and cybersecurity frameworks remained either absent or unenforceable. The July to August 2024 internet shutdown, in which BTRC was made an instrument of executive overreach in ordering a complete nationwide connectivity blackout, crystallized the governance deficit at the highest level.

Significant reform has already commenced. The Bangladesh Telecommunication (Amendment) Ordinance 2025, approved by the Advisory Council in December 2025, permanently prohibits internet and telecom service shutdowns, restores BTRC operational autonomy, and establishes a parliamentary accountability committee to oversee regulatory performance. The Telecommunications Network and Licensing Policy 2025, gazetted on 22 September 2025, replaces the International Long Distance Telecommunication Service (ILDTS) Policy 2010 and consolidates over a dozen fragmented licence categories into four technology neutral categories. Starlink received its operating licence in April 2025 and launched commercial service on 20 May 2025, representing the fastest Non-Geostationary Satellite Orbit (NGSO) licence issuance in Bangladesh's regulatory history.

These are foundational but incomplete actions. Enacting an ordinance or gazetting a policy is necessary but insufficient. The challenge is implementation, institutional embedding, and sustained governance discipline. This chapter provides the detailed reform architecture required to convert legislative intent into operational reality, organized across eleven interconnected reform domains: regulatory architecture, market design, spectrum governance, digital regulatory modernization, investment climate, consumer protection, data governance and cybersecurity, digital inclusion, emerging technology governance, sustainability, and evidence based policymaking.



8.1 Regulatory Architecture Reform

8.1.1 The Governance Deficit

BTRC was established under the Bangladesh Telecommunication Act 2001 as an independent regulator. However, its operational autonomy has been progressively eroded through amendments, ministerial directives, and informal practices. The 2010 amendment framework transferred critical decision making authority, including licensing, tariff setting, and spectrum allocation, to the Ministry, reducing BTRC to a de facto executing agency for ministerial decisions. The Task Force documented that regulatory decisions were routinely subject to ad hoc instructions, ministerial intervention, or political influence rather than rule based governance. This dependency model stands in stark contrast to the standards articulated by the International Telecommunication Union, the OECD Principles for the Governance of Regulators, and the European Electronic Communications Code, all of which mandate clear institutional separation between policy formulation and regulatory execution.

Simultaneously, the Department of Telecommunications (DoT) lacked a well defined regulatory mandate, rendering it largely inactive despite its statutory existence. The resulting institutional vacuum created overlapping authorities with unclear accountability, where neither BTRC nor DoT possessed the mandate, capacity, or political authority to drive coherent sectoral reform.

8.1.2 The Amended Ordinance Framework

The Bangladesh Telecommunication (Amendment) Ordinance 2025 represents the most significant governance restructuring since the original 2001 Act. Its principal provisions establish the following architecture.

Restored Regulatory Autonomy. The Ordinance returns operational decision making authority to BTRC. All licensing authority other than nationally significant licences requiring independent study has been returned to the Commission. This reverses the centralization imposed by the 2010 amendments and aligns Bangladesh with the FCC, Ofcom, and TRAI models where the regulator possesses independent licensing and enforcement authority.

Prohibition of Internet Shutdowns. Section 97 permanently eliminates the scope for nationwide or localized internet shutdowns by administrative order. This directly addresses the July to August 2024 shutdown and provides statutory protection against future connectivity disruption. Bangladesh joins a small group of nations, including India following its Supreme Court ruling in Anuradha Bhasin v. Union of India, that have institutionalized legal protections against internet shutdowns.

Parliamentary Accountability Committee. A new accountability committee, chaired by the head of the relevant parliamentary standing committee, has been established to oversee regulatory performance. This introduces a legislative accountability layer that did not previously exist, modelled on the relationship between the UK Parliament's Select Committee on Culture, Media and Sport and Ofcom.

Privacy Protections. The Ordinance strengthens citizens' privacy protections in alignment with international standards established by the United Nations and the ITU, narrowing the scope of punitive action to direct incitement to violence in line with the Cyber Safety Ordinance 2025.

Investment Facilitation. Licence processing timelines have been reduced and high and repetitive penalties have been scaled back, measures designed to encourage investment in the telecom sector and reduce regulatory friction.

8.1.3 DoT and BTRC Mandate Clarification

The Ordinance framework must be operationalized through a formal Cabinet or Government Order that specifies the functional boundary between DoT and BTRC. Drawing from the Task Force recommendations and international best practice, the mandate split should be structured as follows.

Department of Telecommunications (DoT): Policy formulation and advisory functions, strategic sector planning, inter ministerial coordination on digital economy matters, oversight of state owned enterprise performance, and coordination with Planning Commission on ADP projects. DoT should serve as the policy brain of the sector, responsible for long term strategic direction without involvement in day to day regulatory decisions.

BTRC: Licensing and guideline formulation, spectrum management and allocation, technical standards and enforcement, consumer protection and quality of service monitoring, market analysis and competition oversight. BTRC retains the full operational toolkit of a modern regulator.

Revenue Collection: The Task Force recommends removing revenue collection responsibility from BTRC and transferring it to the Government's appropriate financial authorities. This eliminates the inherent conflict of interest where the regulator responsible for sectoral development simultaneously extracts maximum revenue from the entities it regulates.

A joint steering committee with a public facing tracker should monitor inter agency coordination between DoT and BTRC and ensure timely implementation of decisions. The Government Order should be published within 90 days of this Strategy's adoption and the steering committee should hold its first meeting within 30 days of publication.

International benchmark: Australia's separation between the Department of Infrastructure, Transport, Regional Development, Communications and the Arts (policy) and the Australian Communications and Media Authority (regulation) provides a comparable institutional architecture where policy direction and regulatory execution operate through clearly defined mandates with formal coordination mechanisms.

8.1.4 KPI Framework: Regulatory Architecture

Indicator

Baseline (2025)

Target (2027)

Target (2030)

8.2 Market Design and Licensing Reform

8.2.1 The Legacy Licensing Problem

As of early 2025, BTRC had issued over 3,000 licences across approximately 23 categories, many overlapping in functionality and creating artificial silos in service provision. The fragmented regime compelled Mobile Network Operators to maintain multiple licences for converged services, resulting in increased compliance costs, duplicated infrastructure investments, and slower rollout of innovative solutions. The Task Force documented how the proliferation of licence categories, including International Gateway (IGW), International Internet Gateway (IIG), Interconnection Exchange (ICX), National Internet Exchange (NIX), and Internet Service Provider (ISP) categories, fostered rent seeking, anti competitive cartel formation, and regulatory capture. The IGW Operators Forum (IOF), formed with BTRC approval in 2015, exemplified this failure: a two tier system that held the international voice traffic market to ransom through artificial pricing structures, channelling an estimated BDT 631 crore to a connected account.

8.2.2 The Telecommunications Network and Licensing Policy 2025

The TNL Policy 2025, gazetted on 22 September 2025, represents the most ambitious licensing reform since Bangladesh's telecom liberalization. It consolidates the fragmented legacy structure into four technology neutral categories: Access Network Service Providers (ANSP), covering cellular mobile and fixed telecom operators; National Infrastructure and Connectivity Service Providers (NICSP), covering towers, fibre networks, and national transmission; International Connectivity Service Providers (ICSP), covering submarine cable, IP transit, and international bandwidth; and Non-Terrestrial Networks and Service Providers (NTNSP), covering satellite and NGSO services.

The policy abolishes the intermediary licence layers that historically inflated costs without adding meaningful value. IGW, IIG, ICX, and NIX licences will not be renewed upon expiry, with most expiring by 2027. Existing Cellular Mobile Service Providers will be migrated to the ANSP category. For the first time, the framework formally recognizes Mobile Virtual Network Operators (MVNOs), opening the market to asset light entrants that can stimulate competition and innovation without requiring massive capital expenditure on network infrastructure.

The policy introduces strict performance obligations: mobile operators must connect 50 percent of towers to fibre within 18 months and 80 percent within three years, the national fibre backbone must extend underground to every union parishad, and in disaster prone areas at least 25 percent of towers must have diesel generator backup. A National Quality of Service Dashboard will provide anonymized, near real time data on accessibility, coverage, throughput, and reliability.

8.2.3 Implementation Architecture

BTRC has begun drafting the operational guidelines necessary to translate the policy into enforceable rules. The implementation requires careful attention to several dimensions.

Managed Transition. Legacy licence holders possess legitimate expectations based on existing investments. The transition roadmap must include compensation mechanisms, migration incentives, and safeguards against stranded assets. Stage 1 begins with the Policy taking effect (September 2025). Stage 2 introduces the new licensing regime upon government approval of guidelines. Stage 3 commences in 2027, with overlap continuing until existing licences in each category expire or migrate. This graduated approach avoids the sudden disruption that has undermined telecommunications reforms in other jurisdictions.

Foreign Ownership Framework. The Policy caps foreign equity in mobile operators at 85 percent, requiring at least 15 percent local ownership. Among private operators, only Grameenphone currently meets this threshold. Robi (Axiata 61.82 percent, Bharti Airtel 28.18 percent) and Banglalink (fully owned by VEON) face significant restructuring requirements. The Strategy recommends that BTRC establish a clear timeline and mechanism for compliance, avoiding disruptive forced divestiture while ensuring meaningful local participation in the sector's value creation.

Fiscal Revenue Protection. Licence simplification reduces point of collection revenue. The government must introduce compensatory fiscal measures, including broadened base, revised fee structures, usage growth targets, or temporary levy instruments, and monitor revenue impact quarterly to preserve both regulatory sustainability and investment attractiveness.

Anti-Cartel and Competition Safeguards. The Strategy mandates telecom specific anti cartel rules. Any collective arrangement must be contractually transparent and externally audited. Market Development Expense funds or collective funds must be held in regulated accounts subject to independent audit and public disclosure. The Significant Market Power (SMP) framework should empower BTRC to mandate infrastructure sharing, regulate wholesale access pricing, and intervene to prevent market abuse.

Revenue Generation and Licensing Alignment: Following the Telecommunications Network and Licensing Policy 2025, PTD will execute a comprehensive revenue re-collection and generation plan. This includes:

  • Fiber Bank & Customer Acquisition: Establishing a "National Fiber Bank" to map all existing dark fiber and identify potential commercial customers through partnerships with new license holders.

  • Service Evolution: Accelerating the launch of Triple Play (Voice, Data, IPTV) and Quad Play services, including the sale of mobile devices and IoT hardware in installments supported by private sector financing.

  • MVNO Implementation: Finalizing the technical and commercial integration for Mobile Virtual Network Operators (MVNO) to maximize the utilization of state-owned spectrum and infrastructure.

International benchmark: India's Unified License regime, introduced in 2013, consolidated over a dozen service specific licences into a single framework and catalyzed rapid mobile broadband growth. Malaysia's Unified Licence under the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998 similarly demonstrated that licence simplification, combined with technology neutrality, accelerates investment and reduces consumer prices.



8.3 Spectrum Governance Reform

8.3.1 Diagnosis

Bangladesh's spectrum allocation policy has functioned more as a revenue extraction mechanism than as an enabler of efficient utilization and universal connectivity. The Task Force documented that auctions carried rigid rules and inflated reserve prices, leaving operators overburdened and spectrum unsold. The proposed 700 MHz auction, critical for rural broadband and 5G deployment, set a base price of BDT 263 crore per MHz, far exceeding international benchmarks. If applied, spectrum costs would reach 14 to 16 percent of operator revenue compared to the 9 to 12 percent prevailing rate, actively discouraging the investment needed for network modernization. Overpriced base prices left valuable spectrum unsold, representing a pure loss of opportunity for the duration of non-utilization.

Additional structural deficiencies include fragmented band assignments, the absence of secondary spectrum trading or sharing mechanisms, limited availability of key low frequency bands, weak spectrum monitoring and enforcement capacity, and inconsistent regulatory decisions that undermined investor trust. The absence of a long term, harmonized policy framework resulted in inefficient utilization, with some frequency bands remaining underused while others faced congestion due to pricing asymmetry.

8.3.2 Reform Architecture

National Five-Year Spectrum Plan. The government should develop and publish a comprehensive Spectrum Plan that sets out frequency bands, auction schedules, coverage obligations, and technology pathways through formal public consultation. The Plan should cover IMT bands for 5G deployment (including 3.5 GHz mid-band and 700 MHz for coverage), satellite bands aligned with NGSO licensing, Industrial IoT spectrum for manufacturing and agriculture, and Fixed Wireless Access (FWA) allocations for rural broadband. The Plan should be published for public consultation within six months of this Strategy's adoption.

Technology-Neutral Spectrum Rights. Spectrum rights should be converted into technology neutral permits allowing operators flexibility to deploy 4G, 5G, or FWA services without additional administrative burdens. This aligns with ITU-R Resolution 223 (Rev.WRC-19) promoting flexible broadband spectrum use and eliminates the technology specific constraints that have historically limited operator choice.

Secondary Spectrum Markets. The Policy should enable spectrum trading, leasing, and sharing to enhance efficient utilization. A public spectrum registry should maintain transparency and accountability. Active radio network sharing among operators should be permitted under defined security and technical rules, with fee rebates for shared rural infrastructure that demonstrably reduces diesel consumption and environmental impact.

Performance-Linked Pricing. Spectrum fee structures should shift from large upfront payments to staged, performance linked payments that tie financial obligations to measurable coverage and service delivery outcomes, especially in underserved regions. Reserve prices should be benchmarked against comparable markets, with clear published justifications to prevent overpricing and unsold allocations. BTRC should maintain a public spectrum and performance dashboard, updated quarterly, to track usage, coverage, and efficiency as eligibility criteria for future spectrum awards.

International benchmark: The United Kingdom's Ofcom Spectrum Management Strategy 2021 to 2026 exemplifies a balanced approach, combining competitive auctions with coverage obligations, secondary trading markets, and technology neutral permits. India's spectrum sharing guidelines allow operators to pool spectrum for efficiency, while the European Commission's 5G Action Plan coordinated cross-border spectrum roadmaps with binding coverage targets.

8.3.3 KPI Framework: Spectrum Governance

Indicator

Target (2027)

Target (2030)

8.4 Digital Regulatory Modernization

Governance reform is incomplete without the operational systems that make transparency, efficiency, and accountability technically achievable. The current regulatory ecosystem operates on fragmented manual processes that impede both speed and integrity.

8.4.1 Enterprise Resource Planning for BTRC

The Strategy mandates deployment of a comprehensive Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) system to integrate all BTRC operational functions under a unified digital platform. The ERP should automate licensing and renewal processes, centralize revenue and spectrum management, monitor network quality through real time dashboards, and streamline enforcement, compliance, and consumer grievance handling. It should interlink with Customs, the National Board of Revenue, the Election Commission, BSCPLC, NTTN operators, and other agencies for cross verification of data, while supporting research, analytics, and administrative operations. Equipped with role based access, digital signatures as part of PKI, blockchain secured records, and AI assisted analytics, the ERP should enable BTRC to evolve into a data driven, responsive, and analytically empowered regulator.

BTCL has already commenced ERP procurement, with a MoU signed and ERP consultant procurement underway as of November 2025. The ERP initiative should be extended across all PTD entities as a shared platform or interoperable system, creating a unified information backbone for the entire posts and telecommunications ecosystem.

International benchmark: Singapore's Info-communications Media Development Authority (IMDA) operates a fully digital licensing and compliance platform with real time industry data analytics. The Turkish telecommunications regulator ICTA deployed an integrated regulatory management system covering licensing, spectrum, QoS monitoring, and enforcement within a single digital architecture.

8.4.2 National One-Stop Digital Permit Portal

A national one stop digital permit portal should be established to streamline site construction, licensing, and renewals through coordinated timelines, dig once scheduling, and digital tracking. The portal should cover necessary licences and imports with statutory 15 and 30 day decision clocks. A shared dig once works calendar should prevent duplicative excavation for fibre and utility deployment, reducing both cost and urban disruption. The first phase should launch within 120 days of this Strategy's adoption with an initial scope covering tower construction permits and equipment import clearances. Full scope should be achieved by 2027, covering all licence categories, renewals, spectrum applications, and interconnection approvals.

8.4.3 Digital Equipment Import Registry

A digital registry for pre-approved telecom equipment should be introduced, allowing automatic clearance of repeat shipments and reduced processing times. The registry should include a SAR testing and certification component, enabling local verification and certification of mobile handsets. This reduces reliance on foreign laboratories, prevents falsified compliance reports, and expedites clearance for both imported and locally manufactured devices. The National Equipment Identity Register (NEIR), re-launched in 2025 to curb illegal handset imports in a market where grey channel phones account for an estimated 50 to 60 percent of sales, should be integrated with the equipment registry for comprehensive device governance.



8.5 Investment Climate and Fiscal Reform

The telecommunications sector cannot fulfil its infrastructure and inclusion mandate without a stable, predictable, and investment friendly fiscal environment. The current taxation framework imposes cumulative burdens that erode profitability and deter both domestic and foreign investment.

8.5.1 Taxation Reform

Bangladesh's telecom taxation regime prioritizes short term fiscal gain over long term digital transformation. Excessive taxation erodes profitability and investment incentives, reducing the financial capacity of operators to expand networks, upgrade technologies, and enhance service quality. The Task Force documented that the taxation framework is complicated by inconsistent fiscal treatment across different operator categories, lack of harmonization between BTRC and National Board of Revenue (NBR) policies, and frequent ad hoc changes introduced through annual Finance Acts. India's GST regime, the UK's uniform VAT system, and the FCC's universal contribution framework demonstrate that rationalized tax structures can generate sustainable government revenue while simultaneously encouraging sectoral growth.

The Strategy recommends a predictable, investment oriented, and sector neutral tax structure that harmonizes fiscal obligations across all operators. This includes rationalizing VAT and supplementary duties, reducing corporate tax rates for telecom operators to regional averages, and integrating spectrum and licensing fees within a transparent long term amortization framework. Tax incentives should be introduced for infrastructure sharing, green ICT adoption, subsidized service for underserved regions, and digital innovation. Institutional coordination between BTRC and NBR should be formalized to eliminate regulatory overlap and ensure policy coherence.

8.5.2 Foreign Direct Investment Framework

The entry of Starlink in April 2025, with its 10 year operating licence, commercial launch on 20 May 2025, four local gateways installed by August 2025, and 880 Gbps of deployed capacity, demonstrates Bangladesh's capacity to attract global technology investment when regulatory processes are streamlined. This momentum must be institutionalized. The Strategy recommends clarifying foreign investment rules, maintaining stable ownership frameworks that balance local participation with international capital access, and creating a regulatory environment where licence processing timelines are predictable and transparent. The 15 percent local ownership requirement under the TNL Policy 2025 should be implemented through a phased compliance timeline that avoids forced divestiture and provides adequate transition mechanisms for affected operators.



8.6 Consumer Protection and Quality of Service

Consumer protection has been a persistent weak point in Bangladesh's telecom governance. The Task Force documented weak enforcement, absence of telecom service quality monitoring, consumer dissatisfaction, and reputational damage that deterred investment and affected sector revenue. BTRC drive tests revealed widespread service deficiencies including excessive call drop rates and poor 4G voice performance across major urban and peri-urban areas.

8.6.1 Modernized Customer Code

A modernized customer code should require clear, plain language tariff communication, explicit consent for value added services, standardized billing formats, and transparent fair usage policies. Pricing regulation should move toward a flexible yet fair structure, allowing competitive pricing while preventing predatory practices through benchmark based oversight and published reference rates.

8.6.2 National Quality of Service Dashboard

District level independent performance scorecards should be published regularly, combining drive test and crowdsourced data on quality of service indicators such as speed, latency, call drop rates, and complaint resolution. The National QoS Dashboard, mandated by the TNL Policy 2025, should provide anonymized near real time data accessible to the public. Monthly public reporting will improve user trust and create competitive pressure for operators to improve service standards.

8.6.3 Digital Complaint Management System

BTRC should establish a national digital complaint management system providing a single online and mobile platform for submitting, tracking, and escalating grievances. Complaint resolution SLAs should be published, and operator compliance should be tracked through the system. Monthly complaint data should be published to enable comparative assessment of operator performance.

Technical Monitoring: BTRC will implement advanced systems for Spectrum Occupancy, Spectrum Noise monitoring, SAR/Radiation testing, and regular network Drive Tests.

Grievance Resolution: Launch an "All-in-One" ERP/Complaint Management solution with a mandatory 7-day time-bound ticketing system for resolving customer issues.

Licensing Compliance: Strengthen institutional capacity (labs, software, and data analytics) to ensure all licensees fulfill KPI and Quality of Experience (KQoS) obligations as per the 2025 Licensing Policy.

International benchmark: TRAI (India) publishes quarterly QoS reports with operator-specific performance data, creating transparent competitive pressure. The UK's Ofcom requires operators to publish complaint statistics and resolution rates, with persistent underperformers subject to formal investigation and financial penalties.



8.7 Data Governance, Privacy, and Cybersecurity

The Task Force identified an insufficient legal framework and limited incident response capacity that leaves vital sectors such as telecommunications and energy vulnerable to cybersecurity breaches, data theft, and system intrusions, jeopardizing national security. The lack of user data privacy protection measures damages consumer trust, while surveillance and digital eavesdropping without adequate legal safeguards violates constitutional privacy rights.

8.7.1 Reform Architecture

Information Security Standards. The sector should adopt international standards for information security and privacy, specifically ISO 27001 and ISO 27701, supported by mandatory vendor attestations, independent audits, and 72 hour breach reporting requirements. All operators and major service providers should achieve ISO 27001 certification by 2028.

Data Governance Framework. Data governance reforms should ensure purpose limited access, strict retention policies, and auditable lawful interception, preserving both privacy and national security. The Draft Data Protection Ordinance should be harmonized with the Amended Telecom Ordinance to establish clear jurisdictional boundaries and consistent enforcement mechanisms. Cross border data flow provisions should align with GDPR adequacy principles to facilitate international commerce and attract technology investment.

Cybersecurity Preparedness. Cross agency readiness should be institutionalized through biannual cybersecurity and crisis management drills. A sectoral Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT) should be established or strengthened to coordinate incident response across telecommunications infrastructure. The July 2024 experience demonstrated the catastrophic consequences of infrastructure vulnerability, both from external threats and internal executive overreach.

International benchmark: The European Union's NIS2 Directive imposes mandatory cybersecurity obligations on essential service providers including telecommunications operators, with requirements for risk management, incident reporting within 24 hours, and supply chain security assessment. South Korea's KISA operates a world-class telecommunications CERT with mandatory breach reporting and cross-sector coordination protocols.



8.8 Digital Inclusion and Universal Access

Universal access reforms under this Strategy operate on two parallel tracks: restructuring the Social Obligation Fund for results based financing, and deploying complementary inclusion mechanisms that address the affordability, literacy, and accessibility dimensions of the digital divide.

8.8.1 Results-Based SOF

The SOF should be restructured to operate on a results based financing model, awarding disbursements only for verified delivery of measurable outcomes such as broadband speeds, latency, and active user adoption, confirmed by independent audits. This replaces the input based model that allowed equipment to be installed but not operationalized, as documented by the Task Force's field inspections that found routers and hotspots reported as deployed but not functional.

8.8.2 Affordability Interventions

The government should introduce affordability initiatives including data and device vouchers and zero interest handset programs for low income populations, supported by phased fiscal incentives tied to measurable affordability gains. The Strategy also endorses Teletalk's proposed SIM distribution through post offices, creating a physical touchpoint for affordable connectivity that leverages the Nagorik Sheba Bangladesh platform's 8,000 planned centres by 2030.

8.8.3 Digital Navigator Helpdesks

Locally anchored digital navigator helpdesks should be established to assist rural and marginalized users with connectivity registration, digital safety, and complaint resolution. These helpdesks should be integrated with Nagorik Sheba centres, leveraging trained local entrepreneurs as human intermediaries who bridge the digital divide for populations that cannot independently navigate online services. This addresses the critical insight from the Digital Transformation Strategy 2025 to 2030 that digital transformation cannot succeed through technology deployment alone; it requires investment in human capacity at the last mile.

International benchmark: Colombia's community digital centres provide free digital literacy training and subsidized connectivity, reaching over 10 million citizens in underserved areas. India's Common Service Centres operate a network of over 500,000 village-level outlets providing digital government services through trained operators, a model directly analogous to the Nagorik Sheba concept.



8.9 Emerging Technology Governance

8.9.1 5G Readiness

The Five Year Spectrum Plan should establish a clear 5G deployment pathway, including mid-band spectrum allocation (3.5 GHz), coverage obligations for urban and peri-urban areas, and vertical industry use cases in health, manufacturing, smart agriculture, and education. The Strategy recommends phased 5G deployment beginning with urban centres and economic zones by 2028, with progressive expansion to district headquarters and transport corridors by 2030. BTRC should operate regulatory sandboxes for emerging 5G use cases, including Intelligent Transport Systems, Industrial IoT, and Fixed Wireless Access, allowing controlled experimentation before full scale commercial deployment.

8.9.2 Satellite Connectivity

Starlink's entry in May 2025 inaugurated Bangladesh's satellite broadband era. With four gateways operational by August 2025 (two at Kaliakair Hi-Tech Park, one each in Rajshahi and Jashore) and approximately 1,800 active devices within five months, the service has demonstrated commercial viability. Around half a dozen additional global satellite providers have expressed interest. The NGSO licensing framework should be maintained as an open category, with clear fee structures, revenue sharing provisions (3 percent for years three through five, 5.5 percent from year six), and local gateway requirements that balance market access with data sovereignty and lawful interception obligations.

Starlink's application to export bandwidth from Bangladesh to neighbouring countries, including India, Nepal, Bhutan, and Myanmar, represents an opportunity for Bangladesh to position itself as a regional connectivity hub. This aligns with Bangladesh Submarine Cable Company's existing bandwidth export to northeast India and should be governed through clear IPLC guidelines that protect domestic interests while encouraging transit revenue.

8.9.3 OTT and Digital Platform Governance

The TNL Policy 2025 recognizes that ANSP licensees are permitted to offer any value added or OTT services through their networks. The Amended Telecom Ordinance extends regulatory scope to social media platforms, intermediary services, OTT services, audiovisual and streaming platforms, e-commerce, content based services, cloud providers, data centres, and AI services. While comprehensive scope is important for regulatory coherence, the Strategy cautions against regulatory overreach that could stifle innovation or centralize excessive authority. A proportionate, flexible regulatory approach should be adopted for emerging and innovative services, as the TNL Policy itself prescribes, with distinct governance tracks for telecommunications infrastructure (heavily regulated) versus digital content and platform services (lighter touch, consumer protection focused).

8.9.4 IoT and AI Governance

Governance frameworks for the Internet of Things and Artificial Intelligence should be developed through cross sectoral coordination rather than telecommunications regulation alone. The recent IoT and radio equipment import guidelines represent a useful start but must be complemented by interoperability standards, security certification requirements, and spectrum allocation for low power wide area networks. AI governance should be aligned with the National AI Policy and the Digital Transformation Strategy 2025 to 2030, with BTRC's role limited to AI applications within telecommunications networks rather than general purpose AI regulation.



8.10 Sustainability and Green ICT

Environmental sustainability should be integrated into the telecommunications policy framework through energy efficiency targets, renewable energy adoption for telecom sites and data centres, and enforceable e-waste recycling standards under producer responsibility frameworks. The TNL Policy 2025 mandates eco-friendly practices including renewable energy for towers, aligning with global trends in green telecommunications.

The Strategy recommends measurable green KPIs linked to concessional financing opportunities. Operators should be required to report annual energy consumption, renewable energy adoption rates, and e-waste management volumes. Spectrum fee rebates should be provided for shared rural infrastructure that demonstrably reduces diesel consumption. The Hathazari Green Data Centre project, structured as a PPP with ADB involvement, should serve as the pilot for Tier III certified, energy efficient data hosting that meets both domestic compliance requirements and international sustainability standards.

International benchmark: The European Green Digital Coalition commits ICT companies to science-based emissions targets. GSMA's Climate Action Taskforce tracks mobile industry progress toward net zero by 2050. Kenya's Communications Authority requires operators to report annual carbon footprint and renewable energy adoption, with penalties for non-compliance integrated into licence renewal conditions.



8.11 Evidence-Based Policymaking

8.11.1 BTRC Research Division

To embed evidence based policymaking and future foresight into telecom governance, BTRC should establish a permanent, independent research division modelled on global best practices. The division should be staffed with experts in telecommunications economics, data science, law, and engineering and should collaborate with universities, think tanks, and international institutions. It should be mandated to conduct market and spectrum economics analysis, including auction benchmarking, SMP analysis, and open access cost modelling, and to evaluate unified licensing frameworks and operate regulatory sandboxes for emerging technologies.

The Research Division should publish quarterly transparent KPIs and open datasets covering affordability (effective price per GB), rural latency, spectrum utilization, and permitting benchmarks (p50 and p90 approval times). It should possess operational autonomy to publish insights, convene stakeholder dialogues, and advise on strategic reforms without requiring prior clearance from BTRC's operational leadership.

International benchmark: Ofcom's research and evidence team publishes the annual Communications Market Report and sector-specific analysis that forms the empirical foundation for regulatory decisions. TRAI's research division conducts consultation papers on emerging issues, typically receiving hundreds of stakeholder submissions that inform final regulatory determinations. The FCC's Office of Economics and Analytics was established in 2018 to strengthen the empirical basis for US telecommunications regulation.

8.11.2 Centre of Excellence for Digital Public Infrastructure

An independent Centre of Excellence should be established for third party impact evaluation of SOF projects, digital public infrastructure, and long term sustainability assessment. The Centre should operate independently of BTRC and PTD, with funding from SOF allocations, to provide objective evaluation that informs both project design and policy formulation. It should be mandated to publish all evaluation findings and should develop partnerships with international research institutions for methodology development and peer review. The Centre should also serve as the institutional home for the sectoral knowledge base, maintaining comprehensive databases on project outcomes, technology assessments, market analysis, and policy evaluations that inform future decision making.

8.11.3 Telecom Reform Program Management Office

A dedicated Telecom Reform Program Management Office (PMO) should be established under PTD to oversee the implementation of reforms across all domains, address bottlenecks, coordinate cross agency actions, and publish monthly progress dashboards. The PMO should report directly to the PTD Secretary and should have the authority to convene inter-entity coordination meetings, escalate implementation failures, and recommend course corrections. State owned entities should be required to meet clear performance contracts and Service Level Agreements (SLAs), with annual performance assessments linked to leadership renewal and resource allocation decisions.

8.13 Chapter Summary

This chapter has set out the strategic and governance reform architecture necessary to convert Bangladesh's posts and telecommunications sector from a governance constrained, rent extraction oriented system into a transparent, investment friendly, and citizen centred digital infrastructure ecosystem. The reforms are organized across eleven interdependent domains, each building on the diagnostic findings of the Task Force and the institutional reforms detailed in preceding chapters.

The regulatory architecture has been fundamentally restructured through the Bangladesh Telecommunication (Amendment) Ordinance 2025, which restores BTRC autonomy, prohibits internet shutdowns, and establishes parliamentary oversight. Operationalizing this framework requires a formal DoT-BTRC mandate split through Government Order, with DoT assuming policy and strategic oversight while BTRC retains operational regulatory authority and revenue collection transitions to the Government's financial authorities.

Market design has been reformed through the Telecommunications Network and Licensing Policy 2025, consolidating over 23 fragmented licence categories into four technology neutral categories, abolishing rent seeking intermediary layers, formally recognizing MVNOs, and introducing strict performance obligations including fibre rollout targets, QoS standards, and disaster resilience requirements. The managed transition framework protects legitimate expectations while creating a clear pathway to the simplified regime.

Spectrum governance reform addresses the fundamental misalignment between Bangladesh's revenue maximization approach and the investment enabling approach recommended by ITU guidelines. The Five Year Spectrum Plan, technology neutral permits, secondary trading markets, and performance linked pricing collectively create the conditions for efficient utilization, 5G deployment, and rural broadband expansion.

Digital regulatory modernization, through ERP deployment, the one stop permit portal, and the digital equipment registry, provides the operational infrastructure without which transparency and accountability remain aspirational. Investment climate reform, encompassing taxation rationalization and FDI framework clarification, creates the predictable fiscal environment necessary to attract and retain the capital investment that network modernization demands.

Consumer protection, data governance, and cybersecurity reforms address the trust deficit that constrains both adoption and investment. Digital inclusion reforms restructure the SOF for results based financing and deploy affordability interventions and digital navigator helpdesks through the Nagorik Sheba platform. Emerging technology governance, covering 5G, satellite connectivity (including Starlink), OTT platforms, IoT, and AI, establishes proportionate frameworks for the technologies that will define the sector's next decade.

Sustainability and green ICT reforms integrate environmental responsibility into licensing and operational requirements. Evidence based policymaking, through the BTRC Research Division, the Centre of Excellence, and the Reform PMO, ensures that the governance reforms introduced in this chapter are monitored, evaluated, and adapted on the basis of empirical evidence rather than political expediency.

The consolidated KPI framework provides measurable accountability targets across all reform domains, enabling parliamentary oversight, civil society monitoring, and internal performance management. The Strategy's success depends not on the ambition of its architecture but on the discipline, consistency, and institutional commitment with which it is implemented across electoral cycles and leadership transitions.

— End of Chapter 8 —

9. Implementation Roadmap

9.0 Introduction

The preceding chapters of this strategy have established the diagnostic foundations, legal architecture, institutional reforms, project corrections, citizen service ambitions, and governance frameworks that collectively define the Posts and Telecommunications Division's digital transformation agenda. This chapter consolidates those interventions into a unified, phased implementation roadmap that sequences actions across time, assigns accountability to specific entities, and establishes measurable milestones at each stage.

The roadmap is structured around three primary phases spanning 2025 to 2030, with a fourth horizon extending to 2035 for reforms and infrastructure investments whose impacts necessarily mature over longer cycles. The phasing logic follows a deliberate sequencing principle: foundational legal and institutional reforms in the early years create the enabling conditions for market restructuring, infrastructure deployment, and technology adoption in the middle years, which in turn generate the data, capacity, and trust required for advanced governance and regional positioning in the later years.

This approach reflects lessons from comparable transformation programmes internationally. The United Kingdom's Digital Strategy 2022 and South Korea's Digital New Deal both adopted three-phase models that prioritized legal clarity and institutional readiness before scaling technology deployment. India's Digital India programme similarly sequenced infrastructure rollout after completing its Unified Licence reform in 2013, achieving measurable broadband penetration gains only after the regulatory architecture was stabilized. Bangladesh's roadmap applies the same principle to the specific conditions of the posts and telecommunications sector.

Each phase is defined not by rigid calendar deadlines alone but by the achievement of prerequisite conditions that unlock subsequent actions. Phase 2 activities, for instance, cannot proceed effectively without the Government Orders, recruitment rules, and ERP specifications that Phase 1 is designed to produce. This conditional sequencing reduces the risk of reforms stalling because institutional foundations were incomplete.





9.1 Phase 1: Foundation (2025 to 2026)

Phase 1 constitutes the most intensive reform period, requiring simultaneous action across legal, institutional, and technical domains. The central objective is to establish the legal and governance architecture upon which all subsequent phases depend.

9.1.1 Legal and Regulatory Foundations

The Bangladesh Telecommunication (Amendment) Ordinance 2025, approved in December 2025, represents the single most consequential legislative action in the sector in over a decade. Its provisions permanently prohibiting internet shutdowns, restoring BTRC operational autonomy, establishing a Parliamentary Accountability Committee, and extending regulatory scope to digital platforms must now be translated into operational reality. The immediate priorities are:

• Government Order on BTRC Autonomy: A formal Government Order must codify the division of responsibilities between DoT (policy advisory and SOE oversight) and BTRC (licensing, enforcement, spectrum management, and consumer protection). This order must be gazetted within the first quarter of 2026, with a joint steering committee established to manage the transition period.

• TNL Policy 2025 Migration Framework: The Telecommunications Network and Licensing Policy 2025, gazetted in September 2025, consolidates over twenty three legacy licence categories into four technology-neutral categories: ANSP, NICSP, ICSP, and NTNSP. A managed licence migration framework must be published by mid-2026, with existing operators given a defined twelve-month transition window supported by clear compliance schedules and dispute resolution mechanisms.

• Postal Services Ordinance Modernization: The Postal Services Ordinance requires amendment to reflect Bangladesh Post's evolving role as a logistics, financial services, and digital access platform. Drafting should commence in 2025, with a consultation draft circulated to stakeholders by the second quarter of 2026. The amendments should authorize competitive parcel services, digital financial products, and integration with e-commerce platforms.

• National Network Evolution Framework: A mandatory Five-Year Network Evolution Plan will be required for all licensees to ensure long-term technology parity. To optimize public investment, PTD will enforce a Network Development Plan Sharing protocol between Teletalk, BSCL (Satellite), and BSCPLC (Submarine Cable). This protocol will ensure equitable resource allocation, preventing duplicative infrastructure while ensuring that satellite and submarine capacity are utilized as primary backups for each other to guarantee 100% national uptime.

• Five-Year National Spectrum Plan: BTRC must publish a comprehensive spectrum plan by the end of 2026, addressing 5G mid-band allocation (3.3 to 3.8 GHz), secondary trading frameworks, performance-linked pricing, technology-neutral assignments, and enterprise and localized licensing provisions. The plan should include a public consultation period and incorporate ITU recommendations on spectrum efficiency.

• MOCSLA Recruitment Rules: The Mailing Operator and Courier Services Licensing Authority requires finalized recruitment regulations to enable permanent staffing. This legal instrument should be completed within 2025 to 2026.

9.1.2 Institutional and Human Resource Reforms

The institutional reform agenda detailed in Chapter 6 demands immediate action on several fronts during this phase:

• SOE Board Reconstitution: Boards of Directors across BTCL, Teletalk, BSCPLC, BSCL, BCSL, and TSS must be reconstituted to include a majority of independent members with sectoral expertise. The PTD Secretary should be separated from the Board Chair position. Target: all boards reconstituted by the end of 2026.

• Digital HRIS Deployment: A unified Human Resource Information System must be specified, procured, and deployed across all PTD entities by the end of Phase 1. The system should integrate personnel records, payroll, promotions, deputation tracking, and performance management into a single auditable platform.

• Telecom Cadre Restoration: The proposal to rebuild a dedicated Telecommunication Cadre under DoT through competitive civil service recruitment should be formalized during this phase, with the first recruitment cycle initiated by the end of 2026.

• CAG HR Compliance Audit: The Comptroller and Auditor General should commence the first comprehensive HR compliance audit across all PTD entities, with findings reported to Parliament. This audit cycle should be completed within 2026.

• Performance Contracts: The Reform Programme Management Office should establish formal performance contracts with each SOE, linking measurable KPIs to management accountability. Quarterly dashboards should be operational by mid-2026.

9.1.3 Digital Infrastructure and Systems

The digital systems that underpin regulatory modernization must be initiated during Phase 1, even if full deployment extends into Phase 2:

• BTRC ERP Specification and Procurement: The Enterprise Resource Planning system for BTRC, integrating licensing, revenue management, spectrum allocation, QoS monitoring, and enforcement into a unified platform, requires detailed specification during this phase. A consultant should be engaged to develop the technical specification, with procurement initiated by the end of 2026.

• BTCL ERP Consultant Engagement: BTCL has already issued a tender for an ERP specification consultant. The engagement should be completed and the customized ERP specification finalized within Phase 1.

• Fibre and IP Equipment Digital Mapping: BTCL's ongoing digital mapping of optical fibre and IP equipment resources should be completed during this phase. The mapping has been made live on the BTCL TIP portal and must be expanded to cover all transmission assets.

• BSCPLC SMW-6 Commissioning: The third submarine cable, SEA-ME-WE 6, is expected to become operational in the first quarter of 2026, adding 13,200 Gbps of potential capacity. BSCPLC must complete commissioning, testing, and initial capacity sales during this phase.

• Teletalk 4G Expansion: Teletalk has added 1,699 new 4G sites, increasing daily data consumption from 354 to 464 terabytes (approximately 20 percent growth). The tower sharing agreement with Edotco for 2,300 sites should be operationalized during Phase 1, with site surveys and equipment installation proceeding on schedule toward the December 2026 completion target.

• Bangladesh Post GPS Fleet Tracking: The installation of GPS trackers on 85 mail vans has been completed. During this phase, the system should be integrated with route optimization software and the Domestic Mail Monitoring System (DMS) to enable real-time fleet management.

9.1.4 Service Delivery Foundations

• Nagorik Sheba Centre Rollout: The integration of postal infrastructure with the Nagorik Sheba network should commence during Phase 1, with an initial target of converting 500 post offices into Nagorik Sheba service points by the end of 2026. Each centre should offer NID, passport, tax filing, SIM registration, and basic connectivity services.

• E-Commerce Logistics Pilot: Bangladesh Post's reverse logistics middleware system and API integration with the Domestic Mail Monitoring System should be completed during this phase, establishing the technical foundation for two-way urban-rural e-commerce parcel delivery.

• Cold Chain Infrastructure: The project to install 14 chiller chambers and 84 deep freezers across postal distribution centres should be completed, enabling Bangladesh Post to handle agricultural and perishable goods logistics.

• TSS Strategic Partnerships: TSS has signed a Strategic Partnership Agreement with two technology firms in September 2025 and an MoU with Edotco Bangladesh in December 2025. The pilot manufacturing programmes for CCTV equipment, lithium-ion battery repair, and 4G/5G dual-band WiFi routers should produce initial outputs during Phase 1.

• Starlink Integration: Starlink, licensed in April 2025 and launched in May 2025, has deployed four gateways (Kaliakair, Rajshahi, Jashore) with 880 Gbps capacity and 1,800 active devices. During Phase 1, BTRC should formalize the NGSO satellite service licensing framework drawing on the Starlink precedent, with clear revenue sharing schedules (3 percent in years 3 to 5, rising to 5.5 percent thereafter) and local gateway requirements.
